column15: As published on the NSW Health Government website on the 12th of August 2021, stay at home orders including school cosures for additional Local Government Areas (LGA) were in place from 5pm 12th of August 2021. The policy was implemented for additional LGAs (Bayside, Burwood, and Strathfield), and was already in place for Blacktown, Campbelltown, Canterbury-Bankstown, Cumberland, Fairfield, Georges River, Liverpool, Parramatta, and 12 suburbs in the Penrith LGA.    This represents a tightening of the policy response to COVID-19, as more areas in the region were affected.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220111051024/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/Pages/20210812_00.aspx

column15: On 16th September 2021, the NSW Government announced: "Stay-at-home rules will remain in place for the following regional NSW LGAs: Albury, Bathurst, Bourke, Brewarrina, Broken Hill, Central Coast, Central Darling, Cessnock, Dubbo, Eurobodalla, Gilgandra, Goulburn, Mulwarre, Kiama, Lake Macquarie, Lismore, Lithgow, Maitland, Mid-Western, Narromine, Newcastle, Orange, Port Stephens, Queanbeyan-Palerang, Shellharbour, Shoalhaven, Walgett and Wingecarribee."    Archived link:https://web.archive.org/web/20210916204017/https://education.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/advice-for-families#Levels1

C1: Schools in the Northern Territory were open during this period of time. Student attendance in-person returned to close to normal in May of 2020 and schools were functioning as normal from June 2020.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210128055448/https://education.nt.gov.au/publications/information-for-term-4-2020
column24: Businesses are essentially open as normal with some minor compliance around COVID safety planning and have been since mid-2020.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210118172628/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business#/events_and_gatherings
column33: Public events have additional COVID-safety guidelines however are functioning as normal with no density or percentage capacity restrictions.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210111054507/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business/guidelines-for-events-and-gatherings#/more_than_500_people
column42: Gatherings over 100 have extra guidelines (specifically a COVID-safe checklist), gatherings over 500 people require the approval of state government. These guidelines have been in place since at least September 2020.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210111054507/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business/guidelines-for-events-and-gatherings#/more_than_500_people
column51: There is no evidence that public transport was ever restricted during the pandemic.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210205151150/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/public-transport
column60: The Northern Territory was not under any stay-at-home orders or guidance during this period. In fact the Northern Territory never had a stay-at-home policy in place during the pandemic.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210118172628/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business#/events_and_gatherings
column69: The territory restricts entry from areas declared hotspots. From 1 January to 1 February, there was restriction on travel from LGAs in NSW.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210424124155/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19/former-declared-hotspots
column79: The Northern Territory Government was the only Australian government to not implement a moratorium on evictions during the pandemic after other jurisdictions agreed to this measure in National Cabinet in April. The Northern Territory made some minor changes around notices to tenants and encouraged landlords and tenants to negotiate relief voluntarily. There was also no policy around relief for mortgages (though Australian governments did lobby banks to provide voluntary mortgage relief early in the pandemic which could have benefitted many Territorians)  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419092157/https://consumeraffairs.nt.gov.au/rental-changes-due-to-covid-19  The government did create a program for debt and contract relief for small and medium-sized businesses effective from at least October 21 2020.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210314235933/https://business.gov.au/grants-and-programs/Business-Hardship-Package-NT
column86: The Territory has had a coordinated campaign since February 2020. This includes a COVID website and regular updates from the Chief Minister and other officials.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210120003338/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/
column88: Throughout the pandemic, anyone in the Northern Territory with COVID symptoms could access a COVID test.  https://web.archive.org/web/20201231020114/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/symptoms-testing
column90: The Northern Territory has never had a locally acquired COVID case so it is very difficult to know how good its contact tracing system is. The Territory has implemented contact tracing standards in line with much of Australia since November 2020 including a check-in app released by government which is mandatory in public venues.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210308031847/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/safety-supervisors-and-contact-tracing
column103: Face coverings were not mandated anywhere in the NT from 16 - 19 January 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210511022551if_/https://7news.com.au/lifestyle/health-wellbeing/nt-moves-on-masks-in-airports-and-planes-c-1986183
column106: Vaccinations had not yet begun in the Territory. The first vaccinations in Australia happened on February 22nd 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210508062151/https://www.health.gov.au/news/first-covid-19-vaccinations-in-australia
column115: Anyone with COVID symptoms or who was a close contact of a case could not visit residential aged-care. This was the case since at least 29 July 2020.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210427071803/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/advice-care/residential-aged-care

column103: After a decision by National Cabinet, masks became mandatory in all Australian airports. This entered effect in the NT under a state government directive on January 20 2020 after masks had previously not been needed anywhere in the state.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210511022551if_/https://7news.com.au/lifestyle/health-wellbeing/nt-moves-on-masks-in-airports-and-planes-c-1986183

column74: As published on the Department of Home Affairs website, no foreign arrivals are permitted into the country unless they are in an exempt category or have been granted an individual exemption which includes compassionate and national interest grounds. All international arrivals must quarantine for 14 days at a designated facility. There is the possibility to travel to Australia quarantine free without an exemption if you have been in New Zealand for 14 days or more and not been in a designated COVID-19 outbreak location, and you are travelling to Australia on a quarantine-free flight from 14/12/2020.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210101130014/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20201231084847/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/new-zealand-safe-travel-zone
column77: Individuals are able to receive a $150 coronavirus supplement per fortnight from 01/01/2021 to 31/03/2021 which represents $100 less than the policy previous ending on 31/12/2020. Individuals can receive a Pandemic Leave Disaster Payment of $1400 for each 14 day period someone self-isolate, quarantine or care for a person who has COVID-19 through Centrelink as published on the Services Australia website last updated on 12/12/2020.  https://web.archive.org/web/20201222113322/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/services/centrelink/coronavirus-supplement   https://web.archive.org/web/20210305094501mp_/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/services/centrelink/pandemic-leave-disaster-payment

column15: The archived link below provided for Queensland school policy effective on 17 November 2020. This policy is not a targeted policy and applies to the whole state.   https://web.archive.org/web/20201130014209/https://qed.qld.gov.au/about-us/news-and-media/novel-coronavirus/covid-19-operating-guidelines-for-queensland-state-schools#school-functions
column69: People from certain hotspots require Border Pass to enter Queensland. For example, the archived link below shows that from 22 December 2020, people from New South Wales need the Border Pass to enter the Queensland area and Queensland Police will meet all arrivals to enforce the rules.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210111055333/https://www.qld.gov.au/border-pass

column15: Greater Brisbane is in lockdown from 5pm 29 March to 5pm 1 April 2021. Schools have closed from Tuesday 30 March in an early end to Term 1.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210426194844/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/help-and-advice/education

The lockdown restrictions in Greater Brisbane were lifted from midday of 01/April.2021,  https://www.health.gov.au/news/announcements/protecting-older-australians-covid-19-update-1-april-2021(This link can't be archived)

column86: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 12th December 2021, Tasmanian Government through The Business Tasmania team, alongside Public Health and WorkSafe, supports businesses to have all information and confidence needed for border reopening 15 December 2021. There have been series of face-to face sector-specific forums and comprehensive online videos to address any questions. Videos are on the Business Tasmania website. Roger Jaensch, Minister for State Growth.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220101213558/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/supporting-businesses-for-the-reopening-of-our-border

As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 13th December 2021, the Public Health Hotline has proven essential and has therefore been increased currently employing more than 100 Tasmanians to support the community with information and assist with vaccination or testing bookings.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220101220013/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/public-health-hotline-local-and-ready-to-help
column88: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 10/12/2021, the state is increasing testing capacity across all regions to approximately 5,000 in partnerships with the North West Pathology, Launceston Pathology, and Hobart Pathology to operate clinics in Burnie, Launceston, and Hobart respectively from Monday 13 December 2021. These clinics will be drive-through, have no requirement to book in advance, free of charge and most convenient for people wishing to get tested as part of domestic travel requirements from December 15, 2021. This represents an increase in testing capacity of the policy response to COVID-19 as the state government continues to maintain regional testing capacity with the cooperation of district hospitals and health center sites, whilst seeking further increase in testing capacity. Jeremy Rockliff, Minister for Health.        http://web.archive.org/web/20220101000841/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/increase-in-testing-capacity

column69: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 15th December 2021, additional local government areas (LGAs) in New South Wales and Victoria have been declared high-risk. Anyone travelling from a high-risk area must have a Tas e-Travel pass and negative COVID-19 test before arrival.      http://web.archive.org/web/20220103070330/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/lgas-in-victoria-and-nsw-declared-high-risk
column88: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 15th December 2021, anyone currently in Tasmania who has been in any of the high risk areas declared, must self monitor for symptoms, even if very mild and get tested immediately.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220103070330/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/lgas-in-victoria-and-nsw-declared-high-risk
column103: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, last updated 24th December, masks are mandatory in all public indoor spaces for people aged 12 years and older (unless exempt) in Tasmania.  These are identified as workplaces, educational settings (excluding primary school children but including staff), businesses, shops & accommodation, boats, indoor sporting & exercise facilities, service providers like banks, churches, centrelink, public transport, airports, aircrafts and outdoor events.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220111085651/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/keeping-yourself-safe/face-masks/mandatory-mask-wearing-in-public

column86: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 18th December 2021, more COVID-19 exposure sites have been identified and regularly updated at coronavirus.tas.gov.au for the general public awareness.  The Department of Health will report daily, all COVID-19 case numbers, testing numbers and vaccination progress, posted on the Department’s Facebook page.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220103070610/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/additional-covid-19-exposure-sites
column88: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 18th December 2021, anyone in Norwood, Launceston area experiencing any COVID-19 symptoms, regardless of how mild, should get tested immediately.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220103070238/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/covid-19-case-detected-in-northern-tasmania

column90: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website on 22nd December 2021, by 8pm Public Health Services had identified 12 cases of COVID-19 in Tasmania and is conducting contact tracing for all cases.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220105100355/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/covid-19-case-update2

column88: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 23rd December 2021, clinically recommended external triage areas are being prepared as a contingency for additional patient screening in the event Tasmania experiences community transmission of COVID-19.      http://web.archive.org/web/20220105111911/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/external-triage-areas-tested-at-rhh

column90: As published at Tasmanian Government COVID-19 Website, Media release on 25th December 2021, The Royal Hobart Hospital Incident Management Team is prioritizing contacting and testing all staff and patients who may have been exposed to the positive case of an employee.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220118000424/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/royal-hobart-hospital-employee-covid-19-case

column88: According to the information published on the Department of Health website on 16th October 2020, the national government policy for testing is that anyone with cold or flu-like symptoms should get tested. However, as the situation changes, states and territories may adjust their testing criteria based on local needs. This represents continuation in the COVID-19 policy response. This policy remained current on 17 January 2022.    https://web.archive.org/web/20211127034515/https://www.health.gov.au/news/health-alerts/novel-coronavirus-2019-ncov-health-alert/ongoing-support-during-coronavirus-covid-19/what-you-need-to-know-about-coronavirus-covid-19

column86: http://web.archive.org/web/20210503095648if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/vaccine
column88: People should get tested if they have any symptoms, no matter how mild. Archived web: http://web.archive.org/web/20210503165307if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/taking-test-covid-19#who-should-get-tested
column90: Comprehensive contact tracing for all identified cases. Check-in everywhere people visit using the state government’s Service Victoria QR codeapp, no matter how long a person spends at a location. Web archived: http://web.archive.org/web/20210503165307if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings
column103: You must always carry a face mask with you at all times when you leave the home, unless you have a lawful reason not to. Face masks are mandatory indoors unless an exception applies, or you are:  •	at home  •	at work, if a member of the public isn’t present and you are in an area that members of the public can’t normally access (such as an office space or a factory)  •	a staff member at a school, childcare or early childhood service, and a member of the public isn’t present (apart from at reception, where masks are always required)  •	a student at school  Face masks are recommended outdoors where physical distancing of at least 1.5 metres cannot be maintained, for example, at a busy open-air market or a bus stop.  Web archived: http://web.archive.org/web/20210503165307if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings
column106: The vaccination program in Australia commenced in Victoria from 22 Feb 2021. According to the state government, the vaccine is safe, free and universal. The rollout of the vaccine is made available in phases, with certain groups at highest risk of infection getting the first doses. Currently people in phase 1a and phase 1b are eligible to receive COVID-19 vaccines. From 3 May people who are 50 years and older are eligible to receive a COVID-19 vaccine.  Web archived: http://web.archive.org/web/20210503095648if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/vaccine
column115: Visitors of any age may visit a care facility (including people aged under 16 years). Visitors can be from different households, and they can bring dependents if alternative care arrangement cannot be made. Face masks must be worn.   People cannot visit a care facility (unless they have an exemption) if they are unwell with any COVID-19-like symptoms, waiting a COVID-19 test result, have arrived in Australia within the last 14 days (unless from a ‘green zone’), under self-isolation waiting clearance, and have visited a Tier 1 exposure site and advised to isolate, get tested and isolated for 14 days.   Some care facilities may have further limit visits.  Web archived: http://web.archive.org/web/20210503165307if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/visiting-care-facilities and http://web.archive.org/web/20210503165307if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings

column79: ***Updated from 2 to 1; majority of household-relevant debt relief expired on Jan 1; some limited measures still in place. column81: Date of spending announcements is unspecified, so recording a zero value for this indicator; however, the Australian government does have several fiscal measures in place:    At the Commonwealth level, fiscal stimulus, consisting of expenditure and revenue measures worthA$267billion (13¾ percent of GDP),has been put in place through FY2023-24. These measures are reflected in the FY2021 budget (released on October 6) and have been updated in the Mid-Year Economic and Fiscal Outlook (released on December 17).The majority of the stimulus will be executed through FY2020-21. The stimulus includes the multiyear JobMaker program(A$73 billion), comprising new measures (loss carry-backs and personal income tax cut), as well as the extension of existing measures (the temporary Coronavirus Supplement, other income support measures, full expensing, infrastructure investment, among others).The overall stimulus includes previously announced JobKeeperwage subsidies(5.4percent of GDP), income support to households, cash flow support to businesses, investment incentives, and targeted measures for affected regions and industries (including the HomeBuilder program supporting the construction industry).The Commonwealth government will also help finance a series of fast-track infrastructure projects across States and Territories (A$3.9 billion) and the arts and screen industries to support job creation, and has put in place a home care package to support senior citizens (A$0.3 billion). The Commonwealth government provided free childcare to around one million families through mid-July (A$0.3 billion) and announced targeted support to the education system. In mid-July, the government announced extensions of the JobKeeper wage subsidies through March 2021 and of the additional income support to households through December 2020, with payment reductions to facilitate a gradual transition to a recovery. It also instituted a new JobTrainer skills package (A$2 billion). Pandemic Leave Disaster Payment has been arranged with the State and Territory governments to provide a lump sum payment to help workers during their 14-day self-isolation period. The Commonwealth government will also invest in green technologies (A$1.9 billion) to lower emissions.    Other measures include an allocation of up to A$15 billion to invest in residential mortgage backed securities and asset backed securities to help funding for small banks and non-bank financial institutions, and loan guarantees between the Commonwealth government and participating banks to cover the immediate cash flow needs of SMEs (up to A$20 billion). In mid-July 2020, the latter scheme was extended through June 2021, with the maximum loan size raised from A$250,000 to A$1 million and the maximum maturity extended to five years.    Separately, the Commonwealth government has committed to spend an additional amount ofA$16.6 billion (0.8percent of GDP) to secure access to COVID-19 vaccines, roll out a national Vaccination Program, strengthen the health system, and protect vulnerable people, including those in aged care, from the outbreak of COVID-19. The Commonwealth government has also agreed with the States and the Territories to share the public health costs incurred by the States and Territories in treating the COVID-19.    State and Territory governments also announced fiscal stimulus packages, together amounting to A$47billion (2.5 percent of GDP), including payroll tax relief for businesses and relief for households, such as discount utility bills, cash payments to vulnerable households, support for health spending, construction, infrastructure packages, and green investment (renewable energy and technologies).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210107165916/https://www.imf.org/en/Topics/imf-and-covid19/Policy-Responses-to-COVID-19

column60: On Friday 8th Jan 2021, QLD Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk announced a three-day lockdown for the greater Brisbane area to stop the spread of the highly contagious UK strain of COVID-19. From 6pm Friday 8 January to 6pm Monday 11 January, people in the local government areas of Brisbane, Moreton Bay, Ipswich, Redlands and Logan will be required to stay at home except for essential reasons:   Shopping for essentials, food and necessary supplies  Medical or healthcare needs, including compassionate requirements  Exercise with no more than one other person, unless all from the same household  Providing care or assistance to an immediate family member  Work, or volunteering, or study if it is not reasonably practicable to work or learn remotely  Child custody arrangements  Legal obligations  Visit for end of life  Attend funeral or wedding in line with restrictions  In emergency situations  To escape harm  If you entered the impacted area responding to an emergency as an emergency health services or emergency service worker or national defence, state security or police officer  If required to allow an essential service to occur such as the transport of freight in and out of Greater Brisbane  You're directed to leave by an emergency health officer  You have an exemption from the Chief Health Officer.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210112234745/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91279   https://web.archive.org/web/20210113000857/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/news-events/doh-media-releases/releases/greater-brisbane-lockdown-clarifying-movement-restriction    Further to the Northern beaches restrictions in Sydney a lessening of those restrictions came into effect from the beginning of Sunday 10 January 2021.  "From the beginning of Sunday 10 January 2021, all Northern Beaches residents are to follow the restrictions for Greater Sydney."  While gathering restrictions remain in place (variously total number of people, % capacity of venues, square meter rule, and wearing of masks) all people living in NSW are now allowed to leave their homes for non-essential reasons.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210113002155/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/greater-sydney

From 6pm Friday 8 January to 6pm Monday 11 January, people in the local government areas of Brisbane, Moreton Bay, Ipswich, Redlands and Logan will be required to stay at home except for essential reasons.  With minimal exemptions this direction required that "A person must wear a face mask at all times when a person is outside of a principal place of residence"  https://web.archive.org/web/20210112234745/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91279   https://web.archive.org/web/20210113000857/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/news-events/doh-media-releases/releases/greater-brisbane-lockdown-clarifying-movement-restriction  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113010237/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-impacted-areas
column106: The Australian Government does have a COVID-19 Vaccination Policy published 13 November 2020. https://web.archive.org/web/20210113095811/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/12/australian-covid-19-vaccination-policy.pdf     The Australian Government also has entered into 4 separate agreements for the supply of COVID-19 vaccines, if they are proved to be safe and effective. The Australian Government has invested more than $3.3 billion through these 4 agreements, which will strengthen Australia's position to access safe and effective vaccines when they become available.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210102055658/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/about-covid-19-vaccines/australias-vaccine-agreements    However, this item is coded 0 because  vaccines are not yet being being administered in Australia.
column115: VIC: Directions from Deputy Chief Health Officer in accordance with emergency powers arising from declared state of emergency Care Facilities Directions (No 19). As of 3rd Jan 2021 and still current - minimal restrictions to people entering Aged Care facilities.  Individuals must not enter if they have returned from overseas within the last 14 day; have any COVID symptoms; have been in contact with a COVID-19 positive case; are otherwise required to be in isolation or quarantine. All visitors are required to make a declaration to his effect.   https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202101/Care%20Facilities%20Directions%20%28No%2019%29%20-%203%20January%202021%20signed.pdf    NSW: Public Health (COVID-19 Aged Care Facilities) Order (No 4) 2020. As of 15th Dec 2020 and still in effect, a person may not enter an Aged Care facility if: they have been overseas in the last 14 days; they have been in contact with a confirmed case in the last 14 days; temperature of 37.5 degrees or higher or symptoms of acute respiratory infection  https://web.archive.org/web/20201228041809/https://www.legislation.nsw.gov.au/file/Public%20Health%20(COVID-19%20Aged%20Care%20Facilities)%20Order%20(No%204)%202020.pdf    ACT: Public Health (Residential Aged Care Facilities) Emergency Direction 2020 (No3). From 26th June 2020 and still in effect, a person must not enter an Aged Care Facility if: they have been overseas in the last 14 days; they have been in contact with a confirmed case in the last 14 days; temperature of 37.5 degrees or higher or symptoms of acute respiratory infection, do not have an up to date vaccination against influenza.  Additionally, persons visiting for 'Care and support visit' who are a service provider can visit for a maximum of 2 hrs at one time if services cannot be provided by distance. Only two 'Care and support visitors' are allowed at any one time. Emergency Directions URL unable to be successfully archived.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210115064123/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/stay-safe-and-healthy/aged-care/advice-for-visitors-to-aged-care-facilities    TAS: Public Health Act 1997 DIRECTION UNDER SECTION 16 (Residential Aged Care Facilities - No. 10). In place since n 26 October 2020 and still current.  People may not enter an aged care facility if they: have been outside of Tasmania in a place that the Tasmanian Director of Public Health determines in medium or high risk; has any clinical symptoms of COVID-19; does not have an up-to-date influenza vaccination.  There are exceptions to these rules for end of life visits.  Care and Support visits are limited to 2 people at one time (unless preapproved for a larger number) and must be held in a non-communal area of the facility.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210117231413/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/126294/Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-No.-10-24-Oct-20.pdf        SA: The Emergency Management (Residential Aged Care Facilities No 22) (COVID-19) Direction 2021 came into effect on 9th January 2021 and is still current.  Individual must not enter a residential aged care facility if in the last 14 days they have been outside of SA in a community that is not a low community transmission zone or is not ordinarily a resident in the cross border corridor between South Australia and another state or territory; in the last 14 days has been a confirmed case or in contact with a confirmed case; has a temperature higher that 38 degrees Celsius or has had fever or chills in the preceding 72 hours, or symptoms of acute respiratory infection or last of taste and smell; is awaiting a COVID-19 test result; has not been vaccinated against 2020 seasonal influenza.  Personal care workers cannot works across multiple residential care facilities. All staff must complete COVID-19 infection control training.  This direction appears largely the same as the prior two directions which it has superseded: (Residential Aged Care Facilities No 21) (COVID-19) Direction 2021 was in effect from 1st Jan 2021-8th Jan 2021; and (Residential Aged Care Facilities No 20) (COVID-19) Direction 2021 which was in effect 21st Dec 2020 to 31st Dec 2020.  The primary substantive changes between these directions is the reclassification of areas outside of SA as being low community transmission area or otherwise.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210117232704/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/documents/emergency-directions-covid-19/EMA-Residential-Aged-Care-Direction-No-22.pdf  WA: Visitors to Residential Aged Care Facilities Directions (No.4) effective as of 11:50pm 18 September 2020 and still in effect.  A person must not enter an aged care facility if in the last 14 days they have returned from overseas or been informed that they are a close contact to a positive case.  They must also not enter a residential aged care facility if they not have an up to date influenza vaccination.  A person cannot ender if they have any symptoms of COVID-19.  Where services can be provided online or outside of the facility, they must be.    https://web.archive.org/web/20201003055507/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2020-09/Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Directions-No-4.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210117235140/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/document-collections/covid-19-coronavirus-business-and-community-activity    NT: COVID-19 Directions (No. 40) 2020 Directions for Aged Care Facilities came into effect 24th Jun 2020 and remain current.  This direction limits ‘Care and Support’ visitor to one at any time or two visitors if those visitors are visiting together.  A person must not enter a residential aged care facility if they: have been in contact with a positive case within the last 14 days; they currently have any symptoms of COVID-19; they do not have an up to date influenza vaccination.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210118000055/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210118000056/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0006/898953/cho-directions-no40-directions-aged-care-facilities.pdf

column24: In an easing of restrictions to the Greater Brisbane area QLD  "from 6pm AEST 11 January 2021 to 1am 22 January 2021, the Greater Brisbane region will be subject to new restrictions".  All businesses are now allowed to open with COVIDsafe restrictions including "All businesses may now have one person per 4 square metres on their premises indoors, or one person per 2 square metres outdoors. Businesses with a floor space less than 200 square metres can have one person per 2 square metres, up to 50 people at a time." Further no standing allowed at restaurants and cafes, dancing not allowed except at dance studios/classes and weddings, stadiums and theaters limited to 50% capacity.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210113003355/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/restrictions-impacted-areas  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113004212/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-impacted-areas    COVIDsafe restrictions for businesses in Greater Sydney area NSW remain without any substantive changes.  All businesses (with the exception of nightclubs) can open with COVIDsafe restrictions in place (square meter rule, collection of client contact details, and maximum numbers of patrons allowed).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210113002155/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/greater-sydney   https://web.archive.org/web/20210113004827/https://www.legislation.nsw.gov.au/file/Public%20Health%20%28COVID-19%20Restrictions%20on%20Gathering%20and%20Movement%29%20Order%20%28No%207%29%202020_210108_5.27pm.pdf

column42: In an easing of restrictions to the Greater Brisbane area QLD  "from 6pm AEST 11 January 2021 to 1am 22 January 2021, the Greater Brisbane region will be subject to new restrictions".  'Uncontrolled' public gatherings of up to 20 people allowed.  AND a person who is an owner, resident, tenant, occupier, temporary occupier or person in control of a residence must not organise or allow a gathering of more than 20 people including the members of the person’s household at the residence.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210113003355/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/restrictions-impacted-areas  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113004212/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-impacted-areas    However, even stricter limits on the number of persons allowed on residential premises remain for Greater Sydney NSW.  (a) for a place of residence in Greater Sydney—5 visitors on any day,  (b) otherwise (NSW but not Greater Sydney) —50 visitors at any one time.  Note. NSW Health recommends a maximum 30 visitors if the place of residence does  not have any outdoor space. This has been in effect since beginning of 31 December 2020.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210113004827/https://www.legislation.nsw.gov.au/file/Public%20Health%20%28COVID-19%20Restrictions%20on%20Gathering%20and%20Movement%29%20Order%20%28No%207%29%202020_210108_5.27pm.pdf
column60: In an easing of restrictions to the Greater Brisbane area QLD  "from 6pm AEST 11 January 2021 to 1am 22 January 2021, the Greater Brisbane region will be subject to new restrictions".  While COVIDsafe restrictions on gatherings remain in place (variously total number of people, square meter rules, and need to wear a mask) all people in QLD are able to leave their homes for non-essential reasons.  So long as residents follow these restrictions it does not appear that they are being discouraged from leaving their homes.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210113003355/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/restrictions-impacted-areas  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113004212/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-impacted-areas  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113030518/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/easing-greater-brisbane-restrictions
column69: 11th Jan 2021    ACT: As of 2pm 11th Jan 2021 ACT Chief Health Officer Dr Kerryn Coleman state that Greater Brisbane will no longer be classified as a COVID-19 affected area. This is an easing of restrictions and quarantine in no longer required for people travelling from Greater Brisbane QLD and the ACT.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210111031856/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/act-covid-19-update-11-january-2021    NSW:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     VIC:  At 5.59pm on Monday January 11 VIC introduced a new 'traffic light' boarder permit system. People from red zone areas are not allowed to enter VIC and will be turned away at the boarder. The Greater Sydney region (including the Blue Mountains and Wollongong) and Greater Brisbane (Brisbane City, Moreton Bay, Redlands, Ipswich and Logan local government areas) are currently red zones and will continue to be monitored daily and updated accordingly. Regional NSW will be classified orange - meaning that once in Victoria you must immediately self-isolate, get a COVID-19 test (within 72 hours) and continue to self-quarantine until you get a negative test result. People in Green Zones (the rest of Australia) can apply for a permit and enter Victoria if you don’t have COVID-19 symptoms and haven’t been to an orange or red zone.https://web.archive.org/web/20210113054625/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-11-january-2021    QLD: In an significant easing of restrictions Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk announced that Greater Brisbane lockdown would end at 6pm 11th Jan 2021. COVIDsafe restrictions remain in place.  However, residents of the Greater Brisbane are now able to leave their homes for non-essential reasons. https://web.archive.org/web/20210111000620/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91285    WA:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     SA: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     NT: The NT Chief Health Officer will revoke Greater Queensland as a hotspot from 11am on 11 January 2021. This is an easing of restrictions and quarantine in no longer required for people travelling from Greater Brisbane QLD and the NT.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210112044517/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-01-11-greater-brisbane-revoked-as-a-hotspot    TAS: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021
column103: In an easing of restrictions to the Greater Brisbane area QLD  "from 6pm AEST 11 January 2021 to 1am 22 January 2021, the Greater Brisbane region will be subject to new restrictions".  This required individuals to carry a face mask at all times and wear them: on public transport infrastructure or at public transport infrastructure; in a commercial passenger vehicle or waiting in a designated outdoor space that is not a residence for a commercial passenger vehicle; in an indoor space.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210113003355/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/restrictions-impacted-areas  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113004212/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-impacted-areas    In a tightening of restrictions from Sunday 3 January 2021 (restriction still current) the NSW govt made facemasks in Greater Sydney (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains) face masks are mandatory in the following indoor settings:  shopping (retail, supermarkets and shopping centres)  on public/shared transport and waiting areas for public/shared transport  indoor entertainment (including cinemas and theatres)  places of worship  hair and beauty premises  visiting an aged care facility.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113092916/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/face-masks    In an easing of restrictions the VIC govt From 5pm on 31 December 2020 direct that you must wear a fitted face mask when you are in public indoor spaces unless you have a lawful reason not to. This includes places such as:  shopping centres, supermarkets, retail outlets and markets  when visiting hospitals and aged care facilities   restaurants and cafes, when you are not eating or drinking  libraries  cinemas  churches and places of worship  entertainment facilities  recreational facilities and gyms  indoor workplaces.  Face masks continue to be mandatory on public transport and when in taxis or ride share vehicles.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113093135/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/face-masks-when-wear-face-mask    On the 8th Jan 2021 the National Cabinet met and all state and territory representatives agreed to make directions such that All passengers, excluding children 12 and under or those with particular exemptions, and air crew must wear masks on flights and in airports. https://web.archive.org/web/20210113093456/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/statement-national-cabinet

column69: 12th Jan 2021    ACT: ACT Chief Health Officer Dr Kerryn Coleman  announced changes to travel restrictions from COVID-19 affected areas of NSW.  As of 3pm 12th Jan 2021 ACT Public Health Direction will be amended to remove the Central Coast and Wollongong from the list of COVID-19 affected areas in NSW, as well as some areas of Greater Sydney. It will mean people currently in quarantine in the ACT from the Central Coast, Wollongong and some areas of Greater Sydney will no longer be required to quarantine. A Public Health Direction will remain in place for certain areas of Western and South-western Sydney which present the highest risk of COVID-19 transmission. This change to the Public Health Direction will mean ACT residents and non-residents who have been in the 11 Local Government Areas (LGAs) listed below at any time in the past 14 days will need to complete their quarantine period. https://web.archive.org/web/20210113063218/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/update-changes-to-covid-19-affected-areas-in-nsw    NSW:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021     VIC:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021     QLD: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     WA:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     SA: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021     NT: The Chief Health Officer has revoked Greater Metropolitan Sydney as a hotspot and has declared a number of Local Government Areas across Sydney’s Western Suburbs as hotspots from 11am on 12 January 2021. People arriving in the Northern Territory from Greater Metropolitan Sydney except the Local Government Areas that remain as hotspots, will no longer have to undertake mandatory supervised quarantine. Anyone currently in mandatory quarantine from the areas in Greater Metropolitan Sydney that have been revoked as hotspots will be able to exit. https://web.archive.org/web/20210113064206/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/20201-01-12-greater-metropolitan-sydney-hotspot-update    TAS: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021

13th Jan 2021    ACT: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 13th Jan 2021     NSW:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 13th Jan 2021     VIC:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 13th Jan 2021     QLD: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     WA:  No change to inter/intra state border rules on 11th Jan 2021     SA: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021     NT: No change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021     TAS: No substantive change to inter/intra state border rules on 12th Jan 2021.  While boarder closer with Greater Brisbane for those that were in Brisbane from 2nd Jan was already in place Tasmanian authorities further require as of 12th Jan 2021 that any "anyone in Tasmania who has spent time as a returned traveller from overseas and who was quarantined in Brisbane’s Hotel Grand Chancellor on or from the 30 December 2020 is asked to immediately self-isolate and call public health to arrange for a test." This is the quarantine hotel from which the more  virulent UK strain of COVID-19 has entered the Australian community. https://web.archive.org/save/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/media-releases/update-to-tasmanian-travel-restrictions
column81: On 13th January "[VIC] Minister for Disability, Ageing and Carers Luke Donnellan today announced 93 recipients of the $5 million [AUD] Community Food Relief Fund grants, which will provide a massive 2 million meals and 180,000 food hampers to vulnerable Victorians." https://web.archive.org/web/20210113080935/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/boosting-food-relief-victorian-communities

column69: 14th January 2021: In a modest easing of restrictions that SA govt announced that effective 12:01 am 14 January 2021 travellers may now travel to South Australia from all areas of New South Wales excluding Greater Sydney, Wollongong, and the Central Coast LGA. However, these traveller are required to self-quarantine and to a COVID-19 test on day 1, 5 and 12.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210118023702/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/restrictions-and-responsibilities/travel-restrictions     There were no other changes to Australia's inter/intra state boarder restrictions on 14th January 2021.

column69: ACT: As noted on 12th Jan 2021. ACT Chief Health Officer Dr Kerryn Coleman announced changes to travel restrictions from COVID-19 affected areas of NSW.  As of 3pm 12th Jan 2021 ACT Public Health Direction will be amended to remove the Central Coast and Wollongong from the list of COVID-19 affected areas in NSW, as well as some areas of Greater Sydney. It will mean people currently in quarantine in the ACT from the Central Coast, Wollongong and some areas of Greater Sydney will no longer be required to quarantine. A Public Health Direction will remain in place for certain areas of Western and South-western Sydney which present the highest risk of COVID-19 transmission. This change to the Public Health Direction will mean ACT residents and non-residents who have been in the 11 Local Government Areas (LGAs) listed below at any time in the past 14 days will need to complete their quarantine period.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210113063218/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/update-changes-to-covid-19-affected-areas-in-nsw   Currently as at 16th January the ACT has no other COVID intrastate or interstate boarder restrictions in place.    NSW: Currently 16th Jan NSW govt has no restrictions around intrastate or interstate travel.  However, NSW Health currently recommends delaying non-essential travel within NSW, especially between Greater Sydney and regional and rural areas.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210118010049/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/border-restrictions  VIC:  In an easing of boarder restrictions, Victoria’s Chief Health Officer has declared that from 6pm 16th Jan, Greater Brisbane will be considered ‘Orange Zones’. People from Orange Zones can apply for a Permit and enter Victoria, as long as they have not been in a currently listed red zone in the past 14 days and agree to the conditions of an Orange Zone Permit – self-quarantine, get a coronavirus (COVID-19) test within 72 hours and remain in self-quarantine until you receive a negative test result.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210116114132/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system    QLD: From the 21st Dec 2020 and remaining current as at 16th Jan 2021, the QLD govt has had boarder restrictions in place for the Greater Sydney area (35 Local Government Area).  “People who have been in a hotspot in the last 14 days or since the hotspot was declared (whichever is shorter), will only be able to enter Queensland if they are a returning Queensland resident or required to enter for a limited range of essential reasons. Queensland residents returning to Queensland from a hotspot must quarantine for 14 days on entering Queensland in government arranged accommodation at their own expense.” All people travelling from NSW need to apply for a boarder pass.  However, only those that have visiting a hotspot are required to quarantine. column103: VIC:   In an easing of restrictions the VIC govt has announced that from 11.59pm, Sunday 17 January 2021, face masks will no longer be mandatory in all public indoor settings. “Face masks continue to be mandatory in the following locations: on public transport; when riding in a ride share or taxi; inside shopping centres; including the retail stores inside shopping centres, such as clothing stores inside indoor markets in large retail stores (over 2000 sqm), such as department stores, electronics stores, furniture stores, hardware stores or supermarkets; on domestic flights to and from Victoria at airports;  when working in or visiting hospitals and care facilities. column103: On the 15/02/2021 changed from G2 to T3 to reflect the policy documented immediately below.     WA Perth and Peel region entered a lockdown period 31st Jan 2021 – 5th Feb 2021.  During this period the FACE COVERING DIRECTIONS were in force, requiring that:   " A person in an affected area [Perth and the Peel Region] must wear a face covering at all times while they are outside the place where they ordinarily reside including while in a vehicle unless:   (a) the person is a child under the age of 12 years; or  (b) the person has a physical or mental illness, condition or disability which makes  wearing a face covering unsuitable; or  ( c) the person is a prisoner or detainee in a prison, detention centre or other place  of custody; or   ( d) the person falls within one or more of the exceptions specified in paragraph 4  and is carrying a face covering ( or, in the case of paragraph 4( d), the person has  access to a facemask close by) and resumes wearing the face mask as soon as  reasonably practicable.     4. The exceptions specified in this paragraph are as follows:  (a) the person is communicating with a person who is deaf or hard of hearing and  visibility of the mouth is essential for communication;  (b) the nature of a person's work means that wearing a face covering creates a risk  to their health and safety;  ( c) the nature of a person's work or the activity that they are engaging in means that  clear enunciation or visibility of the mouth is essential;  ( d) the person is engaged in swimming as a form of physical exercise;  (e) the person is travelling in a private vehicle and is the sole occupant of that  vehicle;  (f) the person is consuming food, drink or medicine provided that they are seated  at the time and they are not in a vehicle;  (g) the person is undergoing medical care or treatment to the extent that such care  or treatment requires that no face covering be worn;  (h) the person is asked to remove the face covering to ascertain identity;  (i) not wearing a face covering is required for emergency purposes ( other than  emergency preparation or emergency preparation activities, unless another  exception specified in this paragraph applies);  U) not wearing a face covering is otherwise required or authorised by law;  (k) wearing a face covering is not safe in all the circumstances. "    https://web.archive.org/web/20210209070941/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-01/310121-Face-Covering-Directions.pdf

column79: On 24 September 2020, the Government announced changes to Australia’s insolvency framework to better serve Australian small businesses, their creditors and their employees. The changes, which commenced on 1 January 2021, introduce new insolvency processes suitable for small businesses by reducing the complexity, time and costs required.  The changes will enable more Australian small businesses to quickly restructure in order to survive the economic impact of COVID-19. Where restructure is not possible, businesses can wind up faster, enabling greater returns for creditors and employees.  These reforms are the most significant changes to the Australian insolvency framework in almost 30 years. They form part of the Government’s JobMaker plan to ensure Australia emerges from the pandemic with a stronger, more resilient and more competitive economy. The need to give businesses and their creditors certainty is crucial to kick-starting confidence and activity as the economy transitions to the recovery phase.  /web/20210201223455/https://business.gov.au/risk-management/emergency-management/coronavirus-information-and-support-for-business/temporary-relief-for-financially-distressed-businesses
column88: ***Recoded from 3 to 2; only one day had been changed to 3 without sufficient evidence     Previous coding note:   Where can I find a COVID-19 testing clinic?  Find a COVID clinic in your area, visit:    ACT — covid19.act.gov.au  Qld — covid19.qld.gov.au  NT — coronavirus.nt.gov.au  NSW — nsw.gov.au/covid-19  SA — covid-19.sa.gov.au  Tas — coronavirus.tas.gov.au  Vic — dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus  WA — healthywa.wa.gov.au  Can I get tested for COVID-19 in my home?  Testing for COVID-19 is a matter for states and territories, so visit your state or territory website for more information — or to find local testing centres.    Sydney, New South Wales  People living in Sydney can arrange self-collection tests at home by visiting covidtestsydney.com.au. To get the test, you will first need to complete a questionnaire on the website.    South Australia    Patients need a referral from their GP in metropolitan and regional SA to access home-based COVID-19 testing services. Go to sahealth.sa.gov.au and search for ‘COVID-19 Clinics and Testing Centres’ for more information.    Victoria  The free Call-to-Test service can provide in-home COVID-19 testing to Melbourne-based Victorians who are unable to leave their home to get tested. This includes older people, Victorians with a disability or mental health issue, carers and people with a chronic health condition that affects mobility.    To be eligible, callers must either have COVID-19 symptoms or be asymptomatic but identified by the Victorian Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) as a close contact.    Call the Victorian Coronavirus Hotline on 1800 675 398 and select option 9. An operator can discuss your options and support you through the process. To learn more about the Call-to-Test service, visit dhhs.vic.gov.au/call-to-test-covid-19.    Aged-care homes  The Commonwealth Government has engaged Sonic Healthcare to provide a pathology service for residents of aged-care homes. A specialised collector will attend an aged-care facility if a doctor requests for a test. If a result is positive, Sonic will send a specialised COVID-19 collection team to collect samples from all staff and residents.    If you're unwell and think you might have COVID-19, check your symptoms using the healthdirect Symptom Checker.    Call your doctor for advice. You can also access telehealth services, which are subsidised by Medicare.    You can use telephone or video conferencing from your own home or aged-care facility.   /web/20210201224146/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/coronavirus-covid-19-seeing-a-doctor-getting-tested-faqs

column74: According to the Australian Government Department of Home Affairs website, "Australia has strict border measures in place to protect the health of the Australian community. Very limited flights are currently available to and from Australia and you may not be able to travel at this time... You are automatically exempt from the travel restrictions and can enter Australia (without obtaining an individual exemption) if you are:  an Australian citizen  a permanent resident of Australia  an immediate family member of an Australian citizen or permanent resident*  a New Zealand citizen usually resident in Australia and their immediate family members  a person who has been in New Zealand for 14 days or more immediately prior to arrival by air in Australia  a diplomat accredited to Australia, including their immediate family members (each member of the family unit must hold a valid subclass 995 visa)  a person transiting Australia for 72 hours or less  airline crew, maritime crew including marine pilots  a person recruited under the Government approved Seasonal Worker Program or Pacific Labour Scheme  a person who holds a Business Innovation and Investment (subclass 188) visa."    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208231635/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions
column86: In addition to the preexisting campaign, a Media release by Greg Hunt Minister for Health and Aged Care published 27 January 2021 announced that "a public information campaign to encourage Australians to get a COVID-19 vaccine begins today. column88: Current as of 8th February 2021: There is some variation between states and territories as to who is eligible to receive a COVID-19 test.  However, eligibility criteria do apply across all states and territories.  To receive a test a person must be experiencing symptoms and/or meet additional criteria (for example, have visited a specific location identified by public health officials as a ‘hot spot’ or have been in contact with a positive case).  If eligibility criteria are met, testing is provided free of charge.  Below are archived links documenting the specific policy of each state and territory.     NSW  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209063617/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/how-to-protect-yourself-and-others/symptoms-and-testing   ACT  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209063728/https://www.health.act.gov.au/health-professionals/chief-health-officer-alerts/covid-19-testing-information   VIC  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209063522/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/getting-tested   TAS  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209063831/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/keeping-yourself-safe/testing-for-covid19   SA  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209064005/https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/conditions/infectious+diseases/covid-19/testing+and+tracing/testing+for+covid-19   WA  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209064215/https://www.healthywa.wa.gov.au/Articles/A_E/Coronavirus/COVID19-testing   NT  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209064247/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/symptoms-testing
column103: WA Perth and Peel region entered a lockdown period 31st Jan 2021 – 5th Feb 2021.  During this period the FACE COVERING DIRECTIONS were in force, requiring that:   " A person in an affected area [Perth and the Peel Region] must wear a face covering at all times while they are outside the place where they ordinarily reside including while in a vehicle unless:   (a) the person is a child under the age of 12 years; or  (b) the person has a physical or mental illness, condition or disability which makes  wearing a face covering unsuitable; or  ( c) the person is a prisoner or detainee in a prison, detention centre or other place  of custody; or   ( d) the person falls within one or more of the exceptions specified in paragraph 4  and is carrying a face covering ( or, in the case of paragraph 4( d), the person has  access to a facemask close by) and resumes wearing the face mask as soon as  reasonably practicable.     4. The exceptions specified in this paragraph are as follows:  (a) the person is communicating with a person who is deaf or hard of hearing and  visibility of the mouth is essential for communication;  (b) the nature of a person's work means that wearing a face covering creates a risk  to their health and safety;  ( c) the nature of a person's work or the activity that they are engaging in means that  clear enunciation or visibility of the mouth is essential;  ( d) the person is engaged in swimming as a form of physical exercise;  (e) the person is travelling in a private vehicle and is the sole occupant of that  vehicle;  (f) the person is consuming food, drink or medicine provided that they are seated  at the time and they are not in a vehicle;  (g) the person is undergoing medical care or treatment to the extent that such care  or treatment requires that no face covering be worn;  (h) the person is asked to remove the face covering to ascertain identity;  (i) not wearing a face covering is required for emergency purposes ( other than  emergency preparation or emergency preparation activities, unless another  exception specified in this paragraph applies);  U) not wearing a face covering is otherwise required or authorised by law;  (k) wearing a face covering is not safe in all the circumstances. "    https://web.archive.org/web/20210209070941/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-01/310121-Face-Covering-Directions.pdf
column115: WA Perth and Peel region entered a lockdown period 31st Jan 2021 – 5th Feb 2021.  During this period the VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES DIRECTIONS (NO 5)  were in force.  These directions banned any visitors to residential aged care facilities, except in end of life circumstance.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210209071953/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-01/20210131-Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Directions-No5.pdf

column15: The VIC govt COVID19  website states that "new restrictions apply from 11:59pm, Wednesday 3 February 2021"..."In line with advice from the Victorian Chief Health Officer, all high-school students and school-based staff must carry a face mask at all times and wear a face mask in indoor settings such as schools, early childhood education centres and places of study, unless an exception applies."  The VIC govt COVID19 website also outline physical distancing requirements for schools, which include that adults at school (staff and visitors) must maintain a 1.5m distance and also density limit of 1 person per 2m squared.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208012821if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/face-masks-study-and-school https://web.archive.org/web/20210208013113if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/physical-distancing-health-and-hygiene  ***Updated to indicte this policy on actual start date of Feb 3

column69: Each Australian State and Territory has different boarder travel restrictions in place.  Changes implemented on the 4th Feb 2021 are:   WA:   WA Government announcement published 29 January 2021 – stated that from 12.01am, Friday 5th February Victoria would move from the ‘low risk’ to ‘very low risk’ category.  “Under the ‘very low risk’ category, safe travel is permitted into WA, subject to the following updated conditions: completion of a G2G PASS prior to travel, stipulating: they do not have any COVID-19 symptoms, which jurisdictions the traveller has been in over the previous 14 days, undergoing a health screening and temperature test if arriving at Perth Airport, being prepared to take a COVID-19 test at the airport COVID clinic, if deemed necessary by a health clinician, undergoing a health screening if arriving by land.” The announcement further stated that “New South Wales remains at ‘low risk’ category, which means travel is permitted subject to strict conditions including 14 days of self-quarantine and COVID-19 testing”.    However, at 7:51pm AEST 4th February 2021, Premier Mark McGowan reversed the decision to move Victoria from a ‘low risk’ to a ‘very low risk’ category.  At the 4th Feb 2021, WA considers NSW and VIC to be ‘low risk’.  WA considers all other Australian states and territories to be ‘very low risk’.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208054724/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/queensland-and-victoria-transition-very-low-risk-category   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208060723if_/https://www.facebook.com/photo?fbid=264413555047497&set=a.263657845123068     ACT:   On the 4th February 2021, Victoria imposed new state-wide restrictions starting today after a hotel quarantine worker at the Grand Hyatt Hotel tested positive to COVID-19.  The ACT government stipulated that “If you are currently in the ACT, and you have been in a Tier 1 Victorian exposure site or identified as a close contact, you must immediately enter quarantine and get tested for COVID-19. You must complete an online declaration form within 24 hours of the Public Health Direction coming into effect today. You must remain in quarantine for 14 days from the date on which you attended the exposure site.” Further “If you are planning to enter the ACT, and you have been in a Tier 1 Victorian exposure site or identified as a close contact, you are required by DHHS to quarantine in Victoria. You must not travel to the ACT without an exemption from ACT Health as well as from Victorian authorities. Your exemptions must be granted prior to travel.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063357/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/new-travel-restriction-for-covid-19-affected-areas-of-victoria   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063501/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/case-locations-and-outbreaks-covid-19     NT:   As documented on the NT Government COVID-19 website: The Melbourne (VIC) LGAs of Brighton, Clayton South, Heatherton, Keysborough, Melbourne, Moorabbin Airport, Noble Park, Springvale, West Melbourne, and Wheelers Hill – were declared hot spots at 3:30pm (NT time) 4th Feb 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208072331/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19

All business and venues can re-open, except for the casino and nightclubs;  4 square metre capacity rule and 150-person capacity at hospitality, entertainment, fitness venues and events including weddings, funerals (excluding staff);".   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208010107/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/02/Post-lockdown-plan-to-come-into-effect-6pm-Friday-February-5.aspx  https://web.archive.org/web/20210207114449/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/transition-out-of-lockdown-6pm-friday-5-february     VIC:   The VIC Govt. COVID19 websites states that "if you can’t work from home you can go to work. Workers who must attend onsite due to the nature of the work may continue to do so and there is no limit on the number of people who can attend. From Monday 18 January 2021, up to 50 per cent of office workers who are not in the public service have been able to return to on-site work as well as up to 25 per cent of public service workers. Offices with fewer than 40 staff can have 20 staff on-site subject to density quotients." This is up to date at 8th Feb 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208014511/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/work-and-study    QLD:   The QLD GOVT. COVID -19 website states that from “1am AEST 22 January 2021, the Greater Brisbane region is subject to the same restrictions as the rest of the state.” … “All restricted businesses can open, in line with the COVID Safe Framework. All restricted businesses may now have one person per 2 square metres on their premises, other than a hospitality business that does not comply with the requirement to collect contact details electronically. Venues can operate with up to 100 per cent seated capacity if patrons are in ticketed, allocated seating. Dance floors are also allowed indoors and outdoors, and you can stand up and have a drink at bar.”  All businesses can open.  However, there are requirements for some businesses to collect customer contact details, some density requirements.  Some venues and events are required to use a COVID checklist or develop a COVID Safe Event Plan.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208020314/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/business-activity-undertaking-direction   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208020752/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/easing-greater-brisbane-restrictions     NSW:   Current as of 8th Feb 2021, The NSW Government COVID19 website outlines current restrictions on businesses.  Restrictions vary by type of business and include: mandatory staff mask wearing, customer density rules (one person per 2 or 4 square meters), and maximum numbers of people allowed in specific venues of events.  There are no current mandatory work from home restrictions in NSW.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208020752/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/easing-greater-brisbane-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210114055257/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/covid-safe     ACT:   The Australian Capital Territory Public Health (Restricted Activities –Gatherings, Business or Undertakings) Emergency Direction 2020 (No16), in effect since 11:59pm Mon 16 December 2020 and still current does not require the closure of any business.  However, it does outline restrictions including use of the ACT Government tracing App; total number of allowable customers; customer density requirements; and maximum allowable percentage of venue capacity used.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208030940/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/business-and-work  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208030914/https://www.legislation.act.gov.au/ni/2020-801/     TAS:   According to the Tasmania Government’s COVID-19 website, las updated 5th February 2021, “All businesses and workplaces are permitted to operate, but must implement measures to meet the minimum COVID-19 safety standards and record this in a COVID-19 Safety plan.”  Measures include mandatory contact tracing, maximum allowable numbers within venues/spaces, and maximum allowable percentage of venue capacity used.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208031511/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/business-and-employees/business-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208031755/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/gatherings-density-and-physical-distancing     SA:  The current South Australia Emergency Management (Public Activities No 18) (COVID-19) Direction 2021 does not require the closing of any business.  However, it does stipulate a range of restrictions on business operations.  Restrictions include: maximum person densities, that food and beverage must be consumed while seated at hospitality venues, appropriate personal protective equipment be worn by staff providing personal care services; maximum allowable percentage of venue capacity used.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208032027/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/documents/emergency-directions-covid-19/Emergency-Management-Public-Activities-No-18-Direction-2021.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208032042/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/business-and-work/business-restrictions-and-closures    NT:   The Northern Territory COVID-19 Directions (No. 56) 2020: Safety Measures for Places, Businesses, Activities, Services and Premises does not require the closing of any business.  However, it does require that businesses provide customers with hand sanitiser, collection contract information from customers, ensure physical distancing of 1.5M otherwise keepi9ng contact to less than 15 minutes.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208043605/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0012/951879/cho-directions-no56-safety-measures-places-businesses-activities-services-premises.pdf
