column69: Each Australian State and Territory has different boarder travel restrictions in place.  Outlined below are changes implemented on 5th February 2021.     ACT:   In an easing of travel restrictions, the ACT lifted all restriction to travellers from the WA Perth and Peel region from 6pm AEST 5th February 2021.  The restriction had been in place since 2pm AEDT 1st Feb 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208062659/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/travel-restrictions-in-wa-lifted    NT:  As documented on the NT Government COVID-19 website: Perth and the Peel region WA were declared hot spots between 7:30pm (NT time) 31st Jan 2021 and 7:30pm (NT time) 5th Feb 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208072331/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19     QLD:   As documented on the QLD Government COVID-19 website:From 1am AEST Saturday 6 February 2021 the 12 LGAs that make up the South West region of Western Australia are removed as COVID-19 hotspots.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208070602/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19     NSW:   As documented on the NSW Government COVID-19 website “From the 5th February 2021, Victoria is declared an affected area. Arrivals by air or rail must complete a declaration form.  If you are not a NSW resident and you have been in a place of high concern you must not enter NSW. If you are a returning NSW resident and you have been in a place of high concern at the time specified in a COVID-19 concerns notice, you must self-isolate for 14 days, and follow the Interstate Traveller Guidelines.“  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063914/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/border-restrictions   In an easing of restrictions, as documented on the NSW Government COVID-19 website “The Public Health (COVID-19 Western Australia) Order has been repealed. From 9pm on Friday 5 February: There is no requirement to provide a declaration when entering NSW from Western Australia, and there is no obligation for anyone from WA to follow a stay at home direction. Visit the Government of Western Australia – locations visited by confirmed cases for case locations in Western Australia. Follow the advice on testing and self-isolation rules if you have visited any of these locations.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208064831/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/interstate-hotspots   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208065142/https://www.legislation.nsw.gov.au/file/Public%20Health%20%28COVID-19%20Western%20Australia%29%20Order%202021.pdf     VIC:   In an easing of restrictions, as documented on the VIC Government COVID-19 website: “Victoria’s Chief Health Officer has declared the Perth metropolitan area, the Peel region and the South West region of Western Australia will move from a red zone to an orange zone under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system at 9pm Victorian time today, Friday 5 February.”  Further “Following continued low community transmission and exposure risk, from 6:00pm on 29 January 2021 the last red zone in New South Wales, the Local Government Area (LGA) of Cumberland, has transitioned to an orange zone. All remaining orange zones have transitioned to green. Anyone with a valid permit will be able to enter Victoria from New South Wales.” This was current as of 8th Feb 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208065455/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covid-19-daily-update  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208065849/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system     SA:  As documented on the SA Police Border Travel website:   2.	“In effect from 3:26 pm 5 February 2021 [and still current 08th Feb 2012]:    The regions of Peel, Perth and South West are now considered a restricted zone [this is an easing of previous restriction].  Travellers arriving from Peel, Perth and South West, must self-quarantine at a suitable place and submit to COVID-19 testing on day 1, 5 and 12. They can be released from self-quarantine upon written advice of a negative COVD-19 test result.  All travellers who are required to undertake a COVID-19 test on days 1, 5 and 12 must wear a mask any time they come into contact with the public until they have received their first negative COVID-19 test result.  Travellers who are already in South Australia and arrived from those Peel, Perth and South West are now released from self-quarantine upon written advice of a negative test result, with the exception of persons who had been in the Sheraton Four Points Hotel who must complete their supervised quarantine.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208073826/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/emergency-declarations/cross-border-travel
column103: At 6pm 5th Feb 2021 SAFE TRANSITION FOR WESTERN AUSTRALIA DIRECTIONS came into effect and remain current.   These directions state that a "A person in the affected area [Perth and the Peel Region] must wear a face covering at all times while they are outside the place where they ordinarily reside, including while in a vehicle (including a motor  vehicle, boat or aircraft), unless:  (a) the person is a child 12 years of age or under, except at a time when the child is  attending school in Year 7 or above; or  (b) the person has a physical, developmental or mental illness, injury, condition or  disability which makes wearing a face covering unsuitable; or  ( c) the person is a prisoner or detainee in a prison, detention centre or other place  of custody; or  Note: nothing in these directions affects any other power a person may have to  require a prisoner or detainee to wear a face covering.  ( d) the person falls within one or more of the exceptions specified in paragraph 14  and is carrying a face covering (or, in the case of paragraph 14(g) or (h), the person has access to a face covering close by) and resumes wearing the face covering as soon as reasonably practicable.  14. The exceptions specified in this paragraph are as follows:  (a) the person is communicating with a person who is deaf or hard of hearing and  visibility of the mouth is essential for communication;  (b) the person is at the time attending school as a student in Year 6 or below;  ( c) the nature of a person's occupation means that wearing a face covering is  impractical to perform that occupation or creates a risk to their health and safety;  ( d) the person needs to temporarily remove their mask so as to enable another  person to appropriately perform their occupation;  ( e) the nature of a person's work or the activity that they are engaging in means that  clear enunciation or visibility of the mouth is essential;  (f) the person is a teacher who at the time is engaging in teaching primary,  secondary or tertiary students or a child care worker engaging in child care;  (g) the person is engaged in an activity involving swimming;  (h) the person is running or jogging or otherwise engaged in some form of strenuous  or vigorous exercise or physical activity that is taking place outside;  (i) the person is travelling in a motor vehicle and is the sole occupant of that  vehicle;  G) the person is consuming food, drink or medicine, provided that they are seated  at the time and they are not in a vehicle;  (k) the person is undergoing medical, dental or beauty related care or treatment to  the extent that such care or treatment requires that no face covering be worn;  (1) the person is asked to remove the face covering to ascertain identity;  (m) not wearing a face covering is required for emergency purposes ( other than  emergency preparation or emergency preparation activities, unless another  exception specified in this paragraph applies);  (n) the person is directed by a judicial officer or tribunal member in proceedings in  a court or tribunal to remove their mask to ensure the proper conduct of those  proceedings;  ( o) not wearing a face covering is otherwise required or authorised by law; or  (p) wearing a face covering is not safe in all the circumstances. "  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209070248/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/2021-02-05-Safe-Transition-for-Western-Australia-Directions.pdf
column115: In an easing of restrictions within the WA Perth and Peel region at 6pm 5th February, the VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES DIRECTIONS (NO 6) came into effect.  These directions allow for Care and support visits to residents of aged care facilities.  Care and support visit, in relation to a resident of a residential aged care facility, means a visit made to the resident for the purposes of providing care and support to the resident, provided that no more than one person is  making a care and support visit at any given time.  Therefore reverting code back to 2G.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210209072601/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/2021-02-05Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Directions-%28No-6%29_5-February-2021.pdf

column69: Each Australian State and Territory has different boarder travel restrictions in place.  Below are changes implement on 6th Feb 2021.     QLD:   As documented on the QLD Government COVID-19 website:  From 1am AEST Saturday 6 February 2021 the 12 LGAs that make up the South West region of Western Australia are removed as COVID-19 hotspots.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208070602/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19

column15: Monday the 8th February is the first school day after the announcement and implementation of this policy change.   WA Premier Mark McGowan press release dated 4th Feb 2021 states, in part, " Current lockdown ends, pending no further local cases, as planned at 6pm tomorrow [5th Feb 2021]"..."Under the post-lockdown transition measures, Perth and Peel schools will start from Monday, February 8, with the mandatory mask rule applying to all school staff and secondary students. Masks are not required for primary school students."  While schools are open.  However, mandatory mask wearing is public health precautions that result in significant differences as compared to usual, non-Covid-19 operations. Therefore coded 1.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210207114449/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/transition-out-of-lockdown-6pm-friday-5-february  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208010107/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/02/Post-lockdown-plan-to-come-into-effect-6pm-Friday-February-5.aspx    Mask mandate continues in Victoria high schools   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208012821if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/face-masks-study-and-school
column69: Each Australian State and Territory has different boarder travel restrictions in place.  Outlined below are the restrictions in place and current on the 8th February 2021.     ACT: On the 4th February 2021, Victoria imposed new state-wide restrictions starting today after a hotel quarantine worker at the Grand Hyatt Hotel tested positive to COVID-19.  The ACT government stipulated that “If you are currently in the ACT, and you have been in a Tier 1 Victorian exposure site or identified as a close contact, you must immediately enter quarantine and get tested for COVID-19. You must complete an online declaration form within 24 hours of the Public Health Direction coming into effect today. You must remain in quarantine for 14 days from the date on which you attended the exposure site.” Further “If you are planning to enter the ACT, and you have been in a Tier 1 Victorian exposure site or identified as a close contact, you are required by DHHS to quarantine in Victoria. You must not travel to the ACT without an exemption from ACT Health as well as from Victorian authorities. Your exemptions must be granted prior to travel.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063357/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/new-travel-restriction-for-covid-19-affected-areas-of-victoria   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063501/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/case-locations-and-outbreaks-covid-19       NSW:   As documented on the NSW Government COVID-19 website “From the 5th February 2021, Victoria is declared an affected area. Arrivals by air or rail must complete a declaration form.  If you are not a NSW resident and you have been in a place of high concern you must not enter NSW. If you are a returning NSW resident and you have been in a place of high concern at the time specified in a COVID-19 concerns notice, you must self-isolate for 14 days, and follow the Interstate Traveller Guidelines.“  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063914/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/border-restrictions     VIC:   In an easing of restrictions, as documented on the VIC Government COVID-19 website: “Victoria’s Chief Health Officer has declared the Perth metropolitan area, the Peel region and the South West region of Western Australia will move from a red zone to an orange zone under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system at 9pm Victorian time today, Friday 5 February.”  Further “Following continued low community transmission and exposure risk, from 6:00pm on 29 January 2021 the last red zone in New South Wales, the Local Government Area (LGA) of Cumberland, has transitioned to an orange zone. All remaining orange zones have transitioned to green. Anyone with a valid permit will be able to enter Victoria from New South Wales.” This was current as of 8th Feb 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208065455/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covid-19-daily-update  https://web.archive.org/web/20210208065849/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system       QLD:   As documented on the QLD Government COVID-19 website: “From 7.55pm AEST Sunday 31 January 2021 specific Western Australia LGAs and unincorporated areas are declared as COVID-19 hotspots.  From 1am AEST Saturday 6 February 2021 the 12 LGAs that make up the South West region of Western Australia are removed as COVID-19 hotspots.  Border restrictions apply to anyone who have been in a hotspot in the previous 14 days or since the start date identified for the hotspot (whichever is shorter). For these Western Australia hotspots, the identified start date is 1am AEST 25 January 2021. This is the date is when the risk of COVID-19 transmission started.”  This is current as of 8th February 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208070602/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19      NT:  As documented on the NT Government COVID-19 website: The Melbourne (VIC) LGAs of Brighton, Clayton South, Heatherton, Keysborough, Melbourne, Moorabbin Airport, Noble Park, Springvale, West Melbourne, and Wheelers Hill – were declared hot spots between 3:30pm (NT time) 4th Feb 2021 and 3:30pm (NT time) 8th Feb 2021.  The Melbourne (VIC) LGAs of Maidston, Sunshine, and Taylors Lakes were declared 3:30pm (NT time) 8th Feb 2021 and remain current.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208072331/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19     WA: As of 8th Feb 2021, WA considers NSW and VIC to be ‘low risk’.  WA considers all other Australian states and territories to be ‘very low risk’.  14 day quarantine applies to travelers from 'low risk' areas.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208054724/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/queensland-and-victoria-transition-very-low-risk-category   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208060723if_/https://www.facebook.com/photo?fbid=264413555047497&set=a.263657845123068     At to date as of 8th February 2021, tourists and travellers (including from within WA) are not allowed to enter some remote Aboriginal communities.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063753/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-remote-aboriginal-communities-travel    SA:   As documented on the SA Police Border Travel website:   1.	“In effect from 6.16pm on 4 February 2021 [and still current 08th Feb 2012]:   Any person who was:   physically present on the site of the Grand Hyatt Hotel, 123 Collins St, Melbourne  between 12.01 am 21 January 2021 and  12.01 am 31 January 2021: or  a person who was the close contact of a person who was physically on the site of Grand Hyatt Hotel, 123 Collins St, Melbourne  between 12.01 am 21 January 2021 and  12.01 am 31 January 2021, within an enclosed space for a period of 1 hrs or longer, or within 1.5 metres of the other person for any period of time,  must notify SA Health, to be subjected to supervised quarantine for 14 days and submit to COVID Testing Day 1, 5 and 12.”…  Further ” Any arrival from Greater Melbourne Area since 12.01 am 28 January 2021 to self-quarantine at a suitable place and submit to COVID-19 testing on Day 1, 5 and 12.  Upon written confirmation of a negative COVID-19 result, person is released from the self-quarantine requirement, but must complete the testing regime.”    2.	“In effect from 3:26 pm 5 February 2021 [and still current 08th Feb 2012]:    The regions of Peel, Perth and South West are now considered a restricted zone [this is an easing of previous restriction].  Travellers arriving from Peel, Perth and South West, must self-quarantine at a suitable place and submit to COVID-19 testing on day 1, 5 and 12. They can be released from self-quarantine upon written advice of a negative COVD-19 test result.  All travellers who are required to undertake a COVID-19 test on days 1, 5 and 12 must wear a mask any time they come into contact with the public until they have received their first negative COVID-19 test result.  Travellers who are already in South Australia and arrived from those Peel, Perth and South West are now released from self-quarantine upon written advice of a negative test result, with the exception of persons who had been in the Sheraton Four Points Hotel who must complete their supervised quarantine.”    3.	Effective 12:01 am on 31 January 2021 [and still current 08th Feb 2012]   [This is an easing of previous restriction] A non-essential traveller who is travelling from Greater Sydney Region, Central Coast LGA, or Wollongong LGA, will be permitted to enter South Australia if they self-quarantine at a suitable location and submit to COVID-19 testing within 24 hours of arrival, on day 5, and again on day 12. This also applies to South Australian residents who have travelled from the Greater Sydney Region, Central Coast LGA, Wollongong LGA. Once the person receive a negative test result (in writing), there will no longer be a requirement to remain in quarantine. Testing on day 5 and day 12 will still be mandatory.   No restrictions apply to travellers coming from New South Wales if they have not been in the prohibited locations (Greater Sydney Region, Central Coast LGA, or Wollongong LGA).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208073826/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/emergency-declarations/cross-border-travel     TAS  As documented on the TAS Government COVID-19 website: Some specific premises at defined times and dates have been identified as ‘High Risk’.  These specific premises are in Melbourne VIC and Sydney/Wollongong NSW.  Travellers who have spent time in a high-risk premise or location in the 14 days before arriving in Tasmania are not permitted to enter Tasmania, unless approved as an Essential Traveller.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208074113/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208074301/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert

column74: Current as at the 11th Feb 2021: The Australian Government Department of Home Affairs website states that:   "Australia has strict border measures in place to protect the health of the Australian community. Very limited flights are currently available to and from Australia and you may not be able to travel at this time." ... "You are automatically exempt from the travel restrictions and can enter Australia (without obtaining an individual exemption) if you are:    an Australian citizen  a permanent resident of Australia  an immediate family member of an Australian citizen or permanent resident*  a New Zealand citizen usually resident in Australia and their immediate family members  a person who has been in New Zealand for 14 days or more immediately prior to arrival by air in Australia  a diplomat accredited to Australia, including their immediate family members (each member of the family unit must hold a valid subclass 995 visa)  a person transiting Australia for 72 hours or less  airline crew, maritime crew including marine pilots  a person recruited under the Government approved Seasonal Worker Program or Pacific Labour Scheme  a person who holds a Business Innovation and Investment (subclass 188) visa."     https://web.archive.org/web/20210208231635/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions

The ACT government stipulated that “If you are currently in the ACT, and you have been in a Tier 1 Victorian exposure site or identified as a close contact, you must immediately enter quarantine and get tested for COVID-19. You must complete an online declaration form within 24 hours of the Public Health Direction coming into effect today. Your exemptions must be granted prior to travel.” This is up to date as at 11th Feb 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211055121/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/community/travel/victoria2   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211055307/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/updates/covid-19-affected-areas/covid-19-affected-areas-in-victoria     NSW:   As documented on the NSW Government COVID-19 website “From 5 February 2021 Victoria is declared an affected area.  Arrivals by air or rail must complete a declaration form. If you are not a NSW resident and you have been in a place of high concern you must not enter NSW. If you are a returning NSW resident and you have been in a place of high concern at the time specified in a COVID-19 concerns notice, you must self-isolate for 14 days, and follow the Interstate Traveller Guidelines.“  This is up to date as of 11th Feb 2021. https://web.archive.org/web/20210211055929/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/border-restrictions     Places of high concern continue to be updated.  The below link provides details of the places of high concern and is up to date at 11th Feb 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211060437/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/case-locations-and-outbreaks-covid-19       VIC:   In an easing of restrictions, as documented on the VIC Government COVID-19 website: “Victoria’s Chief Health Officer has declared that from 6:00pm 8 February, the Cumberland Local Government Area (LGA) in New South Wales will move from an orange zone to a green zone under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system.  “… “People in green zones may apply for a permit to enter Victoria provided they have not been in close contact with a coronavirus (COVID-19) case and do not have any coronavirus (COVID-19) symptoms. On arrival in Victoria, they must monitor for symptoms and abide by local directions.” As at 11th Feb 2021, all of NSW is considered a ‘Green Zone’ by VIC.    Victoria continues to consider the Perth and Peel Region of WA to be an Orange Zone. People in orange zones can apply for a permit to enter Victoria provided they have not been in close contact with a coronavirus (COVID-19) case and do not have any coronavirus (COVID19) symptoms.  This up to date as at 11th Feb 2021.  On arrival in Victoria, orange zone permit holders must isolate at their home or accommodation, get a coronavirus (COVID-19) test within 72 hours, and continue isolating until they have received a negative result.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210211061139/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/travel-updates     QLD:   As documented on the QLD Government COVID-19 website: “From 7.55pm AEST Sunday 31 January 2021 specific Western Australia LGAs and unincorporated areas are declared as COVID-19 hotspots.  From 1am AEST Saturday 6 February 2021 the 12 LGAs that make up the South West region of Western Australia are removed as COVID-19 hotspots.  Border restrictions apply to anyone who have been in a hotspot in the previous 14 days or since the start date identified for the hotspot (whichever is shorter). For these Western Australia hotspots, the identified start date is 1am AEST 25 January 2021. This is the date is when the risk of COVID-19 transmission started.”  This is current as of 11th February 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210208070602/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19      NT:  As documented on the NT Government COVID-19 website: The Melbourne (VIC) LGAs of Maidston, Sunshine, and Taylors Lakes were declared 3:30pm (NT time) 8th Feb 2021 and remain current.  The Melbourne LGA of Sunbury was declared on 9am 10th Feb 2021 and remains’ current.  The Holiday Inn Melbourne Airport, 10-14 Centre Rd, Melbourne Airport, Victoria 3045 was declared 4:00pm 10th Feb 2021 and remains current.  Anyone arriving into the Northern Territory from or through a declared hotspot in the last 14 days must undertake 14 days mandatory supervised quarantine.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210211061915/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19     WA: As of 11th Feb 2021, WA considers NSW and VIC to be ‘low risk’.  WA considers all other Australian states and territories to be ‘very low risk’.  14 day quarantine applies to travellers from 'low risk' areas.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211054102/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-interstate-border     As of 8th February 2021, tourists and travellers (including from within WA) are not allowed to enter some remote Aboriginal communities.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210208063753/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-remote-aboriginal-communities-travel   As of 11th Feb 2021, documented on the WA government’s COVID-19 website, “Only essential travel permitted in and out of the Perth and Peel regions to other parts of WA.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211054538/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do#Travel     SA:   In a tightening of restrictions, the SA Government COVID-19 website documents that “For travel on or after 12.01 am 11 February 2021; for persons who have been in Greater Melbourne on or after 4 February 2021, will be prohibited from entering South Australia unless they are an Essential Traveller or an exempt person (returning SA resident, genuine relocation, fleeing Domestic Violence – who will be required to self-quarantine at a suitable place for 14 days upon arrival and submit to COVID-19 testing on day 1, 5 and 12 pursuant to their restrictions).”     In a continuation of restrictions, “From 12:01 am 31 January 2021, travel is permitted from Greater Sydney, Wollongong and the Central Coast to South Australia. Upon arrival, travellers must immediately self-quarantine and must remain in that place until a negative COVID-19 test result is received. They must submit a COVID-19 test within 24 hours after their arrival, on the 5th day after their arrival and on the 12th day after their arrival in South Australia.” This remains current as at 11th Feb 2021.     In a continuation of restrictions “The regions of Peel, Perth and South West are now considered a restricted zone. Travellers arriving from Peel, Perth and South West, must self-quarantine at a suitable place and submit to COVID-19 testing on day 1, 5 and 12. They can be released from self-quarantine upon written advice of a negative COVD-19 test result. All travellers arriving from a restricted location who are required to undertake a COVID-19 test on days 1, 5 and 12 must wear a mask any time they come into contact with the public until they have received their first negative COVID-19 test result.”    https://web.archive.org/web/20210211020613/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/restrictions-and-responsibilities/travel-restrictions     TAS  In a continuation of restrictions and as documented on the TAS Government COVID-19 website: Some specific premises at defined times and dates have been identified as ‘High Risk’.  These specific premises are in Melbourne VIC and Sydney/Wollongong NSW.  Travellers who have spent time in a high-risk premise or location in the 14 days before arriving in Tasmania are not permitted to enter Tasmania, unless approved as an Essential Traveller.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210131102813/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/medium-risk-areas   Locations continue to be updated.  A list of up to date locations, as at the 11th Feb 2021 can be viewed at this link:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211062826/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert
column77: 11th Feb 2021:   According to the Australian Government Treasury website, Jobkeeper continues to be paid to eligible employers who must must pass the full payment onto their eligible employees.  To be currently eligible to receive job keeper: Businesses and not-for-profits will need to demonstrate that they have met the relevant decline in turnover test in the December 2020 quarter to remain eligible for the March 2021 quarter.   https://treasury.gov.au/sites/default/files/2020-10/Fact_sheet-JobKeeper_Payment_extension_0.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210211070818/https://treasury.gov.au/coronavirus/jobkeeper/extension     According to the Australian Government Treasury website, Coronavirus Supplement : The Australian Government has a range of income support payments for eligible residents.  In response to COVID-19 the Australian Government introduced an additional and automatic Coronavirus Supplement for those already receiving JobSeeker Payment, Youth Allowance, Parenting Payment (Partnered or Single), Austudy, ABSTUDY (Living Allowance), Farm Household Allowance, Special Benefit or Department of Veteran's Affairs Education Scheme, or if an eligible New Enterprise Incentive Scheme participant. From 1 January 2021, the Coronavirus Supplement will be $150 per fortnight until 31 March 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211073457/https://treasury.gov.au/coronavirus/households/increased-income-support     According to the Australian Government Treasury website, Extra Payments for Pensioners: "Two separate $750 payments have made to households and two more payments of $250 each will be made recognising the ongoing impact of the Coronavirus pandemic." The last payment is scheduled for  26 February 2021.  Further details at the link:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211074135/https://treasury.gov.au/coronavirus/households/extra-payments-pensioners       According to the Australian Bureau of Statistics, the Full-time adult average weekly earnings  was $1,713.90 for the May 2020 reference period.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211073835/https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/labour/earnings-and-work-hours/average-weekly-earnings-australia/latest-release

column15: 15th Feb 2021 changed from T3 to T1 as the 15th February is the first school day impacted by this policy change.     12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide,  lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021.  Lock-down regulations for the Education and childcare sector are: Early learning centres, childcare, family day care: Open. School: Schools closed but open for vulnerable children and the children of essential workers. Higher education and training: Closed, remote learning only.  As schools and Higher education and training are closed (with the exception of vulnerable children or children of essential workers) changed code to 3T.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053031/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/210212%20-%20Table%20of%20restrictions.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053116/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-premier-85
column24: 12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide,  lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021.  Lock-down regulations include that : If you can work from home, you must work from home.  Essential workers and workplaces may remain open and are defined in information provided at the PDF link.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053031/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/210212%20-%20Table%20of%20restrictions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053116/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-premier-85
column33: 12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide,  lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021.  Lock-down regulations include that Public gatherings are Not permitted (exception is two people for exercise, or members of your household).   An exception has been made for "professional athletes are defined as essential workers, venues hosting professional sporting competitions can open, with essential staff attending to ensure the safe running of the event. No spectators." On face value, this appears to have been designed to allow the Australian Open tennis competition to continue (with no spectators).   Funerals (indoor and outdoor) may go ahead with no more than 10 people (and those necessary to conduct the funeral).  Weddings are not permitted unless end of life or other exceptional reasons. Involves only 5 persons  (including the two persons being married, celebrant, two witnesses)    https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053031/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/210212%20-%20Table%20of%20restrictions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053116/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-premier-85
column42: 12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide,  lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021.  Lock-down regulations include that Private gatherings are not permitted (i.e. no visitors allowed to a household). However, intimate partner visits are allowed.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053031/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/210212%20-%20Table%20of%20restrictions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053116/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-premier-85
column60: ***NOTE recode from a 3T to a 2T following correspondence from the Oxford The Blavatnik School of Government which concluded that restrictions in place are not sufficiently stringent to be coded a 3.     12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide,  lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021.  Lock-down regulations include that residents must "Stay at home unless:  • Shopping for necessary goods and services (one person per household, once per day, a support person can accompany  if required)  • Caregiving or compassionate reasons  • Essential work or permitted education  • Exercise (can leave for two hours of exercise)  • Other specified reasons (specific exemptions apply)"    There is also an Intrastate travel: travel limit of 5km from place of primary residence.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053031/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/210212%20-%20Table%20of%20restrictions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053116/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-premier-85
column69: Each Australian State and Territory has different boarder travel restrictions in place.  Below are changes implement on 12th Feb 2021.    VIC: 12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide, lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021. Consequently, there is an Intrastate travel limit of 5km from place of primary residence.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053031/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/210212%20-%20Table%20of%20restrictions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210212053116/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-premier-85    ACT: The ACT COVID-website was updated in 12th Feb 2021 to include an alert “Travel restrictions for Victoria will be in effect from 11.59pm Friday 12 February. More information coming soon.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210201110716/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/community/travel     WA: One the 12th Feb 2021, In a media statement, WA Premier Mark McGowan, announced that Victoria be reclassified as 'medium risk' (previously ‘low risk’) for a short period, until the situation is controlled. Beginning tonight, from 6pm, the 'medium risk' category will come into force until at least 12.01am, Tuesday, February 16, pending updated health advice.  The 72 hour hard border with Victoria means only exempt travellers are permitted.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210212060056/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/02/Return-to-pre-lockdown-life-from-12-01am-Sunday-February-14.aspx   NT:   The Northern Territory Government COVID-19 website was updated on 12th Feb to include 31 more Melbourne LGAs as declared hotspots from 10:45am 12th Feb 2021 – current.  Details of these LGAs can be found at the link.  Mandatory quarantine is required for people entering the NT who have visited a declared hotspot.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210211061915/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19
column79: Currently, as at the 12th Feb 2021, each state and territory has somewhat different household level debt and contact relief COVID-19 Reponses in place.  However, the only type of COVID-19 related household relief that appears to apply across all jurisdictions is in relation to residential rental payments and/or contracts.     ACT:   According to the ACT government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021: The annual increase will not be applied to 2020-2021 annual rates bills, The ACT Revenue Office will implement a new process for rates deferrals for owner occupiers experiencing hardship because of COVID-19. No interest will apply to the deferred amount, The Government is providing land tax and rates rebates to landlords of residential properties who reduce rents by at least 25 per cent for tenants impacted by COVID-19, The ACT Government is offering stamp duty concessions in the ACT for some home buyers planning to live in their new property, All 2020-21 vehicle registration, parking and public transport fee increases at the current levels., Households already receiving the Utilities Concession will receive an additional $200 rebate through their electricity bill, The government will freeze the Utilities Network Facilities Tax (UNFT) at the current  level.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210131103022/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/business-and-work/economic-survival-package/families-and-households    NSW:  According to the ACT government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021: measures to restrict when landlords can evict tenants due to rental arrears as a result of COVID-19;   https://web.archive.org/web/20210108104616/https://www.fairtrading.nsw.gov.au/resource-library/publications/coronavirus-covid-19/property/moratorium   https://web.archive.org/web/20210212033050/https://www.nsw.gov.au/life-events/living-nsw/renters-financially-affected-by-coronavirus    QLD:   According to the QLD government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021:   There remain some temporary regulatory measures to 30 April 2021 means Queensland remains ready to respond to COVID-19 risks as the transition back to normal residential tenancy arrangements continues.  These are: provisions allowing tenants experiencing domestic and family violence to end their tenancies quickly; protections for tenants against being listed in a tenancy database for rent arrears caused by COVID-19 impacts; limits on reletting costs for eligible tenants who end their fixed term tenancies early; short term tenancy statement extensions for moveable dwellings; entry restrictions and requirements to support COVID-19 social distancing measures; relaxed repair and maintenance obligations.    https://web.archive.org/web/20201125024414/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/the-hub/covid-19-tenancy-update     VIC:   According to The Victorian Government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021, COVID-19 changes to renting laws (implemented 29 March 2020 and still in effect) mean that tenants in financial hardship cannot be given a notice to vacate; landlords must try to negotiated a temporary rent reduction agreement where it is needed by tenants because of the impacts of COVID-19. Tenants can apply for a rent relief grant of up to $3,000.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210212051024/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/renting-laws-and-support-during-coronavirus-covid-19-pandemic     NT:  According to The Northern Territory Consumer Affairs website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021 “On 24 April 2020, the Northern Territory Parliament passed the Tenancies Legislation Amendment Act 2020. This provides the framework for the Territory’s response to tenancy issues arising as a result of the COVID-19 emergency.” These modifications: Extending notice timeframes for certain pre termination actions under the Act; establish New processes for termination of a tenancy; and provide for modified entry and inspection processes.   https://web.archive.org/web/20200930194941/https://nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0010/818308/s28.pdf     WA: According to The Western Australian Government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021” “In response to COVID-19, the Western Australian Government implemented a moratorium on some evictions and all rent increases, under the Residential Tenancies (COVID-19 Response) Act 2020. The extended emergency period ends on 28 March 2021, which means that all the existing laws will stay in place until this date, after which the ordinary tenancy laws will apply again.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210201105910/https://www.commerce.wa.gov.au/consumer-protection/residential-tenancies-covid-19-response   SA:  According to SA Government COVID-19 website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021 “State Parliament has endorsed a number of initiatives aimed at helping landlords and tenants whose incomes have been affected by COVID-19.” This includes “Ensuring tenants cannot be evicted by landlords due to an inability to paid rent because of COVID-19 employment loss”.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210212043922/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/school-and-community/housing-and-in-the-community     TA:   According to The Northern Territory Consumer Affairs website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021.  “Some of the changes to the Residential Tenancy Act 1997 including changes to rental evictions, rental increases, and general repairs and maintenance expired on 31 January 2021.” However, The Tasmanian Government is providing rent relief for residential households who rent homes in the private market and who are experiencing housing stress because of the impact of the coronavirus (COVID-19).  The Support Funds are available until 31 March 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210212044429/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/rental-housing
column103: 12th Feb 2021: In a tightening of restrictions the Premier of Victoria announced a 5 day, Victoria Wide,  lock-down from 11:59pm Friday 12th February 2021 until 11:59pm on Wednesday, 17 February 2021. column69: WA: A press release on the Western Australian Government COVID-19 website states that the "NSW will move from ‘low risk’ to ‘very low risk’ at 12.01am, on Tuesday, 16 February, pending no further outbreaks.  Under the ‘very low risk’ category, safe travel is permitted into WA subject to the following updated conditions:  completion of a G2G Pass declaration, stipulating they do not have any COVID-19 symptoms and which jurisdictions the traveller has been in over the previous 14 days;  all Perth Airport arrivals to undergo a health screening and temperature test;  travellers to be prepared to take a COVID-19 test at the airport COVID clinic, if deemed necessary by a health clinician (voluntary asymptomatic testing also available); and  land arrivals to be met at the border checkpoint, for a health screening and to have their G2G Pass declaration checked.  "  https://web.archive.org/web/20210222073452/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/return-pre-lockdown-life-1201am-sunday-14-february

column15: A 17 February 2021 media release from the Victorian Government published on the Victorian State Government Health and Human Service website announced that "Restrictions under Victoria’s new circuit-breaker actions will end tonight. From 11:59pm 17 February 2021 Victoria returns to COVIDSafe Summer restrictions – with some revised conditions.... There will no longer be four reasons to leave home and the five-kilometre restriction will no longer apply. Schools, restaurants and retails can reopen and both public and private office workers can return up to 50 per cent on site."  Schools across VIC have reopened.  In VIC Students age 12 yrs and older must where a mask while indoors at school and it is strongly recommended outside. Staff are required to practice 1.5m distancing with other adults where ever possible.  Density limits of one person per two square metres apply to staff areas such as staff lunchrooms and areas accessed by the public, such as reception areas.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210220060525/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202102/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20(Victoria)%20(No%2015)%2017%20February%202021%20(signed).pdf    https://web.archive.org/web/20210219220907/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-17-february-2021    https://web.archive.org/web/20210222013328if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/physical-distancing-health-and-hygiene    https://web.archive.org/web/20210222013320/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/face-masks-study-and-school
column24: WA: In a further easing of restrictions Western Australian Premier Mark McGowan announced that "The Perth and Peel restrictions will end as planned at 12.01am, Sunday, February 14.This means the whole of Western Australia will return to the pre-lockdown conditions, as experienced prior to 6pm on Sunday, January 31....Western Australians should continue to practise physical distancing where possible, maintain good personal hygiene at all times and get tested when unwell. It is vital that all Western Australians ensure they are registering their contact details, at businesses and venues where contact registers are mandatory, to assist contact tracing if it is required. As was the case before the lockdown, public or private events involving more than 500 patrons that require local government authority approval will need an approved COVID Event Plan as part of that process." All businesses are allowed to reopen though some COVIDsafe measures remain in place.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210222020726/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do  http://web.archive.org/web/20210222021353/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/02/Return-to-pre-lockdown-life-from-12-01am-Sunday-February-14.aspx      VIC: In an easy of restrictions, a 17 February 2021 media release from the Victorian Government published on the Victorian State Government Health and Human Service website announced that "Restrictions under Victoria’s new circuit-breaker actions will end tonight. There will no longer be four reasons to leave home and the five-kilometre restriction will no longer apply. Schools, restaurants and retails can reopen and both public and private office workers can return up to 50 per cent on site." column119: Priority list updated 18 February 2021    Phase 1a   Quarantine, border and front line health care workers will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility  Quarantine and border workers, including-->    staff at entry points to the country (such as sea ports and land borders)  staff working in quarantine facilities, including those employed under Commonwealth, state or private agreements, and    Commonwealth employees (including Defence personnel) who are identified as having the potential to encounter returning travellers as part of their work.     Frontline health care worker sub-groups for prioritisation-->    frontline staff in facilities or services such as hospital emergency departments,COVID-19 and respiratory wards, Intensive Care Units and High-dependency Units  laboratory staff handling potentially infectious material  ambulance and paramedics service  GP respiratory clinics, and    COVID-19 testing facilities.    *All other healthcare workers are included in Phase 1b, including medical and tertiary students with placements in these healthcare settings.       Aged care and disability care staff-->    people who provide weekly informal care to a person with a disability in a residential care setting  people classified as an essential care person in a residential aged care setting     Aged care and disability care residents-->         Phase 1b   Elderly adults aged 80 years and over  Elderly adults aged 70-79 years  Other health care workers  Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people > 55  Adults with an underlying medical condition, including those with a disability       *People will need to provide proof of these conditions to demonstrate their eligibility for vaccination via My health record, a health professional referral if required or a declaration form.       Critical and high risk workers including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing       * Workers will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility.         Phase 2a   Adults aged 60-69 years  Adults aged 50-59 years  Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people 18-54  Other critical and high risk workers       Phase 2b   Balance of adult population  Catch up any unvaccinated Australians from previous phases    Phase 3  < 16 if recommended*	     https://web.archive.org/web/20210218040646/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

column129: COVID-19 vaccines will be voluntary and free  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia. column69: Each Australian State and Territory has different boarder travel restrictions in place.  Below are the current restrictions as of 26th Feb 2021.  Note has been made of Australian State/Territories that have restricted travel from Auckland New Zealand.  However, code provided is based on restrictions on movement internal to Australia only.     VIC:   Victoria has no current restrictions on movement within the state nor across borders with other Australian jurisdictions.  Victoria has declared that “From 11.59pm Wednesday 24 February all flights arriving from Auckland [New Zealand] into Victoria will be regarded as ‘red zone’ arrivals and anyone arriving will be required to enter mandatory hotel quarantine for 14 days.??   Those who arrived in Melbourne from Auckland yesterday, Tuesday 23 February, are asked to get a COVID-19 test immediately and quarantine until they receive a negative test result.??”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226090601/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/travel-updates   https://web.archive.org/web/20210226090552/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covid-19-daily-update    ACT:   ACT has no current restrictions on movement within the state nor across borders with other Australian jurisdictions.  However, from 25 February 2021, travellers who have arrived into Australia and have been in Auckland in the 14 days prior to their arrival (excluding transits via Auckland Airport), must not travel to the ACT.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226093352/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/community/travel/overseas     NSW:  NSW has no current restrictions on movement within the state nor across borders with other Australian jurisdictions. The NSW Govt has declared Auckland New New Zealand a hot spot.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226091152/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/border-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226091206/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226091415/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/what-you-can-and-cant-do-under-rules/quarantine    WA:   WA continues to declare VIC an area of ‘low risk’.  Travellers from ‘low risk’ areas “If you have suitable premises, enter self-quarantine for 14 days. If not, you will be directed to enter a Government-approved quarantine facility at your own expense for 14 days. You must present for a COVID-19 test on Day 11 if still in WA.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226091951/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-interstate-border   Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  providing essential services and supplies  going to work or school there  entering for family or cultural reasons  delivering a community program or activity  entering in an emergency.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226091842/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/publications/remote-aboriginal-communities-directions-no-3  https://web.archive.org/web/20201002224654/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2020-08/Remote%20Aboriginal%20Communities%20Directions%20%28No.%203%29.pdf    NT:   The NT has no active Australian declared hotspots.  Therefore the NT has no current restrictions on movement within the state nor across borders with other Australian jurisdictions.  The NT has declared specific locations within Auckland to be hotspots.  “In accordance with direction 35 and with reference to direction 35A of the Directions for Territory Border Restrictions (No.49) (consolidated version PDF (188.5 KB), the CHO requires any person who has arrived in the Northern Territory from New Zealand to be tested for COVID 19 if:   They arrived between 19 February 2021 and 5:59pm on 25 February 2021; and  They were in any of the locations of interest declared by the NZ Ministry of Health listed below on or about the relevant times and dates listed below.  A person who is required to be tested must travel directly to a suitable place of quarantine and remain in that place, except to submit to testing for COVID-19 (or in an emergency). Once tested, the person must remain isolated in the suitable place of quarantine until notified of the results of the test.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226092118/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19     SA:   In an easing of restrictions the SA govt covid-19 website states that   “In effect from 12:01 am 26 February 2021:  Travel from Victoria and Greater Melbourne is permitted.    Travellers who arrived from Greater Melbourne and are subject to quarantine – if they have been to a high risk site are to remain segregated and isolated and contact SA Health regarding self-quarantine and COVID-19 testing requirements.  Travellers who arrived from Greater Melbourne and were not in a high risk site, can leave self-quarantine but must complete their day 1, 5 and 12 COVID-19 testing. However, if a person needs to attend a high risk setting (health, disability, aged care or correctional facility), they need to let the facility know they have been in High Community Transmission Zone in the past 14 days and will need to wear Personal Protective Equipment.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226092624/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/restrictions-and-responsibilities/travel-restrictions     QLD:  The QLD Govt website states that: “From 1am AEST Saturday 13 February 2021 specific Victorian LGAs are declared as COVID-19 hotspots. Border restrictions apply to anyone who have been in a hotspot in the previous 14 days or since the start date identified for the hotspot (whichever is shorter). For these Victorian hotspots, the identified start date is 7 February 2021. This date is when the risk of COVID-19 transmission started.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226093034/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19     TAS:   TAS has no current restrictions on movement within the state nor across borders with other Australian jurisdictions.  Tasmania has declared the Auckland region, New Zealand to be high risk.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210226034224/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226093308/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert

column79: The below note made on the 12th Feb remains up to date as of 26th Feb 2021.  Have copied the note from 12th Feb here with updated links.      Currently, as at the 12th Feb 2021, each state and territory has somewhat different household level debt and contact relief COVID-19 Reponses in place.  However, the only type of COVID-19 related household relief that appears to apply across all jurisdictions is in relation to residential rental payments and/or contracts.     ACT:   According to the ACT government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021: The annual increase will not be applied to 2020-2021 annual rates bills, The ACT Revenue Office will implement a new process for rates deferrals for owner occupiers experiencing hardship because of COVID-19. No interest will apply to the deferred amount, The Government is providing land tax and rates rebates to landlords of residential properties who reduce rents by at least 25 per cent for tenants impacted by COVID-19, The ACT Government is offering stamp duty concessions in the ACT for some home buyers planning to live in their new property, All 2020-21 vehicle registration, parking and public transport fee increases at the current levels., Households already receiving the Utilities Concession will receive an additional $200 rebate through their electricity bill, The government will freeze the Utilities Network Facilities Tax (UNFT) at the current  level.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210212032226/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/business-and-work/economic-survival-package/families-and-households    NSW:  According to the ACT government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021: measures to restrict when landlords can evict tenants due to rental arrears as a result of COVID-19;   https://web.archive.org/web/20210108104616/https://www.fairtrading.nsw.gov.au/resource-library/publications/coronavirus-covid-19/property/moratorium   https://web.archive.org/web/20210226073716/https://www.nsw.gov.au/life-events/living-nsw/renters-financially-affected-by-coronavirus    QLD:   According to the QLD government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021:   There remain some temporary regulatory measures to 30 April 2021 means Queensland remains ready to respond to COVID-19 risks as the transition back to normal residential tenancy arrangements continues.  These are: provisions allowing tenants experiencing domestic and family violence to end their tenancies quickly; protections for tenants against being listed in a tenancy database for rent arrears caused by COVID-19 impacts; limits on reletting costs for eligible tenants who end their fixed term tenancies early; short term tenancy statement extensions for moveable dwellings; entry restrictions and requirements to support COVID-19 social distancing measures; relaxed repair and maintenance obligations.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210212040959/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/the-hub/covid-19-tenancy-update    VIC:   According to The Victorian Government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021, COVID-19 changes to renting laws (implemented 29 March 2020 and still in effect) mean that tenants in financial hardship cannot be given a notice to vacate; landlords must try to negotiated a temporary rent reduction agreement where it is needed by tenants because of the impacts of COVID-19. Tenants can apply for a rent relief grant of up to $3,000.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210226073918/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/renting-laws-and-support-during-coronavirus-covid-19-pandemic    NT:  According to The Northern Territory Consumer Affairs website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021 “On 24 April 2020, the Northern Territory Parliament passed the Tenancies Legislation Amendment Act 2020. This provides the framework for the Territory’s response to tenancy issues arising as a result of the COVID-19 emergency.” These modifications: Extending notice timeframes for certain pre termination actions under the Act; establish New processes for termination of a tenancy; and provide for modified entry and inspection processes.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210226074036/https://consumeraffairs.nt.gov.au/rental-changes-due-to-covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226074039/https://nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0010/818308/s28.pdf    WA: According to The Western Australian Government website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021” “In response to COVID-19, the Western Australian Government implemented a moratorium on some evictions and all rent increases, under the Residential Tenancies (COVID-19 Response) Act 2020. The extended emergency period ends on 28 March 2021, which means that all the existing laws will stay in place until this date, after which the ordinary tenancy laws will apply again.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210226074155/https://www.commerce.wa.gov.au/consumer-protection/residential-tenancies-covid-19-response  SA:  According to SA Government COVID-19 website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021 “State Parliament has endorsed a number of initiatives aimed at helping landlords and tenants whose incomes have been affected by COVID-19.” This includes “Ensuring tenants cannot be evicted by landlords due to an inability to paid rent because of COVID-19 employment loss”.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210226074235/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/school-and-community/housing-and-in-the-community    TA:   According to The Northern Territory Consumer Affairs website, and current as at 12th Feb 2021.  “Some of the changes to the Residential Tenancy Act 1997 including changes to rental evictions, rental increases, and general repairs and maintenance expired on 31 January 2021.” However, The Tasmanian Government is providing rent relief for residential households who rent homes in the private market and who are experiencing housing stress because of the impact of the coronavirus (COVID-19).  The Support Funds are available until 31 March 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210226074304/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/rental-housing

column88: Current as of 26th February 2021: There remains some variation between states and territories as to who is eligible to receive a COVID-19 test.  However, eligibility criteria do apply across all states and territories.  To receive a test a person must be experiencing symptoms and/or meet additional criteria (for example, have visited a specific location identified by public health officials as a ‘hot spot’ or have been in contact with a positive case).  If eligibility criteria are met, testing is provided free of charge.  Below are updated archived links documenting the specific policy of each state and territory.     NSW  "If you have a fever (37.5° or higher), cough, sore or scratchy throat, shortness of breath, runny nose, or loss of taste or smell, you should get tested for COVID-19, even if your symptoms are mild.   It is important to maintain high testing rates to identify as many cases in the community as quickly as possible. Other reported symptoms of COVID-19 include fatigue (tiredness), acute blocked nose (congestion), muscle pain, joint pain, headache, diarrhoea, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, unexplained chest pain and conjunctivitis.  You should get tested even if you only have one of the symptoms.  As well as people who have COVID-19 symptoms, there are other people who should get tested, including close contacts of confirmed COVID-19 cases and travellers arriving from overseas.  Healthcare workers, disability and aged care workers, people at increased risk of severe disease and school community workers should be particularly careful in monitoring for symptoms and get tested if they appear."  https://web.archive.org/web/20210217013530/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/how-to-protect-yourself-and-others/symptoms-and-testing#getting-tested    ACT  "Test all patients who meet the suspect case definition:    Fever (=37.5°C) or history of fever (e.g. night sweats, chills) OR acute respiratory infection (e.g. cough, shortness of breath, sore throat) OR loss of sense of smell or taste    AND    One of the following epidemiological risk factors in the 14 days prior to illness onset:    Close contact with a confirmed COVID-19 case; OR  International travel, including cruise ship passengers and crew; in the 14 days prior to illness onset; OR  Travel to a geographical area of risk within Australia; OR  Healthcare, aged or residential care workers and staff with direct patient contact.  In addition to the suspect case definition, test anyone who meets the following clinical criteria:    Fever (=37.5°C) or history of fever (e.g. night sweat, chills) in the absence of a clear alternative cause, such as a urinary tract infection or cellulitis  OR    Acute respiratory infection (e.g. shortness of breath, cough, sore throat).  OR    Sudden onset of loss of sense of smell or taste  Patients with sudden onset of runny nose/blocked nose, muscle pain, joint pain, diarrhoea, nausea, vomiting or loss of appetite can be tested for COVID-19 in the absence of fever or respiratory symptoms if no alternative cause for these symptoms is apparent, at the discretion of the treating clinician.    Testing of asymptomatic individuals is not generally recommended. However, some individuals may be asked by ACT Health (or another public health authority such as NSW Health) to be tested even if asymptomatic."  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226080625/https://www.health.act.gov.au/health-professionals/chief-health-officer-alerts/covid-19-testing-information      VIC  "If you have any of the symptoms of coronavirus (COVID-19), however mild, you should seek advice and get tested.    People with no symptoms can also be tested for coronavirus (COVID-19) in very specific circumstances:    if they are a close contact exposed to someone known to have coronavirus (COVID-19)  as part of an outbreak investigation and response by the Department of Health and Human Services  if they are a returned international traveller  If they have visited a high risk interstate location  if they are due to have surgery or a planned hospital stay  if they are part of a targeted testing program across an industry or group."  https://web.archive.org/web/20210226080824/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/taking-test-covid-19    TAS  It is important to get tested if you have or have had any of the following symptoms in the past 7 days, even mild:  fever (or signs of fever, including chills or night sweats)  runny nose  cough  sore/itchy throat  shortness of breath  loss of taste or smell.    Testing is also encouraged for:  People at higher risk of exposure with atypical symptoms, such as health care workers and residential aged care facility workers, transport and logistic essential travellers  Symptomatic household members of healthcare and aged care workers    https://web.archive.org/web/20210226081024/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/keeping-yourself-safe/testing-for-covid19  SA  People who have any of the symptoms below, even if mild, should get tested for COVID-19 as soon as symptoms appear:  fever or chills  cough  sore throat  runny nose  shortness of breath  loss of taste or smell  diarrhoea and vomiting     https://web.archive.org/web/20210226081137/https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/conditions/infectious+diseases/covid-19/testing+and+tracing/testing+for+covid-19    WA  Get tested for COVID-19 early – people are most infectious when they first experience symptoms.    COVID-19 can cause a range of symptoms. These can range from mild illness to pneumonia. If you experience any of the below symptoms, no matter how mild, please get a COVID-19 test.    What are the symptoms and when should I get tested?  Get tested if you have any of the following, no matter how mild:    fever of 37.5°C or above OR fever in the last few days (for example, night sweats or chills)  coughing, shortness of breath, sore throat, runny nose  loss of smell or taste.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210209064215/https://www.healthywa.wa.gov.au/Articles/A_E/Coronavirus/COVID19-testing    NT  In the Territory, anyone with the following symptoms can access testing for coronavirus (COVID-19):    a recent, acute respiratory infection (e.g. cough, shortness of breath, sore throat)  a fever (37.5°C or higher) or history of fever (e.g. night sweats, chills)  loss of smell or loss of taste.  If you are not displaying these symptoms then you don't need to be tested for coronavirus.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210223183644/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/symptoms-testing
