column69: In a Press Release on Friday, 29 March 2021, Western Australian Premier Mark McGowan announces that   "Following updated advice from the WA Chief Health Officer this morning, Queensland will transition from a 'low risk' State to a 'medium risk' State under WA's controlled interstate border regime, from 12.01am tomorrow (March 30, 2021). column15: Queensland: A joint statement by the Premier and Minister for Trade Annastacia Palaszczuk and Minister for Health and Ambulance Services Yvette D'Ath published Thursday, 01 April, 2021 at 01:26 PM states that "Greater Brisbane’s lockdown ended at 12 noon today....Importantly, a number of restrictions will be put in place for all Queenslanders to protect against further transmission of COVID-19 for the next two weeks."    Schools will not return as previously scheduled school holidays commence Friday 2nd April and will return for the next school term on Monday 19 April.       Restrictions for Queensland (No. 2) is effective from Effective from: 12.00pm AEST 1 April 2021 and revokes the  previous Restrictions for Queensland Direction made on 29 March 2021. This direction stipulates that "Universities and other higher educational institutions such as TAFEs and RTOs must comply with occupant density in large lecture settings.  Note – large lecture settings may operate for one person per 2 square metres or 100 per cent of seated venue capacity with ticketed and allocated seating in accordance with the definition of occupant density in Schedule 2.  The requirement to collect contact information only applies to large lecture settings operating for 100 per cent of seated venue capacity with ticketed and allocated seating.  Note – smaller education and teaching sessions, such as group seminars, tutorials, practicals and laboratory-based learnings, and vocational training sessions are not subject to occupant density. Physical distancing should be observed to the extent possible.  Businesses, activities and undertakings such as cafes or sporting-based activities conducted at universities or other educational institutions must comply with the applicable requirements under this Direction."     Victoria: Victoria:  The Workplace Directions (No 23) came into force at 6:00pm on 26 March 2021 and replace the Workplace Directions (No 22).  They remain current as at 4th April 2021.   “In any shared spaces (such as lunchrooms) and publicly accessible areas at the Work Premises (except in relation to any shared spaces in schools, nonschool senior secondary providers, childcare or early childhood services, or higher education services used by students or children, including classrooms, and clinical areas of a hospital), an employer must comply with the density quotient for each shared space and each publicly accessible area”    The density quotient is one person for every 2 square meters.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210404073238/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/victorias-restriction-levels-covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20210404073211/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202103/Workplace%20%28AIO%29%20Directions%20%28No%2020%29%20-%2026%20March%202021.pdf
column24: New South Wales: The Public Health (COVID-19 Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 commenced on 29 March 2021 and contains directions on gatherings, and the number of persons allowed on non-residential and residential premise.  This direction was a relaxation of COVID-19 restrictions for NSW.  In part, it now allows for nightclubs to operate. There are no restrictions on dancing, including at weddings and in pubs and nightclubs.  NSW  no longer requires any workplace to be closed.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210404064140/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx  https://web.archive.org/web/20210329012105/https://gazette.legislation.nsw.gov.au/so/download.w3p?id=Gazette_2021_2021-127.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210404064320/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/what-you-can-do-nsw      Queensland:  Queensland: A joint statement by the Premier and Minister for Trade Annastacia Palaszczuk and Minister for Health and Ambulance Services Yvette D'Ath published Thursday, 01 April, 2021 at 01:26 PM states that "Greater Brisbane’s lockdown ended at 12 noon today....Importantly, a number of restrictions will be put in place for all Queenslanders to protect against further transmission of COVID-19 for the next two weeks."      Restrictions for Queensland (No. 2) direction is effective from Effective from: 12.00pm AEST 1 April 2021 and revokes the  previous Restrictions for Queensland Direction made on 29 March 2021.   This direction stipulates mandatory density limits ( one person per either 2 or 4 square meters) and mandatory collection of contract tracing information for a large range of workplaces including: Food and drink; Entertainment venues (including night clubs); Retail; Beauty and personal care services; Leisure and recreation; Residential facilities; Outdoor recreation; Non-residential institutions; Professional and elite sport; Adult entertainment venues and the sex industry.    However, no workplaces are directed to close.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210404070838/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-for-queensland   https://web.archive.org/web/20210404071525/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91833

This remains up to date as of 9th April.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210409031619/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-for-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20210404071525/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91833
column33: Queensland: A joint statement by the Premier and Minister for Trade Annastacia Palaszczuk and Minister for Health and Ambulance Services Yvette D'Ath published Thursday, 01 April, 2021 at 01:26 PM states that "Greater Brisbane’s lockdown ended at 12 noon today....Importantly, a number of restrictions will be put in place for all Queenslanders to protect against further transmission of COVID-19 for the next two weeks."      Restrictions for Queensland (No. 2) is effective from Effective from: 12.00pm AEST 1 April 2021 and revokes the  previous Restrictions for Queensland Direction made on 29 March 2021.  In part, this direction restricts public outdoor gatherings to a a maximum of 500 people.  Organised Indoor and outdoor events (Example – marathons, cultural festivals, fetes, expos) may operate with greater numbers of attendees.  However, COVID Safe Event Checklist or COVID Safe Event Plan and occupant density limitations apply.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210409031619/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-for-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20210404071525/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91833  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409033312/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/restrictions-for-queensland
column42: Queensland: A joint statement by the Premier and Minister for Trade Annastacia Palaszczuk and Minister for Health and Ambulance Services Yvette D'Ath published Thursday, 01 April, 2021 at 01:26 PM states that "Greater Brisbane’s lockdown ended at 12 noon today....Importantly, a number of restrictions will be put in place for all Queenslanders to protect against further transmission of COVID-19 for the next two weeks."      Restrictions for Queensland (No. 2) is effective from Effective from: 12.00pm AEST 1 April 2021 and revokes the  previous Restrictions for Queensland Direction made on 29 March 2021.  In part, this direction provides for a relaxing of restrictions for private gatherings in Queensland. It allows for "up to 30 people to gather at a residence, inclusive of the members of a person’s household."  It also allows for "A maximum of 500 people can gather publicly in an outdoor space in Queensland."    This information is up to date as of 9th April 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210409031619/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-for-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20210404071525/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91833
column51: Each Australian State and Territory has some level of additional policies or recommendations for the use of public transport.   Below is the current policy/advise of each jurisdiction as on the 9th April 2021.     New South Wales:   The NSW government NSW transport website states that “From December 14 2020, COVID Safe capacity on trains has been lifted to 55 per cent, capacity on buses lifted to 45 percent, capacity on light rail lifted to 25 per cent and capacity on ferries lifted to 51 per cent.” In conversation with a NSW Transport representative on 10th April 2021 via the 131 500 information line, I was advised that this information remains up to date.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210328222751/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-what-you-can-do  https://web.archive.org/web/20210410021757/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance     Victoria:   The Victorian Government Public Transport Victoria website states that face masks are compulsory on public transport in Victoria.  There is also a recommendation and financial incentive for commuters to travel outside peak hours.  “Travel outside peak times when there are less people on the network. On weekdays get a 30% off peak discount on metropolitan services or travel free before 7.15am on metropolitan trains.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210410023015/https://www.ptv.vic.gov.au/more/coronavirus-covid-19/   Queensland:   The Queensland Government Public Transport website states encourages travellers to physically distance while travelling and to travel at of peak times.  The QLD Government have developed a ‘Service capacity tracker’ that allows commuters to see where there is enough space on their preferred service to maintain physical distancing.  Additional peak hour train and bus services, in response to COVID-19 continue.  Masks are currently mandatory on public transport through-out QLD.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210410024119/https://translink.com.au/updates/14696   https://web.archive.org/web/20210319052936/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/help-and-advice/public-transport  https://web.archive.org/web/20210401222648/https://translink.com.au/travel-with-us/covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20210325183126/https://translink.com.au/travel-with-us/covid-19/capacity-tracker     Western Australia:   The WA Government Public Transport Authority Website states that “The PTA is closely monitoring what is happening with coronavirus (COVID-19) and is increasing the cleaning regime on all the vehicles in our Transperth, Transwa, TransRegional and School Bus Services road and rail fleets.”  No significant COVID-19 policies resulting in or significantly reduce volume/route/means of transport available.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210407044301/https://www.pta.wa.gov.au/     Tasmania:   The Tasmanian Government COVID-19 website states that “Passengers are encouraged to keep physical distancing in mind and choose a seat away from others where possible….anyone feeling unwell should avoid using public transport. For short trips, consider walking or cycling. Travel outside peak periods where possible. While waiting at bus stops, practice social distancing. Adults seeking to board a bus which has a high number of passengers are asked to consider waiting for the next service where available. This request does not apply to children.”  No significant COVID-19 policies resulting in or significantly reduce volume/route/means of transport available.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210403114036/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/transport     Northern Territory:   The Northern Territory government COVID-19 website states that “Public transport, including buses, taxis and rideshare services, continues to operate in the Northern Territory (NT). To minimise the risk of contracting or spreading coronavirus (COVID-19), passengers must practice good hygiene and physical distancing where possible.”  No significant COVID-19 policies resulting in or significantly reduce volume/route/means of transport available.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210410031847/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/public-transport       Australian Capital Territory:   The Australian Capital Territory Government Website site states that “Use of public transport during off peak times is encouraged where possible. This helps with physical distancing onboard and at stops and stations. Off peak travel is also cheaper…. Bus and light rail services continue to run to their regular schedule. Almost 700 more services have been introduced each weekday and more than 200 services each Saturday, to help you spread out your travel.”    You can check the quietest times to travel on light rail and Rapid bus services using the Transport Canberra online capacity tool..”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210410032002/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/community/transport   https://web.archive.org/web/20210410032112/https://www.transport.act.gov.au/getting-around/timetables/when-to-use-public-transport
column69: Travel restrictions vary by Australian States and Territories.  These are the restrictions in place for each jurisdiction on 9th April 2021.      New South Wales:   According to the NSW Government COVID19 website “There are currently no quarantine requirements for travellers who are already in Australia who are entering NSW from other states.”  However, “Some areas in Queensland are considered affected areas”   Effected areas are specific venues on specific days and times (and can be view at the archives link below).    The government website further states that “After arriving in NSW, or if you are already in NSW and have been to a close contact venue, at the time and date listed in the concerns notice you must  immediately travel to your place of residence or another suitable place  self-isolate for 14 days.”    https://web.archive.org/web/20210409034241/https://www.nsw.gov.au/quarantine/planning-your-trip  https://web.archive.org/web/20210407094946/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/border-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409034330/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409034446/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/interstate-hotspots    Victoria:   According to the Victorian Government COVID-19 website   “The Greater Brisbane area (City of Brisbane, City of Ipswich, Logan City, Moreton Bay Region, Redlands City) is designated an orange zone under Victoria’s travel permit system, effective from 6:00pm Victorian time on Thursday 1 April.”…” The Gladstone Region in Queensland and Byron Shire in NSW remain as orange zones under the travel permit system.”  “Those travelling from a designated orange zone must apply for a permit to enter Victoria. You cannot apply for a permit if you have COVID-19 symptoms, nor if you have visited a red zone. You are allowed to travel through a red zone for the purpose of transit. In order to enter Victoria, you must agree to the requirement of self-quarantine, get a COVID-19 test within 72 hours of arrival and remain in self-quarantine until you receive a negative test result.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409035021/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system   Queensland:   The QLD COVID-19 government website states that “You do not need a border pass to enter Queensland from any Australian state or territory.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409035306/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/border-closing     Western Australia:   The WA Government COVID-19 website states that “Effective 12.01am, Wednesday 7 April, travel from Queensland to WA is permitted, subject to 14 days of self-quarantine and COVID-19 testing.” This represents a moderate easing of restrictions.  Previously no traveller were permitted to enter WA from QLD.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409035635/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-interstate-border     Tasmania:   According to the Tasmanian Government COVID-19 website, there are some “high risk premises” identified within NSW and QLD.    “Travellers who have been at any of the high-risk premises listed at the specified times below are not permitted to enter Tasmania.”  LIf you are currently in Tasmania and have been at any of the Queensland or NSW premises on the dates and times identified below, you must self-isolate immediately and contact the Public Health Hotline. You will be required to have a COVID-19 test and quarantine for 14 days regardless of your test result”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409040101/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409040428/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert    Northern Territory:   The Northern Territory Government website states “There are no current active hotspots.”  Northern Territory hotspot declarations have implications for interstate travel.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409040610/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409040712/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19/former-declared-hotspots   Australian Capital Territory:   The ACT government Public Health (COVID-19 Areas of Concern) Notice 2021 (No 14) currently states that there are “No areas declared”.  Declared areas have implications for interstate travel.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409040752/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0009/1738773/COVID-19-Areas-of-Concern-notice_9-April-2021-No-14.pdf
column74: Up to date as of 9th April 2021 - Other than quarantine-free flights from New Zealand, Australia's boarders are closed to non-citizens (with very limited exception). Full details can be read at updated archived link below to the Australian Government Home Affairs website.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210410032643/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions
column86: The Australian Government continues to run a coordinated public health campaign.  This includes use of TV advertisements, Social media, formal government website information, and press conference and media statements.    Updated archived link to the Official Australian Government Public health information website for COVID-19:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210405093811/https://www.health.gov.au/   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409050019/https://www.health.gov.au/news/health-alerts/novel-coronavirus-2019-ncov-health-alert      Archived link to press statement on the 8th April 2021 (and associated media statement) by Australian Prime Minister, Minister for Health and Aged Care Scott Morrison.  In this statement Scott Morrison countenances a new recommendation "that it is preferred that the Pfizer vaccine be provided to adults under the age of 50.  The AstraZeneca vaccine should only be given as a first dose to adults under the age of 50 where the benefit clearly outweighs the risk for that individual." Previous policy was that AstraZeneca was safe for all Australians and the vaccine planned for roll-out to all Australians under 50 years later in 2021.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210409050130/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/press-conference-australian-parliament-house-act-8april21  https://web.archive.org/web/20210408110037/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/astrazeneca-covid-19-vaccine
column88: Each Australian State and Territory have their own COVID019 testing polices are responsible for implementing testing policy.  There are nuances between the testing policies of individual states and territories. For example in how they determine ‘hot spot’ locations.  However, for every state and territory, all residents are able to access free testing if they have any COVID-19 symptoms and/or if they have been in contact with a known or suspected positive case (or visited a declared ‘hot spot’). column103: Queensland: A joint statement by the Premier and Minister for Trade Annastacia Palaszczuk and Minister for Health and Ambulance Services Yvette D'Ath published Thursday, 01 April, 2021 at 01:26 PM states that "Greater Brisbane’s lockdown ended at 12 noon today....Importantly, a number of restrictions will be put in place for all Queenslanders to protect against further transmission of COVID-19 for the next two weeks."      Restrictions for Queensland (No. 2) is effective from Effective from: 12.00pm AEST 1 April 2021 and revokes the  previous Restrictions for Queensland Direction made on 29 March 2021.  In part, this direction maintains the previous mask wearing requirements.     "A person mentioned in paragraph 13 must:  carry a face mask at all times when outside their principal place of residence; and  wear a face mask covering the nose and mouth at all times if they:  are on public transport infrastructure or at public transport infrastructure; or  Example – when on a train or waiting at a train platform.  are in a commercial passenger vehicle or waiting in a designated outdoor space that is not a residence for a commercial passenger vehicle; or  Example – waiting for a taxi at a taxi rank, waiting for a rideshare at a designated pick-up area.  Note – a driver of a commercial passenger vehicle must wear a mask.  are in an indoor space; or  are in an outdoor space where physical distancing is not possible; or  are entering, exiting or inside a Major Sports Stadium, unless the person is seated; or  have any symptoms consistent with COVID-19; or  are required to do so in accordance with any other Public Health Directions in effect under section 362B of the Public Health Act 2005; or  Example – under the Mandatory Face Masks in Airports and on Aircraft Direction or its successors, a person must wear a face mask at all times while on a domestic commercial aircraft flying in Queensland airspace.  are directed to do so by an emergency officer (public health)"      https://web.archive.org/web/20210409031619/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/restrictions-for-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20210404071525/https://statements.qld.gov.au/statements/91833
column106: 5th May 2021 this code was changed from a 3G to a 4G.  This change was made in recognition that in addition to Key workers, Clinically vulnerable groups (non elderly), and Elderly groups - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults over 55 were also able to access the vaccine.      The Hon Greg Hunt MP Minister for Health and Aged Care announced in a media release on 22nd March 2021 that "Today is a momentous day for Australia’s fight against COVID-19, with the rollout of Phase 1B of the Australian Government’s vaccination plan." ...  "Priority groups in Phase 1B include:    elderly adults aged 80 years and over  elderly adults aged 70 years and over  health care workers not vaccinated in Phase 1A  Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults over 55  adults with a specified medical condition  adults with a severe disability who have a specified underlying medical condition  critical and high risk workers including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing."  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia.    Both the Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine and the Oxford/Astrazeneca vaccine are in use for phase 1B.  However, as documented directly below in the later part of this note.  The Australian Government announced on the 8th if April that Astrazeneca is now not the preferred vaccine for people under 50 years.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210326090223/https://www.health.gov.au/ministers/the-hon-greg-hunt-mp/media/phase-1b-covid-19-vaccination-rollout-begins   https://web.archive.org/web/20210326090708/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine   https://web.archive.org/web/20210326090821/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/which-covid-19-vaccine-will-i-receive     Australia remains at Phase 1B of the Vaccine role out.  However, the Australian Government announced yesterday 8th April that they would be reviewing the COVID-19 vaccination program rollout.  This review has been prompted by updated advice from the Australian Technical Advisory Group  about the AstraZeneca vaccine in response to new vaccine safety concerns.    "ATAGI recommends that the COVID-19 vaccine by Pfizer is preferred over COVID-19 Vaccine AstraZeneca in adults aged under 50 years. This recommendation is based on the increasing risk of severe outcomes from COVID-19 in older adults (and hence a higher benefit from vaccination) and a potentially increased risk of thrombosis with thrombocytopenia following AstraZeneca vaccine in those under 50 years. "    https://web.archive.org/web/20210409010631/https://www.health.gov.au/news/atagi-statement-on-astrazeneca-vaccine-in-response-to-new-vaccine-safety-concerns   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409054831/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine
column115: Queensland: The QLD Aged Care Direction (No. 26) came into effect 3pm AEST 8 April 2021 and replaces the Aged Care Direction (No. 25) made on 13 March 2021.    "A person may not visit a resident in a restricted residential aged care facility other than in accordance with paragraph 29(d) above. Note – personal visits are not permitted in a restricted residential aged care facility." 29(d) "the person's presence at the premises is for the purposes of end of life visit for a resident of the restricted residential aged care facility;"    Currently the whole of QLD has been declared restricted area and so all residential facilities are restricted facilities.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210409052026/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/aged-care   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409053822/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/queensland-covid-19-restricted-areas

column103: According to the Queensland Government COVID-19 website, From 6am AEST Thursday 15 April 2021 restrictions have eased across the state:    “Face mask wearing is only mandatory in certain settings.  You must wear your mask when at an airport or on a domestic or international flight departing or arriving in Queensland.  While not mandatory, you are strongly encouraged to:  carry a mask with you at all times when you leave home  wear a mask when travelling on public transport, in a taxi  or rideshare or in shopping centres  wear a mask in any indoor or outdoor space when physical distancing is not possible.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423051415/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/urgent-covid-19-update       Coded as a 2G because it remains compulsory to wear a mask in all Australian airports and on domestic commercial flights. Is this documented on the Australian Government Department of Infrastructure, Transport, Regional Development and Communications website and is up to date as of 23rd April 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423051641/https://www.infrastructure.gov.au/aviation/domestic-passenger-journey-protocol.aspx

column106: 5th May 2021 this code was changed from a 3G to a 4G.  This change was made in recognition that in addition to Key workers, Clinically vulnerable groups (non elderly), and Elderly groups - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults over 55 were also able to access the vaccine.    Press Conference given by Greg Hunt the Australian Minister for Health and Professor John Skerritt, head of the Therapeutic Goods Administration on the 13th April, provides an update on the vaccine rollout and confirms that the Australian government is still considering options for vaccinating those under 50 years of age.  This reconsideration was prompted by the announcement of the 8th April that Australian Technical Advisory Group recommends that the COVID-19 vaccine by Pfizer is preferred over COVID-19 Vaccine AstraZeneca in adults aged under 50 years. This recommendation is based on the increasing risk of severe outcomes from COVID-19 in older adults (and hence a higher benefit from vaccination) and a potentially increased risk of thrombosis with thrombocytopenia following AstraZeneca vaccine in those under 50 years.     Australia remains at vaccine rollout phases 1a and 1b.    Phase 1a includes: Quarantine and border workers, Frontline health care worker sub-groups for prioritisation (frontline staff in facilities or services such as hospital emergency departments,COVID-19 and respiratory wards, Intensive Care Units and High-dependency Units, laboratory staff handling potentially infectious material, ambulance and paramedics service, GP respiratory clinics, COVID-19 testing facilities), Aged care and disability care staff.   Pfizer vaccine only    Phase 1B includes: Elderly adults aged 80 years and over, Elderly adults aged 70-79 years, Other health care workers, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults 55 and over, Adults with an underlying medical condition or significant disability, Critical and high risk workers including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing.    Pfizer vaccine and the AstraZeneca (for those over 50yrs).     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423052444/https://www.health.gov.au/ministers/the-hon-greg-hunt-mp/media/press-conference-with-minister-hunt-and-professor-john-skerritt-about-covid-19-and-the-vaccine-rollout   https://web.archive.org/web/20210411055330/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

column115: Queensland: The QLD Aged Care Direction (No. 26) came into effect 3pm AEST 8 April 2021 and remain current as of the 19th April 2021.   This direction states, in part that :  "A person may not visit a resident in a restricted residential aged care facility other than in accordance with paragraph 29(d) above. Note – personal visits are not permitted in a restricted residential aged care facility." 29(d) "the person's presence at the premises is for the purposes of end of life visit for a resident of the restricted residential aged care facility;"    Queensland COVID-19 Restricted Areas (No. 14) came into effect on 6am AEST 15 April 2021.  This direction declared that there are no Local Government Areas listed as Queensland COVID-19 restricted areas.  However, prior to this 6am on 15th April, all of QLD has been declared restricted area and so all residential facilities had been restricted facilities.     http://web.archive.org/web/20210409053822/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/queensland-covid-19-restricted-areas  https://web.archive.org/web/20210409053822/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/queensland-covid-19-restricted-areas   http://web.archive.org/web/20210419142328/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/aged-care

column15: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.   The revoked direction previously stipulated that "Universities and other higher educational institutions such as TAFEs and RTOs must comply with occupant density in large lecture settings.  Note – large lecture settings may operate for one person per 2 square metres or 100 per cent of seated venue capacity with ticketed and allocated seating in accordance with the definition of occupant density in Schedule 2.  The requirement to collect contact information only applies to large lecture settings operating for 100 per cent of seated venue capacity with ticketed and allocated seating.  Note – smaller education and teaching sessions, such as group seminars, tutorials, practicals and laboratory-based learnings, and vocational training sessions are not subject to occupant density. Physical distancing should be observed to the extent possible.  Businesses, activities and undertakings such as cafes or sporting-based activities conducted at universities or other educational institutions must comply with the applicable requirements under this Direction."     From the 15th April 2021 and up to date as of 19th April 2021 there appears to be no COVID-19 related policy restrictions applying to schools or universities in QLD.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210419104437/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/revocation-of-restrictions-for-queensland-no.-2  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419104947/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-for-queensland-2  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419105036/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/roadmap-to-easing-queenslands-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419105153/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/movement-gathering-direction    Victoria:   In an easing of restrictions, The Workplace Directions (No 24) came into force at 11:59pm on 9 April 2021 and replace the Workplace Directions (No 23).  They remain current as at 19th April 2021.   “Office areas (including shared spaces that form part of an office area such as kitchens, lunchrooms and bathrooms) used only by staff that are not publicly accessible are [now] also not subject to the density quotient. However, the density quotient applies to any publicly accessible areas (including in relation to a school, non-school senior secondary providers, or childcare or early childhood service, or higher education service)”  Therefore except in limited circumstances where members of the public (ie not students or staff) are visiting a school in Victoria - density quotients no longer apply.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210419110159/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Workplace%20Directions%20%28No%2024%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf     New South Wales:   The NSW Public Health (COVID-19 Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 commenced at the beginning of 29 March 2021 and remains current as of the 19th April 2021.  It outlines current gathering restrictions for NSW which, for non-residential premises (with some exceptions are) “(a) the number of persons that is equivalent to 1 person per 2 square metres of space in the premises, or (b) 25 persons.”  However, the order further states that “Gathering at a school, university or other educational institution or child care facility that is necessary for the normal business of the school, university, institution or facility but does not include a school event that involves members of the community in addition to staff and students”  Except in limited circumstances where members of the public (ie not students or staff) are visiting a school, there are no COVID-19 related policies in NSW that currently impact the normal functions of schools or universities.   web.archive.org/web/20210419112216/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx   Unable to archive the URL to the full direction as the Way Back Machine repeatedly generated an “internal error”    Tasmania:   According to the Tasmanian government COVID-19 website and up to date as of the 19th April 2021 Schools, universities, education institutions, childcare facilities, child and family centres are exempt from current COVID-19 gathering restrictions.   There are no COVID-19 related policies in Tasmania that currently impact the normal functions of schools or universities.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419111737/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/gatherings-density-and-physical-distancing     Northern Territory  The Northern Territory COVID-19 Directions (No. 56) 2020: Safety Measures for Places, Businesses, Activities, Services and Premises directs that A school, university or other educational institution (among others) require a COVID-19 safety plan, COVID-19 safety supervisor, COVID-19 safety measures and contact information required.    This means that hand sanitiser or other handwashing facilities must be provided; signage must be displayed to promote COVID safe practices; contact details from members of the public who visit for must be collected; must submit a COVID safety plan every 6 mnths, and must have a nominated COVID-19 safety supervisor.   There are no COVID-19 related policies in The Northern Territory that appear to currently significantly impact the normal functions of schools or universities.  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419113917/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0012/951879/cho-directions-no56-safety-measures-places-businesses-activities-services-premises.pdf     Western Australia   The Western Australian GATHERINGS AND ACTIVITIES DIRECTIONS (NO 2) came into effect at 12.01 am on 10 April 2021 and remain current as of 19th April 2021.  This direction provides that gatherings “at a school, university, educational institution or childcare facility that is necessary for the normal business of the facility;” are exempt from the restrictions outlined in the direction.   There are no COVID-19 related policies in Western Australia that appear to currently significantly impact the normal functions of schools or universities.  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419114440/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/090421-Gathering-and-Activities-Directions-No2.pdf     Australian Capital Territory  Public Health (Restricted Activities –Gatherings, Business or Undertakings) Emergency Direction 2021 (No.3) came into effect at 9:00am on 14April2021 and remain current as of 19th April 2021.  This direction provides that gatherings “at a school, university, educational institution or childcare facility that is necessary for the normal business of the facility;” are exempt from the restrictions outlined in the direction.    There are no COVID-19 related policies in the Australian Capital Territory that appear to currently significantly impact the normal functions of schools or universities.  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419114551/https://www.legislation.act.gov.au/ni/2021-216/     South Australia  The Emergency Management (Public Activities No 23) (COVID-19) Direction 2021 came into effect at 12:46 pm on 14 April 2021 and revokes the Emergency Management (Public Activities No 22) (COVID-19) Direction 2021.  It remains current as of 19 April 2021.   This direction provides that gatherings “a gathering at a school, university or other educational institution or a childcare facility, or in a vehicle used for the purposes of any such institution or facility, that is necessary for the normal business of the institution or facility;” are exempt from the restrictions outlined in the direction.    There are no COVID-19 related policies in South Australia that appear to currently significantly impact the normal functions of schools or universities.  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419115425/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0005/374675/Public-Activities-No-23-14-04-2021.pdf
column24: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.   Restrictions on Businesses, Activities and Undertakings Direction (No. 18) are effective from 6am AEST 15 April 2021.  With exceptions this directions provides for a general density quotient for Queensland workplaces of 1 person per 2 square meters.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210419104947/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-for-queensland-2   http://web.archive.org/web/20210419123343/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/business-activity-undertaking-direction    Victoria:   In a modest loosening of restrictions the Workplace Directions (No 24) commenced at 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021.    With limited exceptions, these directions set a general requirement that workplaces must adhere to a density quotient of 1 person per 2 square meters.  Workplace (Additional Industry Obligations) Directions (No 21) commence at 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021.  These directions provide more stringent restrictions for the following specific:   (a) poultry processing facilities; and  (b) abattoirs and meat processing facilities; and  (c) seafood processing facilities; and  (d) supermarket Work Premises and perishable food Work Premises;  and  (e) warehousing and distribution centres; and  (f) commercial cleaning services; and  (g) commercial passenger vehicle services; and  (h) horticulture operations using seasonal workers for seasonal  horticultural work; and  (i) care facilities; and  (j) ports of entry servicing international arrivals; and  (k) hotel quarantine; and  (l) hospitals; and  (m) Australian air transport operators.     An example of additional restrictions provided for by this direction are: "(i) developing separate shifts in a way that minimises physical  interactions between groups of workers attending different shifts;  (ii) separates workers into work areas;  (iii) dividing work areas up further into separate teams;  (iv) providing separate break areas for the separate teams;   Workplace (Additional Industry Obligations) Directions (No 21) 5 of 19  (v) requiring teams to use separate entrances and exits from other  teams;  (vi) where workers are from the same household, ensuring they work  in the same shift and work area;  "  Particularly due to these additional restrictions in place in Victoria, C2 coded as a T1.     http://web.archive.org/web/20210419122619/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Workplace%20%28AIO%29%20Directions%20%28No%2021%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419122032/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Workplace%20Directions%20%28No%2024%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf
column42: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.  The Movement and Gathering Direction (No. 9) are effective from: 6am AEST 15 April 2021 and remain current as of 19th April 2021.  In part these directions limit the gatherings of up to 100 persons at a residence.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210419104947/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-for-queensland-2  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419130528/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/movement-gathering-direction     Victoria:   In a modest loosening of restrictions the Stay Safe Directions (Victoria) (No 19) came into effect at 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021 and remain current as of 19 April 2021.   In part this direction states that no more that “no more than 99 other persons who are visiting for a social gathering; “are permitted to enter a residence at which they do not ordinarily reside.    http://web.archive.org/web/20210419130942/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20%28No%2019%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf    These are currently the most stringent COVID-19 related policy restrictions around private gatherings in Australia.
column115: All Australian States and Territories, except for New South Wales have mandated narrow protections in place.  New South Wales residential aged care facilities must consider the advice of the Chief Health Office, but are able to make their own policy.  Therefore coded as a 2T.      New South Wales:   The NSW Health COVID-19 website states that “Public Health (COVID-19 Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 commenced on 29 March 2021 (with amendments commencing 30 March 2021 and 9 April 2021) and contains directions on gatherings, and the number of persons allowed on non-residential and residential premises.”    “Residential aged care facilities: Operators of aged care facilities must consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in relation to:  management of visitors  screening of staff and visitors  arrangements for group recreation and other activities for residents  wearing of face masks by staff and visitors  vaccinations against influenza and COVID-19”  http://web.archive.org/web/20210423083322/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx     Victoria:   According to the Victorian Government COVID-19 website from 11:59pm on Friday 9 April 2021 Victoria has further relaxed its COVIDSafe settings.  This changed remain current as of the 23rd April 2021.    Face masks?are mandatory when visiting people in?care facilities.   There are no limits on the purpose of visits, the number of visitors or the length of the visit to care facilities.  Facilities may have additional requirements, conditions or restrictions for entry.   You will not be permitted to enter a care facility if you are a worker or visitor and you:   are a diagnosed person or close contact under the Diagnosed Persons and Close Contacts Directions;   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, arrived in Australia from overseas (other than on a quarantine-free flight from an overseas location designated by the Australian Government as a green travel zone);   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, had known contact with a confirmed case (except in the course of your employment while wearing the appropriate level of personal protective equipment in the circumstances);    have SARS-CoV-2 Symptoms; or   if you are a visitor – have been tested for COVID-19 and are awaiting your test results.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423075712/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/visiting-care-facilities     Queensland: The QLD Aged Care Direction (No. 26) came into effect 3pm AEST 8 April 2021 and remain current as of the 23th April 2021.   Queensland COVID-19 Restricted Areas (No. 14) came into effect on 6am AEST 15 April 2021 and remains in effect as of the 23rd April 2021.  This direction declared that there are no Local Government Areas listed as Queensland COVID-19 restricted areas.    In combination these directions mean that residential aged care facilities can have visitors except in the following circumstance: a person has been outside of Australian within the last 14 days; visited a declare COVID-19 hotspot within the last 14 days; been in contact with a COVID-19 positive person within the last 14 days; have symptoms consistent with COVID-19; or is awaiting the results of a COVID-19 test.   There is no restriction on the number of visitors.  Physical distancing and limiting contact with other persons at the facility must be complied with to the extent practicable.   Residents are able to leave facilities for any purpose.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423074236/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/queensland-covid-19-restricted-areas     Western Australia:   The VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES DIRECTIONS (NO 7) came into effect at 11.59 p.m. on 23rd February 2021 and remains in place as of the 23rd April 2021.    Visitors are allow to residential aged care facilities except where and individual:   returned from overseas in the last 14 days  been in contact with a confirmed case of COVID-19 in the last 14 days  a fever or symptoms of acute respiratory infection (e.g. cough, sore throat, runny nose, shortness of breath)  not had an up-to-date influenza vaccination.  Additionally, quarantine centre workers must wear a face mask and keep 1.5 metres away from other people when visiting residential aged care facilities  People entering residential aged care facilities to provide a service to a resident must only do so if it is not reasonably practicable reasonably practicable for the service to be provided via telehealth or some other adaptive model of care or for the resident to attend an external facility to receive the service.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080044/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/240221-Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Directions-No7.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080150/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/document-collections/covid-19-coronavirus-business-and-community-activity     Tasmania:   Residential Aged Care Facilities - No. 11 came into effect on the 1 March 2021 and remains current as of the 23rd April 2021.   In Tasmania, visits to residential aged care facilities are permitted for people providing care and support, with the following restrictions.   Visitors must not:   have any symptoms of COVID-19  been in an area or premises identified as medium or high-risk for COVID-19 in the previous 14 days  had contact with anyone known to have COVID-19, or reasonably suspected of having COVID-19, within the previous 14 days (except as part of their employment while wearing effective personal protective equipment).    Operators of aged care facilities must screen all people (except people attending for emergency management reasons) before they enter the premises.   Operators of aged care facilities are required to record the contact details for everyone who remains on site for 15 minutes or more    Residents may leave the facility.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080824/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/aged-care-facility-visits   https://web.archive.org/web/20210326083819/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/126294/Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-No.-11.pdf     Northern Territory:   COVID-19 Directions (No. 40) 2020 Directions for Aged Care Facilities were signed on the 24th June 202 and remain current on the 23rd April 2021.    The direct provides that visitors to residential aged care facilities are allow except:   While there are no limits on the total number of visits allow, visitor should generally visit one at a time.   A visitor must not have had contact with a positive case within the last 14 days; must not have symptoms consistent with COVID-19; must generally have an up-to-date vaccination against influenza, if the vaccination was available to the person  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423081313/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210326084231/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0006/898953/cho-directions-no40-directions-aged-care-facilities.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423081313/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions       Australian Capital Territory:   The Australian Capital Territory COVID-19 Government website states that   “This means that you can visit your family member or close friend in an aged care facility for the purposes of providing care and support.  You are not limited in the number of hours you can spend with your relative or friend.  However, you cannot visit aged care facilities if you:  Have returned from overseas in the last 14 days.  Have been in a geographical area of risk in the last 14 days.  Have been in contact with a confirmed case of COVID-19 in the last 14 days.  Have a temperature higher than 37.5 degrees or history of fever (e.g. night sweats or chills).  Symptoms of a respiratory infection such as a cough, sore throat, shortness of breath, loss of smell or loss of taste.”  This is enforced by the Public Health (Residential Aged Care Facilities) Emergency Direction 2021 (No 2) that came into effect on 7 February 2021 and remains current as of the 23rd April 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423082330/https://www.legislation.act.gov.au/ni/2021-56/   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423082111/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/stay-safe-and-healthy/aged-care/advice-for-visitors-to-aged-care-facilities

column69: Western Australia:   In an easing of restrictions, and according to the Western Australian COVID-19 Government website, Queensland transitioned from a ‘Low risk’ state to a ‘Very low risk’ state from 12.01am April 19th 2020.      http://web.archive.org/web/20210419140209/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/controlled-interstate-border-changes-queensland    Requirements for individuals entering WA from Very Low Risk jurisdictions are:   “Before travelling to WA, complete a mandatory G2G PASS registration and declaration.  If travelling by air, you must wear a mask on your flight while in WA airspace, and while inside the airport (with some exceptions – e.g. children under the age of 12 are not required to wear a mask).  Complete a health screening on arrival.  Scan your G2G PASS to exit the airport.  No quarantine required.”    Requirements for individuals entering WA from Low Risk jurisdictions are:   “Before travelling to WA, complete a mandatory G2G PASS registration and declaration.  Complete a health screening on arrival.  Scan your G2G PASS and then verify you have suitable premises for self-quarantine.  If you have suitable premises, enter self-quarantine for 14 days. If not, you will be directed to enter a Government-approved quarantine facility at your own expense for 14 days.  You must wear a mask from when you enter WA until you arrive at suitable self-quarantine premises or government approved quarantine facilities. In addition if travelling by air, you must also wear a mask on the flight while in WA airspace, and while inside the airport (with some exceptions – e.g. children under the age of 12 are not required to wear a mask).  You must present for a COVID-19 test on Day 11 if still in WA.”   http://web.archive.org/web/20210419140547/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-border
column106: 5th May 2021 this code was changed from a 3G to a 4G.  This change was made in recognition that in addition to Key workers, Clinically vulnerable groups (non elderly), and Elderly groups - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults over 55 were also able to access the vaccine.    Midwifery Officer for Australian on 18 April about COVID-19 vaccine rollout.  Announced that 1.5 million vaccinations have been undertaken to date in Australia.  Australia remains at vaccine rollout phases 1a and 1b.    Phase 1a includes: Quarantine and border workers, Frontline health care worker sub-groups for prioritisation (frontline staff in facilities or services such as hospital emergency departments,COVID-19 and respiratory wards, Intensive Care Units and High-dependency Units, laboratory staff handling potentially infectious material, ambulance and paramedics service, GP respiratory clinics, COVID-19 testing facilities), Aged care and disability care staff.   Phase 1B includes: Healthcare workers currently employed and not included in Phase 1a, Household contacts of quarantine and border workers, Critical and high risk workers who are currently employed including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing, Elderly people aged 80 years and over, Elderly people aged 70 years and over, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 55 years and over, Adults with an underlying medical condition or significant disability.     Pfizer and AstraZeneca vaccines and being used by Australia.    The Australian Commonwealth Government website updated on 18th April 2021 has slightly changed categories for accessibility of Phase 1B compared to information available for April 13th 2021.  It is now also less clear whether Phase 1A individuals will only receive the Pfizer vaccine.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423052314/https://www.health.gov.au/ministers/the-hon-greg-hunt-mp/media/press-conference-with-minister-hunt-and-professor-alison-mcmillan-the-chief-nursing-and-midwifery-officer-for-australian-on-18-april-about-covid-19-epidemiology-vaccine-rollout  https://web.archive.org/web/20210418231527/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

column33: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.   Restrictions on Businesses, Activities and Undertakings Direction (No. 18) are effective from 6am AEST 15 April 2021.    In part this Directions provides:   No limit for people gathering in outdoor public spaces across Queensland.  One person per 2m2 (e.g. restaurants, cafés, pubs, clubs, museums, art galleries, places of worship, convention centres and Parliament House), including standing, eating and drinking.  Indoor events: 500 people permitted at indoor events with a COVID Safe Event Checklist. Larger events require a COVID Safe Plan.  Ticketed venues: 100% capacity at allocated seated, ticketed venues with patrons encouraged to wear masks on entry and exit (e.g. theatre, live music, cinemas, indoor sports, universities and other higher education institutions). Performers can distance from audience at 2m, except choirs which remain at 4m from the audience.  Dancing: Dancing is allowed in all indoor and outdoor venues including pubs, beer gardens, clubs and nightclubs, subject to the one person per 2m2 rule.  Outdoor dance festivals and music festivals: Fewer than 500 people per day permitted without a COVID Safe Checklist, no requirement to collect contact details. Events with between 500 and 1499 people permitted with a COVID Safe Event Checklist. Larger events require a COVID Safe Event Plan.  Outdoor events: Anzac Day parades, fetes, fun runs and other outdoor only events do not need a COVID Safe Event Checklist or Event Plan.  Open air stadiums: 100% allocated seated and ticketed capacity (with a COVID Safe Plan). All patrons encouraged to wear masks on entry and exit.  Contact tracing information: All hospitality industry businesses (i.e. pubs, clubs, restaurants and cafés) in Queensland must comply with electronic contact information collection requirements.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210419104947/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-for-queensland-2   http://web.archive.org/web/20210419124003/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/roadmap-to-easing-queenslands-restrictions      Victoria:   The Victorian Government COVID-19 Website states that "From 11:59pm on Friday 9 April 2021, Victoria has further relaxed its COVIDSafe Settings. "  Some of the changes that apply to public events are:  "Seated areas at indoor and outdoor entertainment, cultural and sporting venues can use 100% of their seated capacity up to a maximum of 1,000 patrons per space. The density quotient still applies at these venues in non-seated spaces e.g. bathrooms and lobbies.  These venues include theatres,?cinemas,?music halls, concert halls,?auditoriums,?galleries,?museums, and sports and physical recreation facilities.  Non-seated?venues will continue to be required to apply a density quotient of 1 per 2sqm, except unstaffed gyms?(such as 24-hour gyms), which have a?four-square metre rule.  Non seated venues (outdoor) do not have patron caps but density limits apply.  Non-seated venues (indoor) are capped at 75% capacity and density limits apply.  No cap on dance floors, but density limits apply, depending on the venue type.  Casinos to 75 per cent of venue capacity.  All venues must use the Government QR Code Service, or API linked digital system, to maintain electronic record keeping. "    In addition, the Stay Safe Directions (Victoria) (No 19) effective from 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021 and current as of 19 April 2021, states in part that "a person in the State of Victoria must not arrange  to meet, or organise or intentionally attend a gathering of, more than 199  other persons (with any infant under one year of age not counting towards  this limit) for a common purpose at a public place"    http://web.archive.org/web/20210419124827if_/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/how-we-live  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419125148/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/how-we-work    New South Wales:   In a loosening of restrictions The Public Health (COVID-19 Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 was amended commencing 9 April 2021 and contains directions on gatherings, and the number of persons allowed on non-residential and residential premises.   In part, it states that "Non-controlled outdoor public gatherings (up to 200): These events are restricted to 200 people (unless the gathering is a COVID-19 safe outdoor gathering with a COVID-19 Safety Plan.  COVID-19 safe outdoor gathering (up to 5,000): These events can have the lesser of 1 person per 2 square metres of space or 5,000 persons and do not need to be seated or enclosed by fencing and do not have a time limit. The organiser must have and comply with the relevant COVID-19 Safety Plan.  Controlled outdoor public gatherings (up to 10,000): A ticketed and seated COVID-19 safe outdoor gathering that does not last longer than 5 hours can have the maximum of the lesser of 1 person per 2 square metres of space or 10,000 persons. The event must be enclosed by fencing or another barrier. The organiser must have and comply with the relevant COVID-19 Safety Plan."  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419125747/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx
