column69: T1 code reflects Western Australia continuing intra state travel restrictions.        New South Wales:   As documented on the NSW Government COVID-19 Website on the 12th April 2021l five local government areas (forming a part of Brisbane city QLD) were considered Affected Areas.  This remains up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.  Individuals traveling from Affected Areas are allow to enter NSW.  They must complete the declaration form either 24 hours before entry to NSW or on entry.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210423035934/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423040253/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/interstate-hotspots     Victoria:   According to the VIC Government COVID-19 Website  “Based on an assessment of public health risks, the Chief Health Officer has declared the following areas will be designated green zones under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system effective 11:59pm, 11 April 2021:  Greater Brisbane (City of Brisbane, City of Ipswich, Logan City, Moreton Bay Region, Redlands City)   The Gladstone region in Queensland   Byron Shire in New South Wales”    This is up to date on the 23rd April 2021. There are no VIC Govt declared Orange or Red zones and therefore no inter or intra state travel restrictions.    web.archive.org/web/20210423040908/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system    Queensland:   According to the VIC Government COVID-19 Website restrictions eased for all of QLD from 6am Thursday 15 April 2021.  Since this time there has been no intrastate or interstate travel restrictions in place.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210423040919/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/border-closing   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041041/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19     Western Australia:   In an easing of restrictions, and according to the Western Australian COVID-19 Government website, Queensland transitioned from a ‘Low risk’ state to a ‘Very low risk’ state from 12.01am April 19th 2020.  This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041457/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/controlled-interstate-border-changes-queensland     Requirements for individuals entering WA from Very Low Risk jurisdictions are:   “Before travelling to WA, complete a mandatory G2G PASS registration and declaration.  If travelling by air, you must wear a mask on your flight while in WA airspace, and while inside the airport (with some exceptions – e.g. children under the age of 12 are not required to wear a mask).  Complete a health screening on arrival.  Scan your G2G PASS to exit the airport.  No quarantine required.”    Requirements for individuals entering WA from Low Risk jurisdictions are:   “Before travelling to WA, complete a mandatory G2G PASS registration and declaration.  Complete a health screening on arrival.  Scan your G2G PASS and then verify you have suitable premises for self-quarantine.  If you have suitable premises, enter self-quarantine for 14 days. If not, you will be directed to enter a Government-approved quarantine facility at your own expense for 14 days.  You must wear a mask from when you enter WA until you arrive at suitable self-quarantine premises or government approved quarantine facilities. In addition if travelling by air, you must also wear a mask on the flight while in WA airspace, and while inside the airport (with some exceptions – e.g. children under the age of 12 are not required to wear a mask).  You must present for a COVID-19 test on Day 11 if still in WA.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041556/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-border     However, WA continues to implement policies restricting intrastate movement.  The WA Government COVID-19 website states:  There are more than 200 remote Aboriginal communities who are more vulnerable to COVID-19 than other Western Australians.  To help keep the communities safe from COVID-19, there are strict travel restrictions in place.”   Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  providing essential services and supplies  going to work or school there  entering for family or cultural reasons  delivering a community program or activity  entering in an emergency.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041734/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-remote-aboriginal-communities-travel   http://web.archive.org/web/20210420072233/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do     Tasmania:   From the 13th April 2021 and current up to 23rd April 2021, the Tasmanian COVID-19 Government Website shows that there are no inter state travel restrictions issued.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423042005/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania     Northern Territory:   According to the Norther Territory COVID-19 website there are no currently declared hotspots, which is up to date as of 23rd April 2021.  There most recently declared NT Govt declared hotspot ended on 3:00pm on 1 April 2021.  Therefore there has been not intra or interstate travel restrictions since 1st April 2021 and current as of 23rd April 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423043701/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423043857/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19/former-declared-hotspots    There are currently no COVID-19 specific intra state travel restrictions within the NT.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423044526/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/remote-work-and-travel   Australian Capital Territory:   The Australian Capital Territory Government website states on the 23rd of April 2021 that there is currently “no areas of concern”.  This has been the policy of the ACT Government since 1pm on 1st April 2021, when all travel restrictions on travellers from the Greater Brisbane Area were lifted.   http://web.archive.org/web/20210423044602/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/updates/covid-19-areas-of-concern   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423044840/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/act-travel-restrictions-reduced-for-greater-brisbane
column74: As published on the Australian Government Home affairs website, “quarantine free travel between Australia and New Zealand will commence at 21:59 AEST on 18 April 2021. All travellers who have been in either Australia or New Zealand for 14 days can travel by air between Australia and New Zealand quarantine free, without the need to apply for a travel exemption. This includes travellers who have spent all 14 days in either safe travel zone country, or people who have travelled between the two countries and are returning within the 14 day period.” Previously individuals could travel from New Zealand to Australia quarantine free BUT individuals from Australia could not travel to New Zealand Quarantine free.  This policy change represents a modest loosening of border restrictions.    However, other than quarantine-free flights from New Zealand, Australia's boarders are closed to non-citizens (with very limited exception).  As such, continuing to code as a 4.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423034950/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423035333/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/new-zealand-safe-travel-zone
column77: Up to date as of the 19th April 2021 there continues to be no nation wide income support available in response to COVID-19. Updated archived URLS provided.    According to the Australian Government Services Australia website, the "JobKeeper Payment stopped 28 March 2021."  and "The temporary Coronavirus Supplement, at a rate of $150 per fortnight, ended on 31 March 2021."    "working age payments increase by $50 per fortnight the income free area for certain income support payments, including JobSeeker Payment and Youth Allowance for job seekers, changes to $150 per fortnight"            https://web.archive.org/web/20210404080426/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/subjects/getting-help-during-coronavirus-covid-19/payments-and-services-during-coronavirus/if-you-get-payment/im-job-seeker  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419084146/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/subjects/getting-help-during-coronavirus-covid-19/payments-and-services-during-coronavirus/if-you-need-payment  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419084325/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/news/jobkeeper-payment-ends-28-march-2021
column79: Up to date as of the 19th April 2021 there continues to be no nation wide household debt/contract relief available in response to COVID-19. Updated archived comments and URLS provided. Some support remains in some, but not all Australian state/territory jurisdictions.  Therefore a zero is coded.     Western Australia:   "According to The Western Australian Government website, and current as at 19th April 2021“In response to COVID-19, the Western Australian Government implemented a moratorium on some evictions and all rent increases, under the Residential Tenancies (COVID-19 Response) Act 2020. The extended emergency period ends on 28 March 2021, which means that all the existing laws will stay in place until this date, after which the ordinary tenancy laws will apply again.” ..."The extended COVID-19 emergency period has ended, which means the ordinary tenancy laws under the Residential Tenancies Act 1987 (WA) apply again. "    Further that "If rent arrears have accrued during the emergency period, tenants experiencing financial hardship can apply for a grant of 75 per cent of the debt, up to $4,000, paid directly to their landlord. In return, the landlord and tenant must agree to enter into a lease agreement for at least six months beyond 29 March 2021. Visit the Residential Rent Relief Grant Scheme page for more information.  However, the moratorium on some evictions and all rent increases has now ceased.  "  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419085511/https://www.commerce.wa.gov.au/consumer-protection/residential-tenancies-covid-19-response      ACT:   According to the ACT government website, and current as at 19th April 2021 there remain a range of household level debt/contract relief measures in place.  These include:     The annual increase will not be applied to 2020-2021 annual rates bills,   The ACT Revenue Office will implement a new process for rates deferrals for owner occupiers experiencing hardship because of COVID-19. No interest will apply to the deferred amount.   The Government is providing land tax and rates rebates to landlords of residential properties who reduce rents by at least 25 per cent for tenants impacted by COVID-19, The rebate will be equal to 50 per cent of the rent reduction, capped at $1,300 per quarter (around $100 per week), until 30 June 2021.  The ACT Government is offering stamp duty concessions in the ACT for some home buyers planning to live in their new property until June 30 2021  All 2020-21 vehicle registration, parking and public transport fee increases at the current levels.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210326102021/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/business-and-work/economic-survival-package/families-and-households   https://web.archive.org/web/20210419090525/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/business-and-work/economic-survival-package/families-and-households      NSW:  According to the NSW government Fair trading website, and current as at 19th April 2021:   "The temporary tenancy moratorium introduced to restrict when landlords could evict tenants due to rental arrears as a result of COVID-19 ended on 26 March 2021. From 27 March 2021, a six-month transitional period has begun."..."A six-month transitional period means COVID-19 impacted tenants who accrued rent arrears between 15 April 2020 and 26 March 2021 (the moratorium period) will not be subject to the standard tenancy eviction rules for those arrears."  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419091117/https://www.fairtrading.nsw.gov.au/resource-library/publications/coronavirus-covid-19/property/moratorium       QLD:   According to the QLD government COVID-19 website, and current as at 19th April 2021:   There remain some temporary regulatory measures to 30 April 2021 means Queensland remains ready to respond to COVID-19 risks as the transition back to normal residential tenancy arrangements continues.  These are: provisions allowing tenants experiencing domestic and family violence to end their tenancies quickly; protections for tenants against being listed in a tenancy database for rent arrears caused by COVID-19 impacts; limits on reletting costs for eligible tenants who end their fixed term tenancies early; short term tenancy statement extensions for moveable dwellings; entry restrictions and requirements to support COVID-19 social distancing measures; relaxed repair and maintenance obligations.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210326102104/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/the-hub/covid-19-tenancy-update    VIC:   According to The Victorian Government COVID-19 website, and current as at 19th April 2021, COVID-19 "The temporary changes to renting laws because of COVID-19 ended on Sunday 28 March 2021."  "This means that from Monday 29 March 2021:  rent reduction agreements can no longer be lodged with Consumer Affairs Victoria   you can apply directly to the Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (VCAT) for dispute resolution  applications for the rent relief grant have closed  rental providers (landlords) can issue a notice to vacate to renters (tenants) and raise the rent again, but new rights and responsibilities apply"  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419091806/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/renting-laws-and-support-during-coronavirus-covid-19-pandemic      NT:  According to The Northern Territory Consumer Affairs website, and current as at 19th April 2021 “On 24 April 2020, the Northern Territory Parliament passed the Tenancies Legislation Amendment Act 2020. This provides the framework for the Territory’s response to tenancy issues arising as a result of the COVID-19 emergency.” These modifications: Extending notice timeframes for certain pre termination actions under the Act; establish New processes for termination of a tenancy; and provide for modified entry and inspection processes.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210326102331/https://nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0010/818308/s28.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419092157/https://consumeraffairs.nt.gov.au/rental-changes-due-to-covid-19      SA:  According to SA Government COVID-19 website, and current as at 26th March 2021 “State Parliament has endorsed a number of initiatives aimed at helping landlords and tenants whose incomes have been affected by COVID-19.” This includes “Ensuring tenants cannot be evicted by landlords due to an inability to paid rent because of COVID-19 employment loss”.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210419092340/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/school-and-community/housing-and-in-the-community      TAS:   The Northern Territory Consumer Affairs website, and current as at 19th April 2021 states in part that "the Tasmanian Government is providing financial support for residential tenancies in the private market and who are experiencing financial hardship due to the impact of the coronavirus (COVID-19).  The COVID-19 Rent Relief Fund and Landlord Support Fund offers support of up to $2,000 or four weeks rent per fund to eligible tenants and landlords.  Tenants can apply for a first, second, third, fourth or fifth support payment if eligible.  Landlords can apply for a first, second, third and fourth support payment if eligible.  Additional support is available between 1 April 2021 and 30 June 2021.  See the Consumer, Building and Occupational Services (CBOS) website for more information"  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419093130/https://www.cbos.tas.gov.au/newsroom/covid-19-novel-coronavirus/coronavirus-covid-19-consumer-advice  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419093217/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/rental-housing

column94: On the 21st of April the Premier of Victoria announced that “Victoria will provide $50 million to establish mRNA vaccine and therapeutic manufacturing in Melbourne, through partnerships with our world-class Victorian universities, leading researchers, and Australian and international manufacturing companies.”    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423050928/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/victoria-ready-lead-new-vaccine-manufacturing

column106: 5th May 2021 this code was changed from a 3G to a 4G.  This change was made in recognition that in addition to Key workers, Clinically vulnerable groups (non elderly), and Elderly groups - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults over 55 were also able to access the vaccine.    A press statement on the 22nd April from the Australian Prime Minister announced some changes to Australia’s Vaccination program.    “National Cabinet agreed the imperative to work together to administer COVID-19 vaccinations to Australians as quickly as possible.  National Cabinet agreed to recalibrate the delivery model for the COVID-19 Vaccination Rollout Strategy, taking into account the Australian Technical Advisory Group on Immunisation (ATAGI) medical advice on the use of AstraZeneca vaccines, and COVID-19 vaccine supply arrangements, by:    Limiting access to the Pfizer vaccine to:  People aged under 50 years eligible in phases 1a and 1b (pending commencement of subsequent phases);  Residential Aged Care Facility (RACF) residents (through completion of the Commonwealth’s existing in-reach program):  Disability care residents with complex needs who require in-reach vaccination services;  People in remote and very remote communities where it makes sense to use a single vaccine for all or most of the community; and  In very limited circumstances, quarantine, border and frontline health care workers who are 50 years of age or over and not already vaccinated, where the relevant state or territory determines the worker should be fully vaccinated and protected as soon as possible.    Better utilising the available stock of AstraZeneca doses: by bringing forward access to the AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine for phase 2a eligible people aged 50–69 years commencing with General Practice Respiratory Clinics (GPRCs) and state and territory COVID-19 vaccination sites from 3 May 2021 and General Practice (GP) clinics from 17 May 2021. Individual GP clinics who have the capacity to and wish to commence earlier than 17 May can apply to the Department of Health for access to this cohort earlier.”    Australia remains at stages 1A and 1B of the Vaccine role out (full details at the link – these details remain the same as those provided in the qualitative note made on 18th April 2021).   AstraZenec and Pfizer vaccines are being used.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423073431/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/national-cabinet-4   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423073517/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine
column119: List last updated on the 22nd of April 2021    Phase 1 A  - Quarantine and border workers  - Frontline health workers (including ambulance and paramedics)  - Aged care and disability staff  - Aged care and disability residents    Phase 1 B  - Other healthcare workers  - Households of quarantine and border workers  - Critical defence/police/fire workers and meat processing  - Over 80  - Over 70  - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 50 years and over  - Adults with an underlying medical condition or significant disability    Phase 2 A  - Over 50  - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults aged 18-49 years  - Other critical and high risk workers    Phase 2 B  - Balance of population aged between 16-49 years	   - Catch up any unvaccinated Australians from previous phases    Phase 3  - People 16 and under    https://web.archive.org/web/20210422142225/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine
column121: Phase 1b vaccinations will commence on 22 March 2021.    From Monday 22 March an additional 6.14m Australians will be eligible to receive a coronavirus vaccination.    Unlike the almost 250,000 Pfizer vaccinations that have been administered as part of the invitation-only phase 1a, the Australians eligible under phase 1b are able to book vaccine appointments as soon as the phase begins.    Eligible groups in Phase B are:    Phase 1b  Elderly adults aged 80 years and over  Elderly adults aged 70-79 years  Other health care workers  Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people > 55  Adults with an underlying medical condition, including those with a disability      https://web.archive.org/web/20210423125846/https://www.health.gov.au/resources/publications/covid-19-vaccination-phase-1b-rollout    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423124918/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2021/mar/19/australias-covid-vaccine-rollout-whos-next-are-you-eligible-and-how-to-book-an-appointment    https://web.archive.org/web/20210218040646/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine
column129: COVID-19 vaccines will be voluntary and free  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia. Vaccination providers cannot charge you for the COVID-19 vaccine or your appointments to receive the vaccine.    If your vaccination provider charges for any costs associated with the administration of the COVID-19 vaccination (including booking fees), you should:    contact us by emailing provider.benefits.integrity@health.gov.au,  or call the Provider Benefits Integrity Hotline on 1800 314 808 (9am to 5pm AEST weekdays).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423124712/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19

column24: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.   Restrictions on Businesses, Activities and Undertakings Direction (No. 18) are effective from 6am AEST 15 April 2021.  With exceptions this directions provides for a general density quotient for Queensland workplaces of 1 person per 2 square meters.   This is up to date as of 23rd April 2021. Updated archived links provided.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423030727/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/business-activity-undertaking-direction     Victoria:    In a modest loosening of restrictions the Workplace Directions (No 24) commenced at 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021.    With limited exceptions, these directions set a general requirement that workplaces must adhere to a density quotient of 1 person per 2 square meters.  Workplace (Additional Industry Obligations) Directions (No 21) commence at 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021.  These directions provide more stringent restrictions for the following specific:   (a) poultry processing facilities; and  (b) abattoirs and meat processing facilities; and  (c) seafood processing facilities; and  (d) supermarket Work Premises and perishable food Work Premises;  and  (e) warehousing and distribution centres; and  (f) commercial cleaning services; and  (g) commercial passenger vehicle services; and  (h) horticulture operations using seasonal workers for seasonal  horticultural work; and  (i) care facilities; and  (j) ports of entry servicing international arrivals; and  (k) hotel quarantine; and  (l) hospitals; and  (m) Australian air transport operators.     An example of additional restrictions provided for by this direction are: "(i) developing separate shifts in a way that minimises physical  interactions between groups of workers attending different shifts;  (ii) separates workers into work areas;  (iii) dividing work areas up further into separate teams;  (iv) providing separate break areas for the separate teams;   Workplace (Additional Industry Obligations) Directions (No 21) 5 of 19  (v) requiring teams to use separate entrances and exits from other  teams;  (vi) where workers are from the same household, ensuring they work  in the same shift and work area;  "    This is up to date as of 23rd April 2021. Updated archived links provided.   Particularly due to these additional restrictions in place in Victoria, C2 coded as a T1.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423030856/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/victorias-restriction-levels-covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419122032/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Workplace%20Directions%20%28No%2024%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf
column33: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.   Restrictions on Businesses, Activities and Undertakings Direction (No. 18) are effective from 6am AEST 15 April 2021.    In part this Directions provides:   No limit for people gathering in outdoor public spaces across Queensland.  One person per 2m2 (e.g. restaurants, cafés, pubs, clubs, museums, art galleries, places of worship, convention centres and Parliament House), including standing, eating and drinking.  Indoor events: 500 people permitted at indoor events with a COVID Safe Event Checklist. Larger events require a COVID Safe Plan.  Ticketed venues: 100% capacity at allocated seated, ticketed venues with patrons encouraged to wear masks on entry and exit (e.g. theatre, live music, cinemas, indoor sports, universities and other higher education institutions). Performers can distance from audience at 2m, except choirs which remain at 4m from the audience.  Dancing: Dancing is allowed in all indoor and outdoor venues including pubs, beer gardens, clubs and nightclubs, subject to the one person per 2m2 rule.  Outdoor dance festivals and music festivals: Fewer than 500 people per day permitted without a COVID Safe Checklist, no requirement to collect contact details. Events with between 500 and 1499 people permitted with a COVID Safe Event Checklist. Larger events require a COVID Safe Event Plan.  Outdoor events: Anzac Day parades, fetes, fun runs and other outdoor only events do not need a COVID Safe Event Checklist or Event Plan.  Open air stadiums: 100% allocated seated and ticketed capacity (with a COVID Safe Plan). All patrons encouraged to wear masks on entry and exit.  Contact tracing information: All hospitality industry businesses (i.e. pubs, clubs, restaurants and cafés) in Queensland must comply with electronic contact information collection requirements.    This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423030856/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/victorias-restriction-levels-covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419122032/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Workplace%20Directions%20%28No%2024%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf     Victoria:   The Victorian Government COVID-19 Website states that "From 11:59pm on Friday 9 April 2021, Victoria has further relaxed its COVIDSafe Settings. "  Some of the changes that apply to public events are:  "Seated areas at indoor and outdoor entertainment, cultural and sporting venues can use 100% of their seated capacity up to a maximum of 1,000 patrons per space. The density quotient still applies at these venues in non-seated spaces e.g. bathrooms and lobbies.  These venues include theatres, cinemas, music halls, concert halls, auditoriums, galleries, museums, and sports and physical recreation facilities.  Non-seated venues will continue to be required to apply a density quotient of 1 per 2sqm, except unstaffed gyms(such as 24-hour gyms), which have a four-square metre rule.  Non seated venues (outdoor) do not have patron caps but density limits apply.  Non-seated venues (indoor) are capped at 75% capacity and density limits apply.  No cap on dance floors, but density limits apply, depending on the venue type.  Casinos to 75 per cent of venue capacity.  All venues must use the Government QR Code Service, or API linked digital system, to maintain electronic record keeping. "    In addition, the Stay Safe Directions (Victoria) (No 19) effective from 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021 and current as of 19 April 2021, states in part that "a person in the State of Victoria must not arrange  to meet, or organise or intentionally attend a gathering of, more than 199  other persons (with any infant under one year of age not counting towards  this limit) for a common purpose at a public place"  This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210419130942/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202104/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20%28No%2019%29%20-%209%20April%202021.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423031503/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/how-we-live     New South Wales:   In a loosening of restrictions The Public Health (COVID-19 Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 was amended commencing 9 April 2021 and contains directions on gatherings, and the number of persons allowed on non-residential and residential premises.   In part, it states that "Non-controlled outdoor public gatherings (up to 200): These events are restricted to 200 people (unless the gathering is a COVID-19 safe outdoor gathering with a COVID-19 Safety Plan.  COVID-19 safe outdoor gathering (up to 5,000): These events can have the lesser of 1 person per 2 square metres of space or 5,000 persons and do not need to be seated or enclosed by fencing and do not have a time limit. The organiser must have and comply with the relevant COVID-19 Safety Plan.  Controlled outdoor public gatherings (up to 10,000): A ticketed and seated COVID-19 safe outdoor gathering that does not last longer than 5 hours can have the maximum of the lesser of 1 person per 2 square metres of space or 10,000 persons. The event must be enclosed by fencing or another barrier. The organiser must have and comply with the relevant COVID-19 Safety Plan."  This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423031623/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx
column42: Queensland:   In a loosening of restrictions The Restrictions for Queensland Direction (No. 2) was revoked from 6:00am on 15 April 2021.  The Movement and Gathering Direction (No. 9) are effective from: 6am AEST 15 April 2021 and remain current as of 19th April 2021.  In part these directions limit the gatherings of up to 100 persons at a residence.   This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210419104947/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-for-queensland-2  http://web.archive.org/web/20210419130528/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/movement-gathering-direction     Victoria:   In a modest loosening of restrictions the Stay Safe Directions (Victoria) (No 19) came into effect at 11:59:00pm on 9 April 2021 and remain current as of 19 April 2021.   In part this direction states that no more that “no more than 99 other persons who are visiting for a social gathering; “are permitted to enter a residence at which they do not ordinarily reside.    This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423032200/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/movement-gathering-direction  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423032234/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/revoked/restrictions-for-queensland-2     These are currently the most stringent COVID-19 related policy restrictions around private gatherings in Australia.

column69: T1 code reflects Western Australia continuing intra state travel restrictions.        New South Wales:   As documented on the NSW Government COVID-19 Website on the 12th April 2021l five local government areas (forming a part of Brisbane city QLD) were considered Affected Areas.  This remains up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.  Individuals traveling from Affected Areas are allow to enter NSW.  They must complete the declaration form either 24 hours before entry to NSW or on entry.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210423035934/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423040253/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/interstate-hotspots     Victoria:   According to the VIC Government COVID-19 Website  “Based on an assessment of public health risks, the Chief Health Officer has declared the following areas will be designated green zones under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system effective 11:59pm, 11 April 2021:  Greater Brisbane (City of Brisbane, City of Ipswich, Logan City, Moreton Bay Region, Redlands City)   The Gladstone region in Queensland   Byron Shire in New South Wales”    This is up to date on the 23rd April 2021. There are no VIC Govt declared Orange or Red zones and therefore no inter or intra state travel restrictions.    web.archive.org/web/20210423040908/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system    Queensland:   According to the VIC Government COVID-19 Website restrictions eased for all of QLD from 6am Thursday 15 April 2021.  Since this time there has been no intrastate or interstate travel restrictions in place.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210423040919/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/border-closing   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041041/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19     Western Australia:   In an easing of restrictions, and according to the Western Australian COVID-19 Government website, Queensland transitioned from a ‘Low risk’ state to a ‘Very low risk’ state from 12.01am April 19th 2020.  This is up to date as of the 23rd April 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041457/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/controlled-interstate-border-changes-queensland     Requirements for individuals entering WA from Very Low Risk jurisdictions are:   “Before travelling to WA, complete a mandatory G2G PASS registration and declaration.  If travelling by air, you must wear a mask on your flight while in WA airspace, and while inside the airport (with some exceptions – e.g. children under the age of 12 are not required to wear a mask).  Complete a health screening on arrival.  Scan your G2G PASS to exit the airport.  No quarantine required.”    Requirements for individuals entering WA from Low Risk jurisdictions are:   “Before travelling to WA, complete a mandatory G2G PASS registration and declaration.  Complete a health screening on arrival.  Scan your G2G PASS and then verify you have suitable premises for self-quarantine.  If you have suitable premises, enter self-quarantine for 14 days. If not, you will be directed to enter a Government-approved quarantine facility at your own expense for 14 days.  You must wear a mask from when you enter WA until you arrive at suitable self-quarantine premises or government approved quarantine facilities. In addition if travelling by air, you must also wear a mask on the flight while in WA airspace, and while inside the airport (with some exceptions – e.g. children under the age of 12 are not required to wear a mask).  You must present for a COVID-19 test on Day 11 if still in WA.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041556/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-border     However, WA continues to implement policies restricting intrastate movement.  The WA Government COVID-19 website states:  There are more than 200 remote Aboriginal communities who are more vulnerable to COVID-19 than other Western Australians.  To help keep the communities safe from COVID-19, there are strict travel restrictions in place.”   Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  providing essential services and supplies  going to work or school there  entering for family or cultural reasons  delivering a community program or activity  entering in an emergency.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423041734/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-remote-aboriginal-communities-travel   http://web.archive.org/web/20210420072233/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do     Tasmania:   From the 13th April 2021 and current up to 23rd April 2021, the Tasmanian COVID-19 Government Website shows that there are no inter state travel restrictions issued.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423042005/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania     Northern Territory:   According to the Norther Territory COVID-19 website there are no currently declared hotspots, which is up to date as of 23rd April 2021.  There most recently declared NT Govt declared hotspot ended on 3:00pm on 1 April 2021.  Therefore there has been not intra or interstate travel restrictions since 1st April 2021 and current as of 23rd April 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423043701/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423043857/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19/former-declared-hotspots    There are currently no COVID-19 specific intra state travel restrictions within the NT.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423044526/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/remote-work-and-travel   Australian Capital Territory:   The Australian Capital Territory Government website states on the 23rd of April 2021 that there is currently “no areas of concern”.  This has been the policy of the ACT Government since 1pm on 1st April 2021, when all travel restrictions on travellers from the Greater Brisbane Area were lifted.   http://web.archive.org/web/20210423044602/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/updates/covid-19-areas-of-concern   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423044840/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/act-travel-restrictions-reduced-for-greater-brisbane
column74: As published on the Australian Government Home affairs website, “quarantine free travel between Australia and New Zealand will commence at 21:59 AEST on 18 April 2021. All travellers who have been in either Australia or New Zealand for 14 days can travel by air between Australia and New Zealand quarantine free, without the need to apply for a travel exemption. This includes travellers who have spent all 14 days in either safe travel zone country, or people who have travelled between the two countries and are returning within the 14 day period.” Previously individuals could travel from New Zealand to Australia quarantine free BUT individuals from Australia could not travel to New Zealand Quarantine free.  This policy change represents a modest loosening of border restrictions.    However, other than quarantine-free flights from New Zealand, Australia's boarders are closed to non-citizens (with very limited exception).  As such, continuing to code as a 4.   This is up to date on the 23rd April 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423034950/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423035333/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/new-zealand-safe-travel-zone
column103: The Western Australian government brought the FACE COVERING DIRECTIONS (NO 2) into force at 6pm Friday 23rd April 2021.  This was done in response to a single case of community transmission of COVID-19.  An international arrival tested positive to COVID-19 after being released following 14 days of hotel quarantine.  This international arrival then infected a close contact.    The Face Covering Directions (No2.) Stipulate that those in Metropolitan Perth, the Peel Region, and Rottnest Island must wear a face covering at all times while they are outside the place where they ordinarily reside, including while in a vehicle (with some limited exceptions).     https://web.archive.org/web/20210502064043/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-metro-and-peel-enter-3-day-lockdown   https://web.archive.org/web/20210502071435/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/Face-Covering-Directions-No2.pdf
column115: All Australian States and Territories, except for New South Wales have mandated narrow protections in place.  New South Wales residential aged care facilities must consider the advice of the Chief Health Office, but are able to make their own policy.  Therefore coded as a 2T.      New South Wales:   The NSW Health COVID-19 website states that “Public Health (COVID-19 Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 commenced on 29 March 2021 (with amendments commencing 30 March 2021 and 9 April 2021) and contains directions on gatherings, and the number of persons allowed on non-residential and residential premises.”    “Residential aged care facilities: Operators of aged care facilities must consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in relation to:  management of visitors  screening of staff and visitors  arrangements for group recreation and other activities for residents  wearing of face masks by staff and visitors  vaccinations against influenza and COVID-19”  http://web.archive.org/web/20210423083322/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx     Victoria:   According to the Victorian Government COVID-19 website from 11:59pm on Friday 9 April 2021 Victoria has further relaxed its COVIDSafe settings.  This changed remain current as of the 23rd April 2021.    Face masks?are mandatory when visiting people in?care facilities.   There are no limits on the purpose of visits, the number of visitors or the length of the visit to care facilities.  Facilities may have additional requirements, conditions or restrictions for entry.   You will not be permitted to enter a care facility if you are a worker or visitor and you:   are a diagnosed person or close contact under the Diagnosed Persons and Close Contacts Directions;   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, arrived in Australia from overseas (other than on a quarantine-free flight from an overseas location designated by the Australian Government as a green travel zone);   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, had known contact with a confirmed case (except in the course of your employment while wearing the appropriate level of personal protective equipment in the circumstances);    have SARS-CoV-2 Symptoms; or   if you are a visitor – have been tested for COVID-19 and are awaiting your test results.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423075712/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/visiting-care-facilities     Queensland: The QLD Aged Care Direction (No. 26) came into effect 3pm AEST 8 April 2021 and remain current as of the 23th April 2021.   Queensland COVID-19 Restricted Areas (No. 14) came into effect on 6am AEST 15 April 2021 and remains in effect as of the 23rd April 2021.  This direction declared that there are no Local Government Areas listed as Queensland COVID-19 restricted areas.    In combination these directions mean that residential aged care facilities can have visitors except in the following circumstance: a person has been outside of Australian within the last 14 days; visited a declare COVID-19 hotspot within the last 14 days; been in contact with a COVID-19 positive person within the last 14 days; have symptoms consistent with COVID-19; or is awaiting the results of a COVID-19 test.   There is no restriction on the number of visitors.  Physical distancing and limiting contact with other persons at the facility must be complied with to the extent practicable.   Residents are able to leave facilities for any purpose.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423074236/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/queensland-covid-19-restricted-areas     Western Australia:   The VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES DIRECTIONS (NO 7) came into effect at 11.59 p.m. on 23rd February 2021 and remains in place as of the 23rd April 2021.    Visitors are allow to residential aged care facilities except where and individual:   returned from overseas in the last 14 days  been in contact with a confirmed case of COVID-19 in the last 14 days  a fever or symptoms of acute respiratory infection (e.g. cough, sore throat, runny nose, shortness of breath)  not had an up-to-date influenza vaccination.  Additionally, quarantine centre workers must wear a face mask and keep 1.5 metres away from other people when visiting residential aged care facilities  People entering residential aged care facilities to provide a service to a resident must only do so if it is not reasonably practicable reasonably practicable for the service to be provided via telehealth or some other adaptive model of care or for the resident to attend an external facility to receive the service.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080044/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/240221-Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Directions-No7.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080150/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/document-collections/covid-19-coronavirus-business-and-community-activity     Tasmania:   Residential Aged Care Facilities - No. 11 came into effect on the 1 March 2021 and remains current as of the 23rd April 2021.   In Tasmania, visits to residential aged care facilities are permitted for people providing care and support, with the following restrictions.   Visitors must not:   have any symptoms of COVID-19  been in an area or premises identified as medium or high-risk for COVID-19 in the previous 14 days  had contact with anyone known to have COVID-19, or reasonably suspected of having COVID-19, within the previous 14 days (except as part of their employment while wearing effective personal protective equipment).    Operators of aged care facilities must screen all people (except people attending for emergency management reasons) before they enter the premises.   Operators of aged care facilities are required to record the contact details for everyone who remains on site for 15 minutes or more    Residents may leave the facility.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080824/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/families-community/aged-care-facility-visits   https://web.archive.org/web/20210326083819/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0024/126294/Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-No.-11.pdf     Northern Territory:   COVID-19 Directions (No. 40) 2020 Directions for Aged Care Facilities were signed on the 24th June 202 and remain current on the 23rd April 2021.    The direct provides that visitors to residential aged care facilities are allow except:   While there are no limits on the total number of visits allow, visitor should generally visit one at a time.   A visitor must not have had contact with a positive case within the last 14 days; must not have symptoms consistent with COVID-19; must generally have an up-to-date vaccination against influenza, if the vaccination was available to the person  https://web.archive.org/web/20210423081313/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210326084231/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0006/898953/cho-directions-no40-directions-aged-care-facilities.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423081313/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions       Australian Capital Territory:   The Australian Capital Territory COVID-19 Government website states that   “This means that you can visit your family member or close friend in an aged care facility for the purposes of providing care and support.  You are not limited in the number of hours you can spend with your relative or friend.  However, you cannot visit aged care facilities if you:  Have returned from overseas in the last 14 days.  Have been in a geographical area of risk in the last 14 days.  Have been in contact with a confirmed case of COVID-19 in the last 14 days.  Have a temperature higher than 37.5 degrees or history of fever (e.g. night sweats or chills).  Symptoms of a respiratory infection such as a cough, sore throat, shortness of breath, loss of smell or loss of taste.”  This is enforced by the Public Health (Residential Aged Care Facilities) Emergency Direction 2021 (No 2) that came into effect on 7 February 2021 and remains current as of the 23rd April 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210423082330/https://www.legislation.act.gov.au/ni/2021-56/   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423082111/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/stay-safe-and-healthy/aged-care/advice-for-visitors-to-aged-care-facilities

column15: The Western Australian government announced that people in Perth metropolitan area, the Peel region, and Rottnest Island must enter into a lockdown from 12.01am Saturday, April 24, to limit any potential spread of COVID-19. The lock-down was announced in response to a single case of community transmission of COVID-19.  An international arrival tested positive to COVID-19 after being released following 14 days of hotel quarantine.  This international arrival then infected a close contact.    The lock-down announced was planned for 3 days, and is scheduled to end at 12.01am Tuesday, April 27.    STAY AT HOME AND CLOSURE (PERTH, PEEL AND ROTTNEST) DIRECTIONS were inforce for the duration of the lockdown and stipulated that schools, universities or education institutions must close.  Except for the extent that the institution provides accommodation for students.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210502061717/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/20210423-Stay-at-Home-and-Closure-Perth-Peel-and-Rottnest-Directions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502064043/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-metro-and-peel-enter-3-day-lockdown

column42: The Western Australian government announced that people in Perth metropolitan area, the Peel region, and Rottnest Island must enter into a lockdown from 12.01am Saturday, April 24, to limit any potential spread of COVID-19. The lock-down was announced in response to a single case of community transmission of COVID-19.  An international arrival tested positive to COVID-19 after being released following 14 days of hotel quarantine.  This international arrival then infected a close contact.    The lock-down announced was planned for 3 days, and is scheduled to end at 12.01am Tuesday, April 27.    STAY AT HOME AND CLOSURE (PERTH, PEEL AND ROTTNEST) DIRECTIONS were in force for the duration of the lockdown.  No visitors to private homes were allowed.  Outdoor exercise with a maximum of 4 other people, as long as there’s no more than 2 adults at one time was allowable.  Weddings and funerals allowed to proceed with a limit of 100 patrons, not including staff, and masks must be worn.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210502061717/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/20210423-Stay-at-Home-and-Closure-Perth-Peel-and-Rottnest-Directions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502064043/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-metro-and-peel-enter-3-day-lockdown
column60: The Western Australian government announced that people in Perth metropolitan area, the Peel region, and Rottnest Island must enter into a lockdown from 12.01am Saturday, April 24, to limit any potential spread of COVID-19. The lock-down was announced in response to a single case of community transmission of COVID-19.  An international arrival tested positive to COVID-19 after being released following 14 days of hotel quarantine.  This international arrival then infected a close contact.    The lock-down announced was planned for 3 days, and is scheduled to end at 12.01am Tuesday, April 27.    STAY AT HOME AND CLOSURE (PERTH, PEEL AND ROTTNEST) DIRECTIONS were in force for the duration of the lockdown and stipulated that individuals could leave their home for the following reasons:   work because you cannot work from home if your place of work is not closed  shop for essentials like groceries, medicine and necessary supplies  access medical or health care  provide care and support to a relative or other person who has particular needs  other specifically authorised compassionate requirements  exercise, but only for 1 hour per day. You can exercise with a maximum of 4 other people , as long as there’s no more than 2 adults at one time  leave due to an emergency.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210502061717/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/20210423-Stay-at-Home-and-Closure-Perth-Peel-and-Rottnest-Directions.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502064043/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-metro-and-peel-enter-3-day-lockdown
column69: Western Australia  As published on the Western Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Western Australian government announced that the Perth, Peel Region, and Rottnest Island lockdown would end as planned from 12.01am, Tuesday 27 April.  Further that a post-lockdown transition would be in place until 12.01am, Saturday 1 May.    Regional travel restrictions were removed – that previously restricted travel outside or into the Perth, Peel Region, and Rottnest Island areas.  However, restrictions on travel to remote Aboriginal communities remain in place.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502073200/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/end-of-lockdown-perth-and-peel       Victoria   In response to instances of community transmission of COVID-19 in Western Australia (originating from the Hotel quarantine system) and as published on the Victorian Government’s COVID-19 website, “The Chief Health Officer has declared the Perth metropolitan area and Peel region in Western Australia red zones (other than for transit) under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system from 2:01am Victorian time on Saturday 24 April.”…” A red zone means non-Victorian residents are not allowed to enter Victoria without an exception, Specified Worker Permit, transit permit, freight worker permit, or exemption.” This policy change represented a significant tightening of restrictions.    This Victorian Government policy remained in force until 12.01am Wednesday 28 April.  Victoria’s Chief Health Officer had declared that the Perth metropolitan area and the Peel region in Western Australia would be designated orange zones under Victoria’s travel permit system from 12.01am Wednesday 28 April.   People entering Victoria with an orange zone permit must [self] isolate, get a coronavirus test within 72 hours, and stay isolated until they receive a negative result    https://web.archive.org/web/20210426065312/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system   https://web.archive.org/web/20210507123858/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system

column83: On the 27 April 2021, a media release by Prime Ministers Scott Morrison stated that “Following consultation with the Indian Government, Australia will provide emergency medical supplies.   The initial package of support includes rapid deployment of 500 non-invasive ventilators, with capacity to deploy up to a total of 3,000 ventilators.   Our Government is also supplying a significant package of personal protective equipment (PPE), including one million surgical masks, 500,000 P2/N95 masks, 100,000 surgical gowns, 100,000 goggles, 100,000 pairs of gloves and 20,000 face shields.   The Government has agreed to commence procurement of 100 oxygen concentrators, along with tanks and consumables.”   https://web.archive.org/web/20210521071705/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/flights-india-now-paused-australia-sends-emergency-medical-supplies

The SAFE TRANSITION FOR WESTERN AUSTRALIA DIRECTIONS (NO 2) These directions come into effect at 12.01 am on Tuesday, 27 April 2021.    This direction maintained the previous mask wearing requirements for Metropolitan Perth, the Peel Region, and Rottnest Island.  Individuals must wear a face covering at all times while they are outside the place where they ordinarily reside, including while in a vehicle (with some limited exceptions).     https://web.archive.org/web/20210502073200/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/end-of-lockdown-perth-and-peel  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502074436/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/Safe-transition-for%20Western-Australia-Directions-No2.pdf
column106: 5th May 2021 this code was changed from a 3G to a 4G.  This change was made in recognition that in addition to Key workers, Clinically vulnerable groups (non elderly), and Elderly groups - Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults over 505 were also able to access the vaccine.    According to the Australian Commonwealth Australian Government COVID-19 website on the 27th March 2021:   “We are currently vaccinating people in Phase 1a and Phase 1b. States and territories will begin vaccinating people in Phase 2a in May starting with all adults 50 years and over.”  "Phase 1a   Quarantine and border workers, including:  •	staff at entry points to the country (such as sea ports and land borders)  •	staff working in quarantine facilities, including those employed under Commonwealth, state or private agreements, and    •	Commonwealth employees (including Defence personnel) who are identified as having the potential to encounter returning travellers as part of their work.  Quarantine, border and front line health care workers will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility.	   Frontline health care worker sub-groups for prioritisation  •	frontline staff in facilities or services such as hospital emergency departments,COVID-19 and respiratory wards, Intensive Care Units and High-dependency Units  •	laboratory staff handling potentially infectious material  •	ambulance and paramedics service  •	GP respiratory clinics  •	COVID-19 testing facilities  Frontline health care workers who fit under one of these sub groups will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility. 	   Aged care and disability care staff, including:  •	nursing and personal care staff  •	allied health professionals who routinely provide care  •	kitchen, cleaning, laundry, garden and office staff	   Aged care and disability care residents	    Phase 1b  Learn more about Phase 1b, including eligibility and evidence requirements    Healthcare workers currently employed and not included in Phase 1a	   Household contacts of quarantine and border workers	   Critical and high risk workers who are currently employed including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing	   Elderly people aged 80 years and over	   Elderly people aged 70 years and over	   Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 50 years and over	   Adults with an underlying medical condition or significant disability"    https://web.archive.org/web/20210427083649/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

column69: The Victorian Government Covid-19 website states that   “Victoria’s Chief Health Officer has declared that the Perth metropolitan area and the Peel region in Western Australia will be designated orange zones under Victoria’s travel permit system from 12.01am Wednesday 28 April.   People entering Victoria with an orange zone permit must [self] isolate, get a coronavirus test within 72 hours, and stay isolated until they receive a negative result. ”  This up to date as of the 7th May 2021.   This policy represents an easing of restrictions.  Previously, Perth metropolitan area and the Peel region in Western Australia were considered Red zones by the Victorian Government.  A red zone means non-Victorian residents are not allowed to enter Victoria without an exception, Specified Worker Permit, transit permit, freight worker permit, or exemption.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210507123858/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system
column74: According to the Australian Commonwealth Australian Government COVID-19 website on the 28th March 2021:   “Australia has strict border measures in place to protect the health of the Australian community. Very limited flights are currently available to and from Australia and you may not be able to travel at this time. Travel restrictions are subject to change. Check back regularly. See: National Cabinet media statement.  You cannot come to Australia unless you are in an exempt category or you have been granted an individual exemption.  If you are not in an exempt category you can request an individual exemption to Australia's travel restrictions.  Temporary visa holders  Temporary visa holders in Australia can depart Australia at any time, however, they will generally not be permitted to return. More information can be found at Temporary visa holders leaving Australia for a short trip overseas.  Exempt categories  You are automatically exempt from the travel restrictions and can enter Australia (without obtaining an individual exemption) if you are:  •	an Australian citizen  •	a permanent resident of Australia  •	an immediate family member of an Australian citizen or permanent resident*  •	a New Zealand citizen usually resident in Australia and their immediate family members  •	a person who has been in New Zealand or Australia for 14 days or more immediately prior to arrival by air in Australia  •	a diplomat accredited to Australia, including their immediate family members (each member of the family unit must hold a valid subclass 995 visa)  •	a person transiting Australia for 72 hours or less  •	airline crew, maritime crew including marine pilots  •	a person recruited under the Government approved Seasonal Worker Program or Pacific Labour Scheme  •	a person who holds a Business Innovation and Investment (subclass 188) visa.  * If you hold a temporary visa or do not yet hold a valid visa for Australia, you must provide proof of your relationship (such as your marriage certificate, evidence of your de-facto relationship such as shared finances or property, your birth certificate or birth certificate for your children) to the Department before you travel to Australia. Do not travel until we advise that you can. You can find out more about how to provide this information at Immediate family of Australian citizens or permanent residents or New Zealand citizens usually resident in Australia.  You need to carry evidence that you meet one of the above categories when travelling. Additional evidence may be requested.  If you are not in an exempt category you can request an individual exemption to Australia's travel restrictions.  ”    https://web.archive.org/web/20210428235543/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions

column15: As published on the Wester Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Perth and Peel post-lockdown restrictions will be significantly eased from 12.01am Saturday, May 1, with a small number of transitional measures in place until 12.01am, Saturday 8 May to see out the full 14-day incubation period.    During this period:   Schools, childcare centres, universities and TAFEs are open  Mask wearing requirements apply for all school staff and secondary students, but not primary school students or staff when teaching.  …”school, university, educational institution or childcare facility that is necessary for the normal business of the facility;” are exempt from gathering rules stipulated by the Gathering and Restricted Activities direction (No.2) which is currently in force for Perth, the Peel Region, and Rottnest Island.   Therefore have reverted to a zero code.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210502082103/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-and-peel-lockdown-restrictions-ease-further   https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114440/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/090421-Gathering-and-Activities-Directions-No2.pdf
column24: As published on the Wester Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Perth and Peel post-lockdown restrictions will be significantly eased from 12.01am Saturday, May 1, with a small number of transitional measures in place until 12.01am, Saturday 8 May to see out the full 14-day incubation period.    During this period:   All businesses, workplaces and higher education can now reopen.  Seated hospitality venues, including restaurants, cafés, food courts and bars that provide seated service only, can open at 75 per cent capacity or apply the 2sqm rule – whichever is greater  Gyms, fitness centres, dance studios, beauty salons, hairdressers can open with the 2sqm rule in place  Public venues, including, community centres, libraries, museums, galleries, recreation centres, can open with the 2sqm rule in place.  Nightclubs can open subject to the 4sqm rule, with masks mandatory  Major stadiums, including Optus Stadium, RAC Arena and HBF Park can operate at 75 per cent capacity.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502082103/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-and-peel-lockdown-restrictions-ease-further
column33: As published on the Wester Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Perth and Peel post-lockdown restrictions will be significantly eased from 12.01am Saturday, May 1, with a small number of transitional measures in place until 12.01am, Saturday 8 May to see out the full 14-day incubation period.    Outdoor gatherings can take place with up to 500 people without a COVID event plan  Seated hospitality venues, including restaurants, cafés, food courts and bars that provide seated service only, can open at 75 per cent capacity or apply the 2sqm rule – whichever is greater  Public venues, including, community centres, libraries, museums, galleries, recreation centres, can open with the 2sqm rule in place.    It was initially announced that during this period Major stadiums, including Optus Stadium, RAC Arena and HBF Park can operate at 75 per cent capacity; and that casinos and night clubs could open with square meter restrictions.  However, subsequently on the 2nd May at 11:35am the Additional Measures (Closure of Certain Premises) Directions were enacted.  These directions require all WA major stadiums, the casinos and nightclubs to close until 12:01am on 8 May 2021, provided that the West Coast Eagles v Fremantle match on 2 May 2021 may proceed without crowds.    The change came after a quarantine hotel security guard and two of his close contacts tested positive to COVID-19.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210502082103/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-and-peel-lockdown-restrictions-ease-further   https://web.archive.org/web/20210502084012/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/Additional-Measures-Closure-of-Certain-Premises-Directions.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210502084305/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-05-02/no-crowds-at-perth-stadium-for-western-derby/100110712
column42: As published on the Wester Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Perth and Peel post-lockdown restrictions will be significantly eased from 12.01am Saturday, May 1, with a small number of transitional measures in place until 12.01am, Saturday 8 May to see out the full 14-day incubation period.    Outdoor gatherings can take place with up to 500 people without a COVID event plan  30-person limit in all homes for private gatherings (indoors and outdoors) - masks are not required  Indoor weddings and funerals permitted for up to 200 guests without an exemption   https://web.archive.org/web/20210502082103/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-and-peel-lockdown-restrictions-ease-further
column103: As published on the Wester Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Perth and Peel post-lockdown restrictions will be significantly eased from 12.01am Saturday, May 1, with a small number of transitional measures in place until 12.01am, Saturday 8 May to see out the full 14-day incubation period.    Face Covering Directions (No 3) came into effect at 12.01 am on Saturday, 1 May 2021.    They stipulate that those in Perth, the Peel region, and Rottnest Island must (with some exceptions) wear a mask:   (a) in an indoor space that is not in a private home;  (b) on public transport (including, for the avoidance of doubt, a bus, ferry, train,  taxi or rideshare vehicle);  ( c) in an outdoor space where it is not possible to physically distance;  ( d) in a residential aged care facility if the person is a quarantine centre worker;  ( e) . in a private home while a wedding, wedding reception, funeral or wake is being  held at the private home and more than 30 persons are present in the private  home; or in ( or in the process of entering or leaving) a major stadium.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210502082103/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/perth-and-peel-lockdown-restrictions-ease-further   http://web.archive.org/web/20210502090558/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-04/Face-Covering-Directions-No3_0.pdf

column33: Note updated on the 17th May 2021 to address some typos included in the original note.  No substantive changes made.     Some Australian state/territory jurisdictions continue to impose capacity and square meter restrictions on public events.  However, not every Australian state and territory continues to impose capacity and square meter restrictions.  All jurisdictions require that contact tracing information be collected.  Coded as a 1T  Western Australia is a jurisdiction that currently maintains capacity and square meter restrictions on public gatherings.  The Northern Territory is a jurisdiction that does not currently have square meter or capacity restrictions on public gatherings.    Western Australia  As published on the Wester Australian Government COVID-19 website, The Perth and Peel post-lockdown restrictions will be significantly eased from 12.01am Saturday, May 1, with a small number of transitional measures in place until 12.01am, Saturday 8 May to see out the full 14-day incubation period.    Events with over 500 people subject to 2sqm rule up to a maximum of 10,000 people and are required to have an approved COVID Event Plan  Seated hospitality venues, including restaurants, cafes, food courts and bars that provide seated service only can open at 75 per cent capacity or apply the 2sqm rule – whichever is greater  2sqm rule applies to all other unseated hospitality venues, except nightclubs  Gyms, fitness centres, dance studios, beauty salons and hairdressers can open with the 2sqm rule in place  Public venues, including community centres, libraries, museums, galleries and recreation centres can open with the 2sqm rule  Certain indoor and outdoor entertainment venues with fixed seating (theatres, cinemas, concert halls, comedy lounges and performing arts centres) can operate at 100 per cent capacity  Places of worship can operate at 100 per cent capacity    This is up to date as of the 7th May 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210507114523/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do     The Northern Territory  According to the Northern Territory Government COVID-19 website, public events with more than 500 people must have an approved COVID safe plan.  The COVID safe plan includes collecting contact tracing information, consideration to encouraging social distancing, and consideration to cleaning and providing sanitation facilities.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210507115147/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business/guidelines-for-events-and-gatherings
column69: The Victorian Government Covid-19 website states that   “Victoria’s Chief Health Officer has declared that the Perth metropolitan area and the Peel region in Western Australia will be designated orange zones under Victoria’s travel permit system from 12.01am Wednesday 28 April. column24: Western Australia:  On the 6th of May 2021, Mark McGowan premier of Western Australia released a press statement announcing "The easing of restrictions for Perth and Peel will proceed as planned from 12.01am Saturday, May 8. Following three COVID-19 cases detected in Perth over the weekend, the Chief Health Officer has advised Perth and Peel will need to see through the full 14-day incubation period, from the last case detected, which ends 12.01am Saturday, May 15, 2021."  In part, this announcement included the relaxation of previous workplace closing requirements for stadiums, night clubs, and casinos.  "The following transitional restrictions are set to take effect from 12.01am Saturday, May 8:…  The casino gaming floor can reopen subject to the two square metre rule;  Nightclubs can open subject to the two square metre rule;  100-person limit in all homes, for private indoor or outdoor gatherings;  Major stadiums, including Optus Stadium, RAC Arena and HBF Park can operate at 75 per cent capacity, with masks mandatory.  “  http://web.archive.org/web/20210517111820/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/05/Restrictions-set-to-significantly-ease-from-12-01am-Saturday-May-8.aspx    Therefore, revert to a 1T code.  Some, but not all, other Australian state and territory jurisdictions have requirements in place that meet threshold for a 1 code.  The Northern Territory is provided as an example of a jurisdiction that does not meet threshold for a 1 code on this indicator.      The Northern Territory  Businesses in the Northern Territory must lodge a COVID-19 Safety Plan.   According to the COVID-19 Directions (No. 56) 2020: Safety Measures for Places, Businesses, Activities, Services and Premises – COVID-19 Safety Plans must include: details of the primary contact person/person responsible for making sure COVID-19 safety requiements are followed; details of sanitation facilities; details of signage displayed at the business to remind staff and vistors of covid safe practices; to document how where possible social distancing is encouraged.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517122741/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0012/951879/cho-directions-no56-safety-measures-places-businesses-activities-services-premises.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517122424/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business
