column103: Western Australia:  On the 6th of May 2021, Mark McGowan premier of Western Australia released a press statement announcing "The easing of restrictions for Perth and Peel will proceed as planned from 12.01am Saturday, May 8. Following three COVID-19 cases detected in Perth over the weekend, the Chief Health Officer has advised Perth and Peel will need to see through the full 14-day incubation period, from the last case detected, which ends 12.01am Saturday, May 15, 2021."  In part, this announcement included the relaxation of mask wearing requirements.  "The following transitional restrictions are set to take effect from 12.01am Saturday, May 8:  Masks are not required except on public transport, in hospitals, aged care and disabilities facilities, and at stadiums or events with more than 1,000 people"  Western Australia's mask wearing policy no longer meets requirements for a 3 code and instead meets requirement for a 2 code.  http://web.archive.org/web/20210517111820/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/05/Restrictions-set-to-significantly-ease-from-12-01am-Saturday-May-8.aspx    New South Wales:   The NSW Premier released a media statement on the 6th May 2021 announcing the introduction of new restrictions for the Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains.  This announcement was in response to the discovery of community transmission of COVID-19 from and unknown source.   In part the NSW Government COVID-19 website states “The following measures will be effective from midday today, 6 May 2021 (enforceable from 5pm) until 12:01am Monday, 10 May for the Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains)”  “A face mask (fitted face covering) is mandatory in the following indoor settings:  •	shopping (retail, supermarkets and shopping centres)  •	public and community transport  •	indoor areas of NSW airports, including passenger waiting areas   •	during a flight, when the plane is    o	landing at or taking off from the airport or   o	flying in NSW airspace.   •	indoor entertainment (including cinemas and theatres)  •	places of public worship (unless you are a person who is integral to the conduct of the religious service and are singing or chanting)  •	hair and beauty premises  •	tattoo parlours  •	massage parlours  •	betting agencies  •	gaming areas in licensed premises  •	visiting residential aged care facilities.  Face masks are also mandatory for all staff in  •	hospitality venues (for staff who have a customer-facing role).  Patrons at hospitality venues and kiosks do not need to wear a face mask (unless you are in an area of the venue used primarily for gaming, including a gaming lounge).”  This remained up to date on the 8th of May 2021.  As facemasks are not required in ALL public places (for example patrons of hospitality venues are not required to wear masks) New South Wales does not reach threshold to be coded at 3 level.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210507095539/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/greater-sydney  https://web.archive.org/web/20210507100126/https://www.nsw.gov.au/media-releases/covid-19-restrictions-update-6-may    New South Wales and Western Australia currently have the most stringent policies regarding mask wearing.  However, every other Australian state and territory also mandates the wearing of facemasks at airports and on domestic flights (at a minimum).  Therefore, a 2G code is given.   Updated archived links are provided below to sources for the remaining Australian states and territories to their mask wearing requirements.      Victoria:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114504/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20%28No%2021%29.pdf   Queensland:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114545/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/mandatory-masks   Tasmania:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114757/http://www.gazette.tas.gov.au/editions/2021/february_2021/22061_-_Gazette_17_February_2021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114925/https://www.fedcourt.gov.au/covid19/legislation/tas   Northern Territory:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517115020/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0009/965628/cho-directions-no1-2021-wearing-face-masks-in-aircraft-and-airports.pdf   http://web.archive.org/web/20210517114953/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions   Australian Capital Territory:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517115218/https://www.legislation.act.gov.au/ni/2021-38/
column115: Western Australia  VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES (STAY AT HOME AND CLOSURE (PERTH, PEEL AND ROTTNEST)) DIRECTIONS (No 3) came into effect at 12.01 am on 8 May 2021 and continued to be in effect until 12.01 am on 15 May 2021.   In a modest easing of restrictions, a resident of an aged care facility may now have up to four visitors per day.  End of life circumstance are exempt from the one visitor per day restriction.  This easing was in response to no recent community transmission of COVID-19.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210511033531/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/070521-Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Stay-at-Home-and-Closure-Perth-Peel-and-Rottnest-Directions-No4.pdf     Western Australia no longer meets criteria for a level 3 code.  Some, but not all, other Australian states and territories continue to implement narrow required policy protections of the elderly in response to COVID-19.  For example, below is included a description Victoria’s continuing required narrow policy restrictions.  New South Wales currently has no required restrictions.  Instead NSW Residential aged care facilities are required to consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in their visitor and access policies and requirements.  Therefore code changed to 2T.     Victoria:   As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, “From 11:59pm on Friday 9 April 2021, Victoria has further relaxed its COVIDSafe settings.”…” There are no limits on the purpose of visits, the number of visitors or the length of the visit to care facilities.”…”You must wear a face mask when visiting care facilities. “Care facilities must comply with the two square metre rule that limits the number of people that can be in a shared communal space at the same time. Density limits do not apply in bedrooms of aged care facilities…  You will not be permitted to enter a care facility if you are a worker or visitor and you:   are a diagnosed person or close contact under the Diagnosed Persons and Close Contacts Directions;   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, arrived in Australia from overseas (other than on a quarantine-free flight from an overseas location designated by the Australian Government as a green travel zone);   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, had known contact with a confirmed case (except in the course of your employment while wearing the appropriate level of personal protective equipment in the circumstances);    have SARS-CoV-2 Symptoms; or   if you are a visitor – have been tested for COVID-19 and are awaiting your test results.  ”  This policy is up to date as of the 17th May 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517095721/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/visiting-care-facilities   New South Wales  In NSW Residential aged care facilities are required to consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in their visitor and access policies and requirements. Individual operators may choose to implement their own decisions about access restrictions and screening requirements, having regard to the advice of the Chief Health Officer.  On the 10th May 2021, the NSW Government announced in a press release on the NSW COVID-19 government website that the advice for facilities Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains) was that visitors were recommended to be limited to 2 people per day.  This advice was a continuation of the previous advice issued on the 6th May 2021.  The policy was put in place as part of a suite of restrictions responding to the discovery of community transmission of COVID-19 from and unknown source.    On the 17th May, as published on the NSW Government COVD-19 website, the Chief Health Officer relaxed her advice for residential aged care facilities in NSW.  The relaxation of advice was in response to there being “no further local transmission of COVID-19 in NSW,”.   “Visitors  Visitors must not enter the facility at all if they:  have been to any close or casual contact locations in NSW or Interstate places of high concern  have COVID-19 symptoms - fever (37. 5°C or higher) or symptoms of COVID-19  are a close contact of a person with confirmed COVID-19 and are within their self-isolation period  live in a household with a person who is currently self-isolating  are waiting for a COVID-19 test result  From 1 June to 30 September 2021 visitors should not enter an aged care facility if they have not received a dose of the 2021 influenza vaccine, unless they meet the criteria under the exceptional and special circumstances.    Residents  Residents who are attending outings and excursions should ensure that:  avoid crowded indoor areas, such as shopping centres and restaurants  the event complies with the current Public Health Gathering Restriction Order 2021”    This remains up to date as of the 17th May 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210517102526/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517101832/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/pages/20210509_00.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210507121312/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/racf-latest-advice.aspx

column42: New South Wales:   On the 9th May 2021 the new South Wales Government published a COVID-19 update on the NSW Government website.  This announcement represented a modest relaxation of the mask wearing rules in NSW.  “To safeguard the community and reduce the risk of further transmission, the NSW Government has extended by one week the temporary COVID-safe measures (except for one change in regard to retail) which were initially put in place until 12:01am Monday 10 May for the Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains)….  The following measures will now be in effect until 12:01 am Monday 17 May.  Visitors to households are limited to 20 guests, including children.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210517112850/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/Pages/20210509_00.aspx  Coded a 3T in line with the most stringent restrictions in place in greater Sydney.
column103: Western Australia:  On the 6th of May 2021, Mark McGowan premier of Western Australia released a press statement announcing "The easing of restrictions for Perth and Peel will proceed as planned from 12.01am Saturday, May 8. Following three COVID-19 cases detected in Perth over the weekend, the Chief Health Officer has advised Perth and Peel will need to see through the full 14-day incubation period, from the last case detected, which ends 12.01am Saturday, May 15, 2021."  In part, this announcement included the relaxation of mask wearing requirements.  "The following transitional restrictions are set to take effect from 12.01am Saturday, May 8:  Masks are not required except on public transport, in hospitals, aged care and disabilities facilities, and at stadiums or events with more than 1,000 people"  Western Australia's mask wearing policy no longer meets requirements for a 3 code and instead meets requirement for a 2 code.  http://web.archive.org/web/20210517111820/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/05/Restrictions-set-to-significantly-ease-from-12-01am-Saturday-May-8.aspx     New South Wales:   On the 9th May 2021 the new South Wales Government published a COVID-19 update on the NSW Government website.  This announcement represented a modest relaxation of the mask wearing rules in NSW.  “To safeguard the community and reduce the risk of further transmission, the NSW Government has extended by one week the temporary COVID-safe measures (except for one change in regard to retail) which were initially put in place until 12:01am Monday 10 May for the Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains).  The one change to the existing orders removes the requirement for retail customers to wear masks, however public-facing staff in retail outlets must continue to do so.  The following measures will now be in effect until 12:01 am Monday 17 May.  Visitors to households are limited to 20 guests, including children.  Masks are compulsory on public transport and in public indoor venues such as theatres and gaming areas. Masks should also be worn in hospitals, and aged care facilities.  Masks are not required in gyms or other similar facilities like indoor pools or dance studios.”  NSW meets the requirement for a 2 code.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517112850/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/Pages/20210509_00.aspx     New South Wales and Western Australia currently have the most stringent policies regarding mask wearing.  However, every other Australian state and territory also mandates the wearing of facemasks at airports and on domestic flights (at a minimum).  Therefore, a 2G code is given.   Updated archived links are provided below to sources for the remaining Australian states and territories to their mask wearing requirements.      Victoria:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114504/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20%28No%2021%29.pdf   Queensland:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114545/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/mandatory-masks   Tasmania:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114757/http://www.gazette.tas.gov.au/editions/2021/february_2021/22061_-_Gazette_17_February_2021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517114925/https://www.fedcourt.gov.au/covid19/legislation/tas   Northern Territory:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517115020/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0009/965628/cho-directions-no1-2021-wearing-face-masks-in-aircraft-and-airports.pdf   http://web.archive.org/web/20210517114953/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/chief-health-officer-directions   Australian Capital Territory:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517115218/https://www.legislation.act.gov.au/ni/2021-38/

column69: *Updated note on 21/05/2021 to fix typo. No substantive change made.     Coded a 1T in recognition of the COVID-19 related intra state restrictions in place limiting travel to Remote Aboriginal Communities in Western Australia, with exceptions for providing essential services and supplies; going to work or school there; entering for family or cultural reasons; delivering a community program or activity; entering in an emergency.  No other jurisdictions have policy in place that meets threshold for coding at C7.       Victoria  The Victorian Government Covid-19 website states that   “The Chief Health Officer has declared that the Perth metropolitan area and the Peel region in Western Australia will be designated green zones under Victoria’s ‘traffic light’ travel permit system, effective 11:59pm, 10 May 2021.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210507123858/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/victorian-travel-permit-system    New South Wales:   As published on The New South Wales COVID-19 Government website, the NSW Government has issued a “A COVID-19 concerns notice has been issued for Greater Melbourne local government areas. Follow the rules for interstate travellers.” This applies to individuals “entering NSW on or after 6am Wednesday 12 May who has been in Greater Melbourne in the previous 14 days”.  It requires anyone who has entered of plans to enter NSW from Greater Melbourne to complete a “NSW entry declaration form” only.  However, the NSW Government has further identified places of high concern with Greater Melbourne.  These are specific venues on particular dates and times.  People who have entered NSW after visiting a place of high concern within the last 14 days must self-isolate until it has been 14 days since they visited the place of high concern.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517135301/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/interstate-hotspots   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517135104/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf     Queensland:   According to the Queensland Government COVID-19 website states that “You can enter Queensland from any Australian state or territory. There are no COVID-19 hotspots in Australia.    The Queensland Declared Hotspots Direction shows that there have been no hotspots declared by the Queensland Government since 27th April 2021.   The Queensland Border Declaration Pass system is not active. You do not need a border pass to enter Queensland.”  There are current contact tracing alerts and requirements to quarantine or self-isolate if you have visited specific venues at specific times in NSW and VIC.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517132159/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/contact-tracing   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517132704/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/border-closing  https://web.archive.org/web/20210507122910/https://www.health.qld.gov.au/system-governance/legislation/cho-public-health-directions-under-expanded-public-health-act-powers/declared-hotspots-direction     Western Australia:   The Western Australian Government COVID-19 website shows that since the 5th May 2021 and current at 17th May 2021 all Australian stated and territories have been considered “very low risk”.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517140903/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/covid-communications/covid-19-coronavirus-controlled-border   However, as also published on the Western Australian Government COVID-19 website and enforced by the Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions (No. 3) there remains travel restrictions in place to protect residents of Remote Aboriginal Communities in WA.    “Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  providing essential services and supplies  going to work or school there  entering for family or cultural reasons  delivering a community program or activity  entering in an emergency.  These people cannot enter a community if they have COVID-19 or symptoms of COVID-19, or if they are waiting for a test result or have been in contact with someone who has COVID-19.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210517141218/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-remote-aboriginal-communities-travel   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517141440/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2020-08/Remote%20Aboriginal%20Communities%20Directions%20%28No.%203%29.pdf     Tasmania:   As published on the Tasmanian government’s COVID-19 website, all Australian stated and territories are considered ‘low risk’.  However, there are some specific travel alerts for high-risk premises (at specific times and dates) in NSW and VIC.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210517140344/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517140729/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert     Northern Territory:   The Northern Territory COVID-19 Government website shows that there have been no Norther Territory declared hotspots since 2.30 pm on 27 April 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210517133101/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19/former-declared-hotspots     Australian Capital Territory:   The Australian Capital Territory considered some specific venues (at specific times and dates) in NSW and Victoria to be place of concern.  “Persons who have been to a COVID-19 place of concern in the specified timeframe must follow the public health guidance outlined in the COVID-19 Area of Concern Notice. This may require a person to take a specific action such as get tested for COVID-19 and isolate until receiving a negative result, or to monitor for symptoms. The specific actions may differ if you are identified as a casual contact or a close contact.”  However, the Australian Capital Territory does not have any currently identified areas of concern.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210517133609/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0005/1748417/COVID-19-Areas-of-Concern-notice_17-May-2021-No-40.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210517133734/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/updates/covid-19-areas-of-concern
column81: The Australian Federal Budget was released on the 11th May 2021.    The Australian National Government authored a budget summary document titled: Securing Australia’s Recovery Supporting Australians through COVID-19.    In part, this document they outline New and extended COVID-19 economic support measures provided in the 2021-2022 budget.    These are the measures that the Government has identified as being in response to COVID-19 and are listed here:   An additional $7.8 billion in the hands of more than 10 million individuals by retaining the low and middle income tax offset in 2021-22  $20.7 billion to extend the temporary full expensing and loss carry-back measures by 12 months  $15.2 billion in additional commitments to infrastructure projects across Australia  $780 million to extend the HomeBuilder construction commencement period from 6 to 18 months  $1.2 billion package to support Australia’s tourism and aviation sectors   A new SME Recovery Loan Scheme building on the SME Guarantee Scheme which has assisted around 45,000 businesses to date [No dollar figure provided in the summary]  Around $300 million to support Australia’s creative and entertainment sector”  This sums to 38.18 billion AUD.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210521084457/https://budget.gov.au/2021-22/content/download/glossy_covid19.pdf
column83: The Australian Federal Budget was released on the 11th May 2021.    The Australian Official Development Assistance budget summary 2021-22 outlined   “Additional COVID-19 support in 2021-22  $162.6 million for safe and effective vaccines in the Pacific and Southeast Asia  $100 million to help address the impacts of COVID-19 in the Pacific and Timor-Leste  $56 million to support economic recovery in Southeast Asia  $16.7 million for emergency medical supplies to India”  This is a total of 335.3 million AUD.  It is not clear yet whether this aid will be in the form of cash or donations of goods and services.  As such, a dollar value has not been entered for this date.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210521073417/https://www.dfat.gov.au/about-us/corporate/portfolio-budget-statements/pbs-2021-22-aid-budget-summary
column92: The Australian Federal Budget was released on the 11th May 2021.    The Official Government Budget Overview document states, in part, that “The Government is providing $1.5 billion in this Budget to extend a range of health responses, to keep Australians protected alongside the roll-out of COVID-19 vaccines. This includes continued funding for telehealth services, which has seen over 56 million services delivered to 13.7 million patients since March 2020, COVID-19 testing and support to prevent outbreaks in remote communities.  Over 2.5 million vaccine doses have already been administered.  In this Budget, the Government is investing a further $1.9 billion in the COVID-19 vaccination roll-out. This is to protect Australians from COVID-19 and facilitate Australia’s social and economic recovery.  Funding for the roll-out of COVID-19 vaccines includes administering vaccines, managing distribution and logistics, recording and monitoring data, communications and supporting the states and territories.  In this Budget, the Government has committed to purchasing additional vaccine doses. This expands our vaccine portfolio to around 170 million doses.”  This is a total $3.4 billion AUD health spend related to COVID-19.  $1.5 billion AUD is recoded at H4: Emergency investment in healthcare and $1.9 billion AUD is recoded at H5: Investment in vaccines.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210521050942/https://budget.gov.au/2021-22/content/overview.htm  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521081136/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2021/05/budget-2021-22-portfolio-budget-statements-budget-2021-22-health-portfolio-budget-statements.pdf

column24: Western Australia:  On the 13th of May 2021, Mark McGowan premier of Western Australia released a press statement announcing " From 12.01am Saturday, May 15, Perth and Peel will return to pre-lockdown life."  The Western Australian COVID-19 Government Website further states that “There will be no capacity limits for weddings and funerals;  Major stadiums including Optus Stadium, RAC Arena, HBF Park and HBF Stadium – main arena can operate at 100 per cent capacity;  Visitor limits for patients in hospital, aged care or disability facilities will be lifted;”  However, also according to the Western Australian COVID-19 government website, some modest capacity restrictions do remain in place for some workplaces.  This includes the 2 square meter rule for the following venues: Nightclubs; Unseated bars; Unseated events/festivals (music, beer, wine, cultural etc.); Galleries; Museums; Libraries; Sport and recreation facilities including gyms, recreation centres, group fitness classes and indoor and outdoor swimming pools; Amusement parks; Wildlife parks and Perth Zoo; Function centres; Community, recreation or youth centres or facilities, including but not limited to community halls, sporting clubs, Returned and Services League facilities, and Police and Community Youth Centres; Adult entertainment premises, including but not limited to strip clubs, brothels and sex on premises venues; Casino and gambling venues; Arcades; Funeral parlours; Events that involve unfixed seating (e.g. BYO chair concerts).  The following hospitality venues are subject to a 75% capacity rule: Restaurants; Hotels (excluding bottleshops); Cafes; Food courts; Bars with seated service only.  While modest, these square meter rules and capacity limitations continue to meet threshold for a 1 code.     http://web.archive.org/web/20210517121029/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/05/Pre-lockdown-life-to-resume-in-full-from-12-01am-this-Saturday.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517121130/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/pre-lockdown-life-resumes-perth-and-peel-regions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210517124028/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do     Some, but not all, other Australian state and territory jurisdictions have requirements in place that meet threshold for a 1 code.  The Northern Territory is provided as an example of a jurisdiction that does not meet threshold for a 1 code on this indicator.      The Northern Territory  Businesses in the Northern Territory must lodge a COVID-19 Safety Plan.   According to the COVID-19 Directions (No. 56) 2020: Safety Measures for Places, Businesses, Activities, Services and Premises – COVID-19 Safety Plans must include: details of the primary contact person/person responsible for making sure COVID-19 safety requiements are followed; details of sanitation facilities; details of signage displayed at the business to remind staff and vistors of covid safe practices; to document how where possible social distancing is encouraged.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517122741/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0012/951879/cho-directions-no56-safety-measures-places-businesses-activities-services-premises.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517122424/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business
column33: NOTE: Amended code on the 28th May 2021, after receiving advice from the Oxford School of Government project team on the evening of 27th May 2021.  It was decided that square meter density rules ought to be considered analogous to explicit % capacity rules.  As documented at archived links on original note, a number of Australian states and territories continue to implement square meter density rules for public events.    One jurisdiction that clearly does not have any current square meter rules (and no explicit % capacity rules) is the Northern Territory.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528092239/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business/guidelines-for-events-and-gatherings   Therefore code changed from 0 to 1T.       Western Australia:  On the 13th of May 2021, Mark McGowan premier of Western Australia released a press statement announcing " From 12.01am Saturday, May 15, Perth and Peel will return to pre-lockdown life."  The Western Australian COVID-19 Government Website further states that “There will be no capacity limits for weddings and funerals;  Major stadiums including Optus Stadium, RAC Arena, HBF Park and HBF Stadium – main arena can operate at 100 per cent capacity;  Visitor limits for patients in hospital, aged care or disability facilities will be lifted;”  However, also according to the Western Australian COVID-19 government website, some modest capacity restrictions do remain in place for some workplaces.  This includes the 2 square meter rule for the following venues: Nightclubs; Unseated bars; Unseated events/festivals (music, beer, wine, cultural etc.); Galleries; Museums; Libraries; Sport and recreation facilities including gyms, recreation centres, group fitness classes and indoor and outdoor swimming pools; Amusement parks; Wildlife parks and Perth Zoo; Function centres; Community, recreation or youth centres or facilities, including but not limited to community halls, sporting clubs, Returned and Services League facilities, and Police and Community Youth Centres; Adult entertainment premises, including but not limited to strip clubs, brothels and sex on premises venues; Casino and gambling venues; Arcades; Funeral parlours; Events that involve unfixed seating (e.g. BYO chair concerts).    The following hospitality venues are subject to a 75% capacity rule: Restaurants; Hotels (excluding bottleshops); Cafes; Food courts; Bars with seated service only.  Further WA’s major venues (Optus Stadium, RAC Arena, HBF Stadium – Main Arena, HBF Park) and Indoor and outdoor fixed seating entertainment spaces including: auditoriums and amphitheatres; theatres; concert halls; cinemas; comedy lounges; performing arts centres; - are all 100% capacity (with no square meter rule).    Decided that WA does not meet criteria to be coded at a 1 for C3 as primary public event venues are at 100% capacity.   http://web.archive.org/web/20210517121029/https://www.mediastatements.wa.gov.au/Pages/McGowan/2021/05/Pre-lockdown-life-to-resume-in-full-from-12-01am-this-Saturday.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517121130/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/pre-lockdown-life-resumes-perth-and-peel-regions  https://web.archive.org/web/20210517124028/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do    No other Australian States or Territories have percentage capacity rules for public event venues.  The norm is to operate at a one person per two squared meter rule).  South Australia has a 3 people per 4 square meter rule.  Decided, according to the interpretation guide, this does not reach threshold for a 1 code under C3.   Therefore code changed to a 0. column115: Western Australia  A media announcement published on the 13th May 2021 state that “The Perth and Peel regions return to pre-lockdown life from 12.01am Saturday 15 May, following the latest public health advice.  No additional cases have been detected in the Perth and Peel regions since the detection of the last community case on Saturday, May 1.” In part, this means that “Visitor limits for patients in hospital, aged care or disability facilities will be lifted”  The VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES (STAY AT HOME AND CLOSURE (PERTH, PEEL AND ROTTNEST)) DIRECTIONS (No 3) were repealed at 12.01 am on 15 May 2021.  As such, the VISITORS TO RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE FACILITIES DIRECTIONS (NO 7) is now apply to the Perth and Peel Regions (as well as the rest of Western Australia).  This direction stipulates that a person not enter a residential aged care facility if they: have been outside of Australian within the last 14 days; have been in contact with a positive case within the last 14 days, have any symptoms consistent with COVID-19; or have not had an updated influenza vaccination (with some exceptions).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210514212351/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/pre-lockdown-life-resumes-perth-and-peel-regions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210423080044/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/240221-Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Directions-No7.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210511033531/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/070521-Visitors-to-Residential-Aged-Care-Facilities-Stay-at-Home-and-Closure-Perth-Peel-and-Rottnest-Directions-No4.pdf  Like Western Australia, some, but not all, other Australian states and territories continue to implement narrow required policy protections of the elderly in response to COVID-19.  For example, below is included a description Victoria’s continuing required narrow policy restrictions.  New South Wales currently has no required restrictions.  Instead NSW Residential aged care facilities are required to consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in their visitor and access policies and requirements.  Therefore code remains at 2T.     Victoria:   As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, “From 11:59pm on Friday 9 April 2021, Victoria has further relaxed its COVIDSafe settings.”…” There are no limits on the purpose of visits, the number of visitors or the length of the visit to care facilities.”…”You must wear a face mask when visiting care facilities. “Care facilities must comply with the two square metre rule that limits the number of people that can be in a shared communal space at the same time. Density limits do not apply in bedrooms of aged care facilities…  You will not be permitted to enter a care facility if you are a worker or visitor and you:   are a diagnosed person or close contact under the Diagnosed Persons and Close Contacts Directions;   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, arrived in Australia from overseas (other than on a quarantine-free flight from an overseas location designated by the Australian Government as a green travel zone);   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, had known contact with a confirmed case (except in the course of your employment while wearing the appropriate level of personal protective equipment in the circumstances);    have SARS-CoV-2 Symptoms; or   if you are a visitor – have been tested for COVID-19 and are awaiting your test results.  ”  This policy is up to date as of the 17th May 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517095721/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/visiting-care-facilities   New South Wales  In NSW Residential aged care facilities are required to consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in their visitor and access policies and requirements. Individual operators may choose to implement their own decisions about access restrictions and screening requirements, having regard to the advice of the Chief Health Officer.  On the 10th May 2021, the NSW Government announced in a press release on the NSW COVID-19 government website that the advice for facilities Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains) was that visitors were recommended to be limited to 2 people per day.  This advice was a continuation of the previous advice issued on the 6th May 2021.  The policy was put in place as part of a suite of restrictions responding to the discovery of community transmission of COVID-19 from and unknown source.    On the 17th May, as published on the NSW Government COVD-19 website, the Chief Health Officer relaxed her advice for residential aged care facilities in NSW.  The relaxation of advice was in response to there being “no further local transmission of COVID-19 in NSW,”.   “Visitors  Visitors must not enter the facility at all if they:  have been to any close or casual contact locations in NSW or Interstate places of high concern  have COVID-19 symptoms - fever (37. 5°C or higher) or symptoms of COVID-19  are a close contact of a person with confirmed COVID-19 and are within their self-isolation period  live in a household with a person who is currently self-isolating  are waiting for a COVID-19 test result  From 1 June to 30 September 2021 visitors should not enter an aged care facility if they have not received a dose of the 2021 influenza vaccine, unless they meet the criteria under the exceptional and special circumstances.    Residents  Residents who are attending outings and excursions should ensure that:  avoid crowded indoor areas, such as shopping centres and restaurants  the event complies with the current Public Health Gathering Restriction Order 2021”    This remains up to date as of the 17th May 2021.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210517102526/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517101832/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/pages/20210509_00.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210507121312/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/racf-latest-advice.aspx

column15: School closures are the responsibility of the states. During the period of 18 – 21 May 2021, no states had active school closure policies in place. This continues all states being open since the WA lockdown ended on 1 May. column24: Since WA relaxed lockdown restrictions in the Perth and Peel areas on 8 May 2021, the strictest restrictions have been around density requirements in states. In WA there has been a 1 person per 2m2 restriction or 75% capacity.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210517124028/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do  Elsewhere in Australia the norm is still density limits of 2 sqm per person e.g. Victoria:  https://web.archive.org/web/20210511223714/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/how-we-live  Queensland:  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521012147/https://www.business.qld.gov.au/running-business/covid-19-restrictions/current  NSW:  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521012333/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/square-metres-rules
column33: NOTE: Amended code on the 28th May 2021, after receiving advice from the Oxford School of Government project team on the evening of 27th May 2021.  It was decided that square meter density rules ought to be considered analogous to explicit % capacity rules.  As documented at archived links on original note, a number of Australian states and territories continue to implement square meter density rules for public events.    One jurisdiction that clearly does not have any current square meter rules (and no explicit % capacity rules) is the Northern Territory.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528092239/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business/guidelines-for-events-and-gatherings   Therefore code changed from 0 to 1T.           ORIGINAL NOTE.   Creator	Patrick Rehill  Timestamp	21 May 2021 06:27:55  While many states have density restrictions in place for event venues, the only state with a percentage capacity restriction (which could be counted as a 1 in this scale) in place is WA (see C2), however restaurants, bars etc. probably fall into C2 rather than C3. Events venues like theatres, cinemas, comedy clubs, sports grounds are all operating at 100% capacity.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210517124028/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do    WA and every other jurisdiction bar NT has density restrictions (1 person per 2sqm) in place, however, these do not fall within the definition of a 1 on this variable in the coding guide.

column51: COVID has changed the way public transport operates in all states. In two states, these policies would be classified as a 1 under the interpretation that an increase in services in order to decrease the density of passengers counts as a 1.  Victoria: Victoria is encouraging travel outside of peak times by discounting fares in order to reduce the density of passengers. https://web.archive.org/web/20210521014823/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/more-choices-victorian-train-passengers  NSW: NSW has capacity restrictions of 75% on all public transport. This is a relaxation of earlier policies from 19 April 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521015031/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance
column60: There were no stay-at-home requirements in Australia during this period. The last stay-at-home order was in Perth and Peel in WA which ended on 27 April 2021  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502073200/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/end-of-lockdown-perth-and-peel
column69: ***Recoded from 2T to 1T.     Previous coding note:   NT has had in place mandatory quarantine for those who have visited exposure sites in Victoria and SA that have been active since May 17.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521020359/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19  Tasmania has barred travel by those who have been at an exposure site in Victoria within the past 14 days.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521020444/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert  Queensland has implemented mandatory quarantine for those who have been to a Victorian exposure site.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521020611/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/contact-tracing
column74: Australia has had in place very restrictive border policies since the beginning of the crisis. The only people who may travel to Australia are citizens, permanent residents or their immediate family members. In these cases there is mandatory 14 day hotel quarantine. The one exception both these rules is travel from New Zealand which is governed as if it is domestic travel (i.e. it is the role of the states to regulate travel unless the federal government chooses to abolish its travel bubble agreement).  Previously, travel from India was banned even for classes of people otherwise allowed to return. However, since May 15, returns from India have been allowed.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521023454/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions
column77: Since the abolition of the JobKeeper program on 28 March, Australia has not had an income support policy in place. This kind of relief would generally be the purview of the federal government and no states have had income support policies of their own during the pandemic either.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521024323/https://www.ato.gov.au/general/jobkeeper-payment/
column79: Australia’s financial relief programs (governed primarily by the states) are no longer in effect since Victoria, NSW and WA removed their eviction moratorium programs in March 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521024522/https://www.sbs.com.au/news/a-wave-of-homelessness-could-be-about-to-hit-australia-as-rules-preventing-evictions-end  Changes to tenancies were the main government-enforced contract relief as government-encouraged load relief was implemented by banks.
column86: From previous entry - The Australian Government continues to run a coordinated public health campaign.  This includes use of TV advertisements, Social media, formal government website information, and press conference and media statements.    Updated archived link to the Official Australian Government Public health information website for COVID-19:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210405093811/https://www.health.gov.au/   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409050019/https://www.health.gov.au/news/health-alerts/novel-coronavirus-2019-ncov-health-alert
column88: Australia’s states have controlled testing, however there has been a largely unform policy since the beginning of the pandemic that anyone with symptoms can be tested with some nuances that differ between states (though practically all would be a 2 on this scale). Asymptomatic testing has not been encouraged outside of meeting asymptomatic testing obligations like for hotel quarantine workers or close contacts of confirmed cases.  New South Wales:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409044225/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/how-to-protect-yourself-and-others/clinics   Victoria:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409044805/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/taking-test-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409044540/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/getting-tested   Queensland:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409045004/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/stay-informed/testing-and-fever-clinics   Western Australia:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409045136/https://healthywa.wa.gov.au/Articles/A_E/Coronavirus/COVID19-testing   Tasmania:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409045301/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/keeping-yourself-safe/testing-for-covid19   Northern Territory:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409045650/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/symptoms-testing   Australian Capital Territory:   https://web.archive.org/web/20210409045813/https://www.health.act.gov.au/health-professionals/chief-health-officer-alerts/covid-19-testing-information
column90: States have been in control of contact tracing with some assistance from the federal government. It is difficult to know what the capacity of states to contact trace has been as there have been few community cases in Australia recently. However, the fact that states have been able to tamp down those few outbreaks quite quickly. States have centralised check-in apps to their own systems and in most states contact tracing is now mandatory in venues.  A review of Australia’s contact tracing capabilities in November 2020 found that while there is room for improvement, the capabilities of Australia’s contact tracing system have greatly improved since the start of the pandemic. Events since the release of that report have given no reason to doubt that we have comprehensive capabilities.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210416224611/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/national-contact-tracing-review-national-contact-tracing-review.pdf
column103: The most stringent jurisdiction in Australia for masks was previously Greater Sydney in NSW where masks were mandatory on public transport and for customer-facing staff until May 17 as part of a ramp up of restrictions in response to community transmission of COVID from a man in hotel quarantine to a couple in Sydney through a hypothesised missing link case. NSW has now returned to its previous restriction settings across the state.  Masks remain mandatory in all Australian airports under an agreement between states which therefore corresponds to a coding of 2G.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521055709/https://www.infrastructure.gov.au/aviation/domestic-passenger-journey-protocol.aspx
column106: Australia entered stage 2a of vaccination on May 3 and this extended to GPs on May 17. This phase allows any person over the age of 50 to receive a vaccine. This is in addition to the previously allowed groups:  Quarantine and border workers, including:  •	staff at entry points to the country (such as sea ports and land borders)  •	staff working in quarantine facilities, including those employed under Commonwealth, state or private agreements, and    •	Commonwealth employees (including Defence personnel) who are identified as having the potential to encounter returning travellers as part of their work.  Quarantine, border and front line health care workers will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility.	   Frontline health care worker sub-groups for prioritisation  •	frontline staff in facilities or services such as hospital emergency departments,COVID-19 and respiratory wards, Intensive Care Units and High-dependency Units  •	laboratory staff handling potentially infectious material  •	ambulance and paramedics service  •	GP respiratory clinics  •	COVID-19 testing facilities  Frontline health care workers who fit under one of these sub groups will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility. 	   Aged care and disability care staff, including:  •	nursing and personal care staff  •	allied health professionals who routinely provide care  •	kitchen, cleaning, laundry, garden and office staff	   Aged care and disability care residents	  Healthcare workers currently employed and not included in Phase 1a	   Household contacts of quarantine and border workers	   Critical and high risk workers who are currently employed including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing	   Essential outbound travellers with a travel exemption	   Elderly people aged 80 years and over	   Elderly people aged 70 years and over	   Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 50 years and over	   Adults with an underlying medical condition or significant disability    People aged 50 years and over	   Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 16-49 years	   Other critical and high risk workers	    https://web.archive.org/web/20210521060032/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

Density limits do not apply in bedrooms of aged care facilities…  You will not be permitted to enter a care facility if you are a worker or visitor and you:   are a diagnosed person or close contact under the Diagnosed Persons and Close Contacts Directions;   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, arrived in Australia from overseas (other than on a quarantine-free flight from an overseas location designated by the Australian Government as a green travel zone);   have, during the 14 days prior to entry, had known contact with a confirmed case (except in the course of your employment while wearing the appropriate level of personal protective equipment in the circumstances);    have SARS-CoV-2 Symptoms; or   if you are a visitor – have been tested for COVID-19 and are awaiting your test results.  ”  This policy is up to date as of the 17th May 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517095721/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/visiting-care-facilities   New South Wales  In NSW Residential aged care facilities are required to consider the advice of the Chief Health Officer in their visitor and access policies and requirements. Individual operators may choose to implement their own decisions about access restrictions and screening requirements, having regard to the advice of the Chief Health Officer.  On the 10th May 2021, the NSW Government announced in a press release on the NSW COVID-19 government website that the advice for facilities Greater Sydney region (including Wollongong, Central Coast and Blue Mountains) was that visitors were recommended to be limited to 2 people per day.  This advice was a continuation of the previous advice issued on the 6th May 2021.  The policy was put in place as part of a suite of restrictions responding to the discovery of community transmission of COVID-19 from and unknown source.    On the 17th May, as published on the NSW Government COVD-19 website, the Chief Health Officer relaxed her advice for residential aged care facilities in NSW.  The relaxation of advice was in response to there being “no further local transmission of COVID-19 in NSW,”.   “Visitors  Visitors must not enter the facility at all if they:  have been to any close or casual contact locations in NSW or Interstate places of high concern  have COVID-19 symptoms - fever (37. 5°C or higher) or symptoms of COVID-19  are a close contact of a person with confirmed COVID-19 and are within their self-isolation period  live in a household with a person who is currently self-isolating  are waiting for a COVID-19 test result  From 1 June to 30 September 2021 visitors should not enter an aged care facility if they have not received a dose of the 2021 influenza vaccine, unless they meet the criteria under the exceptional and special circumstances.    Residents  Residents who are attending outings and excursions should ensure that:  avoid crowded indoor areas, such as shopping centres and restaurants  the event complies with the current Public Health Gathering Restriction Order 2021”    https://web.archive.org/web/20210517102526/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210517101832/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/pages/20210509_00.aspx   https://web.archive.org/web/20210507121312/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/racf-latest-advice.aspx

column51: COVID has changed the way public transport operates in all states. In two states, these policies would be classified as a 1 under the interpretation that an increase in services in order to decrease the density of passengers counts as a 1.  Victoria: Victoria is encouraging travel outside of peak times by discounting fares in order to reduce the density of passengers. https://web.archive.org/web/20210521014823/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/more-choices-victorian-train-passengers  NSW: NSW has capacity restrictions of 75% on all public transport. column60: There were no stay-at-home requirements in Australia during this period. The last stay-at-home order was in Perth and Peel in WA which ended on 27 April 2021  https://web.archive.org/web/20210502073200/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/end-of-lockdown-perth-and-peel
column69: Originally coded as a 1G by Patrick Rehill Timestamp.  25 May 2021 01:40:25. Original noted below.  Code changed from a 1G to a 1T by Lucy Ellen 18th June 2021.  Changed because, after reviewing news article linked in the archived URL, all policy responses are limited to people that have visited specific exposure sites/venues.  None of the policies linked within the article prevent travel for all residents of any state or city.  These policies are best captured under H3_Contact tracing.     However, coded a 1T in recognition of the COVID-19 related intra state restrictions in place limiting travel to Remote Aboriginal Communities in Western Australia, with exceptions for providing essential services and supplies; going to work or school there; entering for family or cultural reasons; delivering a community program or activity; entering in an emergency.  No other jurisdictions have policy in place that meets threshold for coding at C7.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210526161843/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-remote-aboriginal-communities-travel   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618120538/https://www.wa.gov.au/sites/default/files/2020-08/Remote%20Aboriginal%20Communities%20Directions%20%28No.%203%29.pdf       Original note “After community transmission in Victoria, all other jurisdictions have introduced some kind of restriction on those who have visited the exposure sites identified by Victorian contact tracers. This has not extended to a ban on travel from Victoria or Greater Melbourne though so far. Those who have been to exposure sites must quarantine and in the case of Tasmania they will be flat out banned from entering the state. This media link summarises the immediate responses by states. https://web.archive.org/web/20210525011014/https://www.9news.com.au/national/coronavirus-how-states-and-territories-are-reacting-to-victorias-new-covid19-cases-nsw-queensland-sa-wa-nt-tasmania-travel-quarantine-restrictions/82272f87-e551-41a8-93cd-54af29b564b9”
column74: Australia has had in place very restrictive border policies since the beginning of the crisis. The only people who may travel to Australia are citizens, permanent residents or their immediate family members. In these cases there is mandatory 14 day hotel quarantine. The one exception both these rules is travel from New Zealand which is governed as if it is domestic travel (i.e. it is the role of the states to regulate travel unless the federal government chooses to abolish its travel bubble agreement).  Previously, travel from India was banned even for classes of people otherwise allowed to return. However, since May 15, returns from India have been allowed.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521023454/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions
column77: Since the abolition of the JobKeeper program on 28 March, Australia has not had an income support policy in place. This kind of relief would generally be the purview of the federal government and no states have had income support policies of their own during the pandemic either.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521024323/https://www.ato.gov.au/general/jobkeeper-payment/
column79: Australia’s financial relief programs (governed primarily by the states) are no longer in effect since Victoria, NSW and WA removed their eviction moratorium programs in March 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521024522/https://www.sbs.com.au/news/a-wave-of-homelessness-could-be-about-to-hit-australia-as-rules-preventing-evictions-end  Changes to tenancies were the main government-enforced contract relief as government-encouraged load relief was implemented by banks.

column90: States have been in control of contact tracing with some assistance from the federal government. It is difficult to know what the capacity of states to contact trace has been as there have been few community cases in Australia recently. However, the fact that states have been able to tamp down those few outbreaks quite quickly. States have centralised check-in apps to their own systems and in most states contact tracing is now mandatory in venues.  A review of Australia’s contact tracing capabilities in November 2020 found that while there is room for improvement, the capabilities of Australia’s contact tracing system have greatly improved since the start of the pandemic. Events since the release of that report have given no reason to doubt that we have comprehensive capabilities.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210416224611/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/national-contact-tracing-review-national-contact-tracing-review.pdf
column103: After community transmission occurred in Victoria, new masking rules were introduced there on May 25 which makes wearing a mask mandatory in all indoor settings.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210525010125if_/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/additional-covidsafe-measures-keep-victorians-safe    Masking is still mandatory in all Australian airports per the decision of national cabinet.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210521055709/https://www.infrastructure.gov.au/aviation/domestic-passenger-journey-protocol.aspx    Even though the Victorian policy is vastly more stringent than the airport policy elsewhere in Australia, the fact that masking outdoors is not required means that both policies fall under a 2 and so this has been coded as 2G.
column106: Australia entered stage 2a of vaccination on May 3 and this extended to GPs on May 17. This phase allows any person over the age of 50 to receive a vaccine. This is in addition to the previously allowed groups:  Quarantine and border workers, including:  •	staff at entry points to the country (such as sea ports and land borders)  •	staff working in quarantine facilities, including those employed under Commonwealth, state or private agreements, and    •	Commonwealth employees (including Defence personnel) who are identified as having the potential to encounter returning travellers as part of their work.  Quarantine, border and front line health care workers will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility.	   Frontline health care worker sub-groups for prioritisation  •	frontline staff in facilities or services such as hospital emergency departments,COVID-19 and respiratory wards, Intensive Care Units and High-dependency Units  •	laboratory staff handling potentially infectious material  •	ambulance and paramedics service  •	GP respiratory clinics  •	COVID-19 testing facilities  Frontline health care workers who fit under one of these sub groups will need to provide proof of occupation to demonstrate their eligibility. 	   Aged care and disability care staff, including:  •	nursing and personal care staff  •	allied health professionals who routinely provide care  •	kitchen, cleaning, laundry, garden and office staff	   Aged care and disability care residents	  Healthcare workers currently employed and not included in Phase 1a	   Household contacts of quarantine and border workers	   Critical and high risk workers who are currently employed including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing	   Essential outbound travellers with a travel exemption	   Elderly people aged 80 years and over	   Elderly people aged 70 years and over	   Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 50 years and over	   Adults with an underlying medical condition or significant disability    People aged 50 years and over	   Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 16-49 years	   Other critical and high risk workers	    https://web.archive.org/web/20210521060032/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system. From 11:59 pm on Thursday, 3 June 2021, the travel from home distance was extended for 10kms for those living in metropolitan Melbourne. column15: The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions apply across the whole of the state of Victoria and are enforced by the Restricted Activity Directions (Victoria) (No 17).  In part, these restrictions prohibit in person attendance at schools and universities.  There are exceptions for children where all of their primary carer/parent (s) are essential workers or where children have been identified as vulnerable (for example reside in the care of the state or are at risk of family violence).      Victoria has the most stringent COVID-19 policy restrictions in Australia, relating to the operation of schools and universities.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082507/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Restricted%20Activity%20Directions%20%28No%2017%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf

These new restrictions apply across the whole of the state of Victoria and are enforced by the Restricted Activity Directions (Victoria) (No 17).  In part, these restrictions require most workplaces must close for in person trading.  See 4th link for a list of ‘authorised providers and workers list’.  The vast majority of those on the ‘authorised providers and workers list’ would be considered essential workers and work places.  Therefore coded at a level 3.    However, it is noted that there are two work places/categories for services that are allow to continue which may not be considered essential.
