column33: The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions apply across the whole of the state of Victoria and are enforced by the Restricted Activity Directions (Victoria) (No 17) and the Stay at Home Directions (Victoria)  These directions require all Entertainment and Leisure venues to be closed and also prohibit public gatherings.    Victoria has the most stringent COVID-19 restrictions in Australia relating to the cancelling of public events.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082507/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Restricted%20Activity%20Directions%20%28No%2017%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528085737/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Victoria%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528083855/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/210527%20-%207%20Day%20Circuit%20Breaker%20Restrictions.pdf
column42: The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions apply across the whole of the state of Victoria and are enforced by the Restricted Activity Directions (Victoria) (No 17) and the Stay at Home Directions (Victoria)  These directions prohibits social gatherings with anyone who does not live in your household. .    There are three exceptions to this restrictions:   1)	You can exercise outside for 2 hours a day with the members of your household or one other friend or family member as long as you both do not travel more than 5km.  2)	If you live alone or are a single parent you can create a ‘single social bubble’ by nominating one other person to be a part of your bubble.  You can have the person you form a bubble with visit your home.   Your nominated person can be from a household or share house, and you are also able to visit them in their home, but only when they are alone. If you and / or your single social buddy are single parents, your children can be a part of your bubble.  3)	Victorians may also leave home to visit their intimate partner.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082507/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Restricted%20Activity%20Directions%20%28No%2017%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528085737/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Victoria%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528083855/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/210527%20-%207%20Day%20Circuit%20Breaker%20Restrictions.pdf
column51: Previous note remains up to date:   COVID has changed the way public transport operates in all states. In two states, these policies would be classified as a 1 under the interpretation that an increase in services in order to decrease the density of passengers counts as a 1.  Victoria: Victoria is encouraging travel outside of peak times by discounting fares in order to reduce the density of passengers. https://web.archive.org/web/20210521014823/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/more-choices-victorian-train-passengers  NSW: NSW has capacity restrictions of 75% on all public transport. This is a relaxation of earlier policies from 19 April 2021.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521015031/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidanced
column60: The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions apply across the whole of the state of Victoria and are enforced by the Restricted Activity Directions (Victoria) (No 17) and the Stay at Home Directions (Victoria)  These directions mean that Victorian’s cannot leave their home except for the following reasons:   shopping for necessary goods and services  care and caregiving, or to get a COVID-19 test  exercise   authorised work and permitted study  to get a COVID-19 vaccination.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082507/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Restricted%20Activity%20Directions%20%28No%2017%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528085737/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Victoria%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528083855/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/210527%20-%207%20Day%20Circuit%20Breaker%20Restrictions.pdf
column69: NOTE: originally coded 2G by Lucy Ellen 04 Jun 2021 11:49:07.  Decision made to recode from a 2G to a 2T.  Reason for change of code is that while all states/territory governments have introduced restrictions on travellers from Victoria or Greater Melbourne; it is only residents from Victoria or Greater Melbourne that have their ability to travel curtailed.  Australian residents outside of Victoria have no restrictions on movement around Australia.  With the exception of some closures to remote communities in Western Australia.       “From the 28th May 2021 all Australian states and territories had travel restrictions in place for residents from all or part of Victoria.      Western Australia  The WA Government COVID-19 website states that “Based on the latest updated advice from the WA Chief Health Officer, Victoria has transitioned from ‘very low risk’ to ‘medium risk’ under WA’s controlled interstate border, effective from 10.00am, Thursday, 27 May 2021.  Travel from Victoria into WA will no longer be permitted, unless you are exempt.”  This is up to date as of the 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210531123423/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/wa-introduces-hard-border-victoria  https://web.archive.org/web/20210604102408/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-travel-wa     New South Wales:   The NSW Government declared the whole Victoria to be an area of concern from 4pm on 27th May 2021.  Travelers from Victoria must self quarantine for 14 days since last being in Victoria.   This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604102722/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/border-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604103051/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf       Australian Capital Territory  The ACT government COVID-19 government states that “All travellers:  If you leave Victoria after 11:59pm on Thursday 27 May 2021 and enter the ACT you must stay at home unless:  to undertake essential work or study if they cannot work from home or remotely  to shop for essentials like groceries, medicine and necessary supplies  to attend to medical or health care needs including compassionate requirements, and looking after the vulnerable  to get a COVID-19 vaccine if you are not isolation or quarantine and have a scheduled appointment  to exercise outdoors, limited to one hour per day  for essential animal welfare purposes (e.g. to feed pets or livestock that live elsewhere)  to attend usual childcare arrangements, where parents or guardians need to undertake essential work or study.  If you are aged 12 years and over you must wear a mask if leaving your premises for any of these essential purposes. Mask wearing is not required if you are undertaking vigorous exercise outdoors.  The stay-at-home requirement will be in place until at least 11.59pm on 10 June 2021.”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604103802/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/community/travel/victoria2   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604103911/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/new-stay-at-home-requirement-in-act-if-travelling-from-victoria     Northern Territory  The Northern Territory Government COVID-19 website states that “Anyone travelling to the Northern Territory from a COVID-19 hotspot which currently includes Greater Melbourne and Bendigo Local Government Areas must undertake 14 days of mandatory, supervised quarantine at the Alice Springs or Howard Springs quarantine facilities from 12:01am on 27 May 2021.”  On the 2nd June 2021, the NT Government further announced that “In addition to Greater Melbourne and the Local Government Area of Bendigo, the Acting Chief Health Officer has declared the remainder of the state of Victoria to be a COVID-19 hotspot for the purposes of travel to the Northern Territory effective 12 noon on 3 June 2021.”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604104900/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-05-26-greater-melbourne-and-bendigo-declared-covid-19-hotspots   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604104557/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604105207/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-06-02-covid-19-update-state-of-victoria-declared-a-hotspot     Tasmania  The Tasmanian government COVID-19 website states “Victoria has been declared a high-risk area from 2pm Thursday 27 May by Tasmanian Public Health authorities.  The declaration was made in response to Victoria announcing a state-wide lockdown and additional cases of COVID-19 being detected in the community.  Anyone intending to travel to Tasmania after 2pm Thursday May 27, who has been in Victoria in the 14 days prior to their travel, will not be able to enter Tasmania unless approved as an Essential Traveller. Quarantine and other requirements are in place for Essential Travellers”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604105517/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604105937/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert       South Australia  The South Australian government COVID-19 website states that “If you have been in Regional Victoria (excluding Greater Melbourne and the City of Greater Bendigo) after 7.00 pm 26 May and entered South Australia before 7.45 pm 28 May 2021, you must:  get a COVID-19 test as soon as possible and quarantine until you receive a negative result.  get tested again on day 5 and day 13 after arrival. There is no requirement to quarantine after receiving a negative result in your first test, unless you have symptoms.  ”    Additionally, “From 7.45pm 28 May 2021, if you have been in Victoria in the past 14 days you are not permitted to enter South Australia.  Entry is permitted for returning residents, people relocating to South Australia, and people fleeing domestic violence, however, on arrival you must:  quarantine for 14 days  get a COVID-19 test as soon as possible  get tested on day 5 and day 13 of quarantine  wear a face mask (covering mouth and nose) at any time you come into contact with the public for 14 day quarantine period (i.e. when seeking testing).”    This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604110704/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/restrictions-and-responsibilities/travel-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604111830/https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/conditions/infectious+diseases/covid-19/testing+and+tracing/contact+tracing     Queensland  The Queensland government COVID-19 website states that “If you are entering Queensland and have been in Victoria in the last 14 days  From 1am AEST Friday 28 May all of Victoria will be declared a COVID-19 hotspot.  If you have been in a COVID-19 hotspot in the last 14 days or since the start date identified for the hotspot (whichever is shorter) you will not be allowed to enter Queensland unless you are a Queensland resident, except for a limited range of people who can enter for essential purposes.  The Border Restrictions Direction outlines the restrictions that apply to anyone who has been to a COVID-19 hotspot.  If you are in Queensland and have been to Victoria in the last 14 days  Greater Melbourne lockdown requirements continue to apply from 11.59pm AEST Thursday 3 June 2021  If you arrived in Queensland after 1am AEST Thursday 27 May and before 12.59am AEST Friday 28 May and have been in Greater Melbourne in the last 14 days, you must:  continue to follow the stay at home requirements. The Places of Concern Direction outlines these requirements  get tested if you develop COVID-19 symptoms.  monitor the list of interstate exposure venues for 14 days after entering Queensland  If you arrived in Queensland before 1am AEST Thursday 27 May and had been to Greater Melbourne in the last 14 days, you do not need to follow the stay at home requirements. However, you should:  get tested if you develop COVID-19 symptoms.  monitor the list of interstate exposure venues for 14 days after entering Queensland”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604112413/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604112235/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/border-closing     Victoria:   The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions applied across the whole of the state of Victoria and were enforced, in part, by the and the Stay at Home Directions (Victoria).  These directions required, with some exceptions, that Victorian residents not travel more than 5kms from their homes.    From 11:59 pm on Thursday, 3 June 2021, the travel from home distance was extended for 10kms for those living in metropolitan Melbourne.  The permitted travel distance from home restriction was abolished for Regional Victoria.  However, residents of Regional Victoria can attend work in metropolitan Melbourne only if you are an authorised worker or work for an authorised provider.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604113455/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings     https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082507/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Restricted%20Activity%20Directions%20%28No%2017%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528085737/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Victoria%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528083855/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/210527%20-%207%20Day%20Circuit%20Breaker%20Restrictions.pdf”
column103: The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions apply across the whole of the state of Victoria and, in part, are enforced by the Stay at Home Directions (Victoria).   These directions mean that Victorian’s over 12 years of age cannot leave their home without wearing a mask.  There are some exemptions for safety reasons or if it is necessary not to wear a mask in order to carry out your authorised work role.  Furthermore, if you are alone in a confined space wearing a mask is not required.  For example: an essential worker working alone in a closed office would not need to wear a mask.   Coded a 3 level as there are some exceptions and also those outside of the home, but alone in an enclosed space are not required to wear masks.       https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528021246/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Victoria%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf    https://web.archive.org/web/20210528083855/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/210527%20-%207%20Day%20Circuit%20Breaker%20Restrictions.pdf

These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system. Victoria has the most stringent COVID-19 policy restrictions in Australia, relating to protecting the elderly. column129: COVID-19 vaccines will be voluntary and free  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia. Vaccination providers cannot charge you for the COVID-19 vaccine or your appointments to receive the vaccine.    If your vaccination provider charges for any costs associated with the administration of the COVID-19 vaccination (including booking fees), you should:    contact us by emailing provider.benefits.integrity@health.gov.au,  or call the Provider Benefits Integrity Hotline on 1800 314 808 (9am to 5pm AEST weekdays).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423124712/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19

column81: On 30 May 2021, “The Victorian Government has launched the $250.7 million Circuit Breaker Business Support Package to assist small to medium businesses and sole traders, under the circuit breaker restrictions announced on 27 May 2021.  The package will support businesses unable to operate due to these necessary public health measures, including targeted support for the events industry.  With safety nets such as JobKeeper no longer available, this package provides vital support to businesses doing their part to help keep Victorians safe throughout the circuit breaker action.”  https://web.archive.org/web/20210604074819/https://business.vic.gov.au/grants-and-programs/circuit-breaker-business-support-package

On 2 June 2021, the Victorian Government announced the “Building on the $250.7 million Circuit Breaker Business Support Package announced Sunday 30 May, a new allocation of up to $209.3 million will flow to small and medium-sized businesses, including sole traders, increasing the total support package to $460 million.  The Business Costs Assistance Program Round Two grants for eligible businesses located in metropolitan Melbourne will be doubled from $2,500 to $5,000.  Regional businesses unable to open due to the ongoing restrictions, such as nightclubs and amusement parks, will also be eligible for the extended payment.  Metropolitan businesses that will have restrictions eased from Friday 3 June 2021 will not be eligible for the extended payment.  The Licensed Hospitality Venue Fund 2021 grants for businesses in metropolitan Melbourne with eligible liquor licences and food certificates will be doubled from $3,500 to $7,000.”  The additional 209.3 million AUD announce is entered here for 2 June 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210604074819/https://business.vic.gov.au/grants-and-programs/circuit-breaker-business-support-package

column15: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, from 11:59 pm on Thursday 3 June 2021, there were some modest easing of restrictions impacting residents of Greater Melbourne and a more significant easing of restrictions impacting regional Victoria.  These changes are in response to continued community transmission within Greater Melbourne and no community transmission within regional Victoria within the last week.   In metropolitan Melbourne schools and universities:   “TAFE and university study must be done from home.  Schools and face-to-face learning are open for  students enrolled in Years 11 and 12, and any other students enrolled in Unit 3/4 VCE studies or the final year of a VCAL qualification or IB qualification  vulnerable children  children of authorised workers who cannot work from home or who are working from home and cannot provide supervision.”    In regional Victoria:   “Schools can resume face-to-face learning for all year levels.  Universities and higher education facilities can resume face-to-face learning with a density quotient of 1 person per 4 sqm.”      Greater Melbourne has the most restrictive government policy response to COVID-19 (including policies specifically related to school/university closures).  In Greater Melbourne some school students (in additional to children of authorized workers and vulnerable children) are able to attend face to face schooling.  Therefore, code changed to a 2.        https://web.archive.org/web/20210604010742/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604011649/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-2
column24: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, from 11:59 pm on Thursday 3 June 2021, there were some modest easing of restrictions impacting residents of Greater Melbourne and a more significant easing of restrictions impacting regional Victoria.  These changes are in response to continued community transmission within Greater Melbourne and no community transmission within regional Victoria within the last week.   In Greater Melbourne employees:   “You must work from home, unless you are an authorised worker , or work for an authorised provider who cannot work from home. If you can work from home, you must work from home.  You can only travel to regional Victoria for work if you are an authorised worker, or work for an authorised provider, and your place of work is in regional Victoria. The restrictions that apply to you in metropolitan Melbourne follow you to regional Victoria. For example, you must wear a mask indoors and outdoors when in regional Victoria.  Authorised outdoor workers will be able to resume work and must maintain physical distancing of at least 1.5 meters from others..”  The vast majority of those on the ‘authorised providers and workers list’ would be considered essential workers and work places.  The updated list is slightly expanded compared to the list most recently previously released list.  Nonetheless, continued level 3 code.    However, it is noted that there are some work places/categories for services that are allow to continue which may not be considered essential.  For example, non-essential general retail is allowed to open for click and collect orders only and hospitality is allowed to remain open for take away orders only.  See link 3 for the list of current authorized workers and workplaces.      In regional Victoria:   “If you can work or study from home, you should continue to do so.  Workplaces like offices can increase to 50 per cent or 20 people, whichever is greater.  You can attend work in metropolitan Melbourne only if you are an authorized worker or work for an authorized provider.”      Greater Melbourne has the most restrictive government policy response to COVID-19 (including policies specifically related to workplace closures).  Therefore, continue 3 code.        https://web.archive.org/web/20210604010742/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604011649/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-2   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604013145/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/authorised-provider-and-authorised-worker-list
column33: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, from 11:59 pm on Thursday 3 June 2021, there were some modest easing of restrictions impacting residents of Greater Melbourne and a more significant easing of restrictions impacting regional Victoria.  These changes are in response to continued community transmission within Greater Melbourne and no community transmission within regional Victoria within the last week.     Within Greater Melbourne these extended restrictions are enforced by the Restricted Activity Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne) and the Stay at Home Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne).    These directions require all Entertainment and physical recreation venues to be closed (with exception that approved sporting events may go ahead but with no spectators) and also prohibit public gatherings.    Greater Melbourne has the most restrictive government policy response to COVID-19 (including policies specifically related to public events). column69: Most recent note relevant and up to date as of the 4th June 2021    Western Australia  The WA Government COVID-19 website states that “Based on the latest updated advice from the WA Chief Health Officer, Victoria has transitioned from ‘very low risk’ to ‘medium risk’ under WA’s controlled interstate border, effective from 10.00am, Thursday, 27 May 2021.  Travel from Victoria into WA will no longer be permitted, unless you are exempt.”  This is up to date as of the 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210531123423/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/wa-introduces-hard-border-victoria  https://web.archive.org/web/20210604102408/https://www.wa.gov.au/organisation/department-of-the-premier-and-cabinet/covid-19-coronavirus-travel-wa     New South Wales:   The NSW Government declared the whole Victoria to be an area of concern from 4pm on 27th May 2021.  Travelers from Victoria must self quarantine for 14 days since last being in Victoria.   This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604102722/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/border-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604103051/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Documents/interstate-concerns-notice.pdf       Australian Capital Territory  The ACT government COVID-19 government states that “All travellers:  If you leave Victoria after 11:59pm on Thursday 27 May 2021 and enter the ACT you must stay at home unless:  to undertake essential work or study if they cannot work from home or remotely  to shop for essentials like groceries, medicine and necessary supplies  to attend to medical or health care needs including compassionate requirements, and looking after the vulnerable  to get a COVID-19 vaccine if you are not isolation or quarantine and have a scheduled appointment  to exercise outdoors, limited to one hour per day  for essential animal welfare purposes (e.g. to feed pets or livestock that live elsewhere)  to attend usual childcare arrangements, where parents or guardians need to undertake essential work or study.  If you are aged 12 years and over you must wear a mask if leaving your premises for any of these essential purposes. Mask wearing is not required if you are undertaking vigorous exercise outdoors.  The stay-at-home requirement will be in place until at least 11.59pm on 10 June 2021.”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604103802/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/community/travel/victoria2   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604103911/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/news-articles/new-stay-at-home-requirement-in-act-if-travelling-from-victoria     Northern Territory  The Northern Territory Government COVID-19 website states that “Anyone travelling to the Northern Territory from a COVID-19 hotspot which currently includes Greater Melbourne and Bendigo Local Government Areas must undertake 14 days of mandatory, supervised quarantine at the Alice Springs or Howard Springs quarantine facilities from 12:01am on 27 May 2021.”  On the 2nd June 2021, the NT Government further announced that “In addition to Greater Melbourne and the Local Government Area of Bendigo, the Acting Chief Health Officer has declared the remainder of the state of Victoria to be a COVID-19 hotspot for the purposes of travel to the Northern Territory effective 12 noon on 3 June 2021.”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604104900/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-05-26-greater-melbourne-and-bendigo-declared-covid-19-hotspots   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604104557/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine/hotspots-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604105207/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-06-02-covid-19-update-state-of-victoria-declared-a-hotspot     Tasmania  The Tasmanian government COVID-19 website states “Victoria has been declared a high-risk area from 2pm Thursday 27 May by Tasmanian Public Health authorities.  The declaration was made in response to Victoria announcing a state-wide lockdown and additional cases of COVID-19 being detected in the community.  Anyone intending to travel to Tasmania after 2pm Thursday May 27, who has been in Victoria in the 14 days prior to their travel, will not be able to enter Tasmania unless approved as an Essential Traveller. Quarantine and other requirements are in place for Essential Travellers”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604105517/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604105937/https://coronavirus.tas.gov.au/travellers-and-visitors/coming-to-tasmania/travel-alert       South Australia  The South Australian government COVID-19 website states that “If you have been in Regional Victoria (excluding Greater Melbourne and the City of Greater Bendigo) after 7.00 pm 26 May and entered South Australia before 7.45 pm 28 May 2021, you must:  get a COVID-19 test as soon as possible and quarantine until you receive a negative result.  get tested again on day 5 and day 13 after arrival. There is no requirement to quarantine after receiving a negative result in your first test, unless you have symptoms.  ”    Additionally, “From 7.45pm 28 May 2021, if you have been in Victoria in the past 14 days you are not permitted to enter South Australia.  Entry is permitted for returning residents, people relocating to South Australia, and people fleeing domestic violence, however, on arrival you must:  quarantine for 14 days  get a COVID-19 test as soon as possible  get tested on day 5 and day 13 of quarantine  wear a face mask (covering mouth and nose) at any time you come into contact with the public for 14 day quarantine period (i.e. when seeking testing).”    This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604110704/https://www.covid-19.sa.gov.au/restrictions-and-responsibilities/travel-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604111830/https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/conditions/infectious+diseases/covid-19/testing+and+tracing/contact+tracing     Queensland  The Queensland government COVID-19 website states that “If you are entering Queensland and have been in Victoria in the last 14 days  From 1am AEST Friday 28 May all of Victoria will be declared a COVID-19 hotspot.  If you have been in a COVID-19 hotspot in the last 14 days or since the start date identified for the hotspot (whichever is shorter) you will not be allowed to enter Queensland unless you are a Queensland resident, except for a limited range of people who can enter for essential purposes.  The Border Restrictions Direction outlines the restrictions that apply to anyone who has been to a COVID-19 hotspot.  If you are in Queensland and have been to Victoria in the last 14 days  Greater Melbourne lockdown requirements continue to apply from 11.59pm AEST Thursday 3 June 2021  If you arrived in Queensland after 1am AEST Thursday 27 May and before 12.59am AEST Friday 28 May and have been in Greater Melbourne in the last 14 days, you must:  continue to follow the stay at home requirements. The Places of Concern Direction outlines these requirements  get tested if you develop COVID-19 symptoms.  monitor the list of interstate exposure venues for 14 days after entering Queensland  If you arrived in Queensland before 1am AEST Thursday 27 May and had been to Greater Melbourne in the last 14 days, you do not need to follow the stay at home requirements. However, you should:  get tested if you develop COVID-19 symptoms.  monitor the list of interstate exposure venues for 14 days after entering Queensland”  This remains up to date as of 4th June 2021.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604112413/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/hotspots-covid-19   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604112235/https://www.covid19.qld.gov.au/government-actions/border-closing     Victoria:   The acting Premier of Victoria, James Merlino, released a statement on the 27 May 2021 stating that “On the advice of our public health team, Victoria will move to circuit breaker restrictions from 11:59pm tonight. These settings will remain in place until 11:59pm on 3 June – but if we can end that sooner, we will.”  This tightening of restrictions is in response to growing community transmission of the B1.617.1 variant which escaped from Australia’s hotel quarantine system.   These new restrictions applied across the whole of the state of Victoria and were enforced, in part, by the and the Stay at Home Directions (Victoria).  These directions required, with some exceptions, that Victorian residents not travel more than 5kms from their homes.    From 11:59 pm on Thursday, 3 June 2021, the travel from home distance was extended for 10kms for those living in metropolitan Melbourne.  The permitted travel distance from home restriction was abolished for Regional Victoria.  However, residents of Regional Victoria can attend work in metropolitan Melbourne only if you are an authorised worker or work for an authorised provider.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604113455/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings     https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082124/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/covid-19-chief-health-officer-update   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082326/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-1   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528082507/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Restricted%20Activity%20Directions%20%28No%2017%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528085737/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202105/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Victoria%29%20-%2027%20May%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210528083855/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-05/210527%20-%207%20Day%20Circuit%20Breaker%20Restrictions.pdf”
column77: On 3 June 2021, the Australian Prime Minister Scott Morrison released a media statement announcing a new temporary COVID Disaster Payment.  Australians who have had their hours of work and income significantly affected due to state lockdowns will be eligible.    “The rapid support will be paid weekly to those workers who reside or work in a Commonwealth declared hotspot and are therefore unable to attend work and earn an income as a result of state imposed health restrictions, which last for greater than one week.  Eligible recipients will receive up to $500 per week for losing 20 hours or more of work, and $325 per week for losing under 20 hours. They must not have liquid assets of more than $10,000.  The payment will be made in respect of the second and any subsequent weeks of restrictions.”  This announcement appears to have been made in response to the extension of stay at home orders in Greater Melbourne.    Those eligible for this new payment will be able to apply for it from the 8th June 2021.  Therefore , while the announcement is noted here, a zero code remains to date.      The payment will complement existing payments including the Pandemic Leave Disaster Payment.  The Pandemic Leave Disaster Payment is only available to those who “can't earn an income because you must self-isolate or quarantine, or are caring for someone with COVID-19.” Ordinarily this payment is for a maximum 14 day period only.  It does not meet the threshold for a 1 code according to the interpretation guide.  As such, a zero code has been coded and continues.     Since the abolition of the JobKeeper program on 28 March, Australia has not had an income support policy in place. This kind of relief would generally be the purview of the federal government and no states have had income support policies of their own during the pandemic either.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521024323/https://www.ato.gov.au/general/jobkeeper-payment/   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604082420/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/news/covid-19-disaster-payment-people-affected-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604082327/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/temporary-australian-government-assistance-workers   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604082852/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/services/centrelink/pandemic-leave-disaster-payment

column103: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, from 11:59 pm on Thursday 3 June 2021, there were some modest easing of restrictions impacting residents of Greater Melbourne and a more significant easing of restrictions impacting regional Victoria.  These changes are in response to continued community transmission within Greater Melbourne and no community transmission within regional Victoria within the last week.     Within Greater Melbourne these extended restrictions are enforced, in part, Stay at Home Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne).    The directions mean that for those living in Metropolitan Melbourne:   “People aged 12 years or older must wear a fitted face mask when leaving home, unless they have a lawful reason not to. For example:  if you have a medical condition, including problems with your breathing, a serious condition of the face, a disability or a mental health condition.  if you are in the car alone or with someone from your household  if you are doing strenuous exercise such as running or jogging, but you must still carry one..”    There are some exemptions for safety reasons or if it is necessary not to wear a mask in order to carry out your authorised work role.  Furthermore, if you are alone in a confined space wearing a mask is not required.  For example: an essential worker working alone in a closed office would not need to wear a mask.       Within Regional Victoria extended restrictions are, in part, enforced by the Stay Safe Directions (Regional Victoria) (No 3)  For Regional Victoria:   “Face masks must be carried at all times and must be worn indoors. You do not need to wear a mask in your own home or if a lawful exception applies.  Face masks are required outdoors where physical distancing of 1.5m cannot be maintained, for example, at a busy open-air market.”    Greater Melbourne has the most restrictive government policy response to COVID-19 (including with respect to wearing face masks).  Therefore, continue 3 code.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210604023611/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20%28Regional%20Victoria%29%20%28No%203%29%20-%203%20June%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604010742/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604021312/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Stay%20at%20Home%20Directions%20%28Metropolitan%20Melbourne%29%20-%203%20June%202021%20%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604011649/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-2
column106: While there have been some regional accelerations of the vaccine roll-out, Nationwide Australia is current at Phase 2a of the Vaccine rollout.  This means that :People aged 50 years and over; Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 16-49 years; Other critical and high risk workers are all eligible for vaccination.  As well as those who were previously eligible at earlier stages of the roll out.     Full details for current phased National vaccine roll-out plan can be seen at the first archived URL.   Australia is currently using the AstraZeneca and pfizer vaccines.  There is not individual cost of receiving a COVID-19 vaccination.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604085743/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604090219/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19

Victoria has the most restrictive government policy response to COVID-19 (including with respect to protecting older adults). This kind of relief would generally be the purview of the federal government and no states have had income support policies of their own during the pandemic either.  https://web.archive.org/web/20210521024323/https://www.ato.gov.au/general/jobkeeper-payment/   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604082420/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/news/covid-19-disaster-payment-people-affected-restrictions   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604082327/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/temporary-australian-government-assistance-workers   https://web.archive.org/web/20210604082852/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/individuals/services/centrelink/pandemic-leave-disaster-payment
column106: On the 4th June 2021 Australian Prime Minister announces that “the National Cabinet agreed to opening access to a COVID-19 vaccine to the following groups by 8 June 2021:  people aged 40-49 years not otherwise eligible;  all Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 16 to 49 years;  NDIS participants aged 16 years and over and carers aged 16 years and over of NDIS participants of any age; and  temporary visa holders aged under 50 years who are currently in Australia and have been approved for return travel to Australia through the travel exemption process.”  While different states and territories are rolling out the vaccine at differing speed, from the 8th of June 2021, the general community of people 39 years and younger are not yet eligible for a COVID-19 Vaccine in every jurisdiction across Australia.   Australia currently has access to the AstraZeneca and the Pfizer Vaccines.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618124202/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/national-cabinet-statement-040621   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618124455/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

column129: COVID-19 vaccines will be voluntary and free  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia. Vaccination providers cannot charge you for the COVID-19 vaccine or your appointments to receive the vaccine.    If your vaccination provider charges for any costs associated with the administration of the COVID-19 vaccination (including booking fees), you should:    contact us by emailing provider.benefits.integrity@health.gov.au,  or call the Provider Benefits Integrity Hotline on 1800 314 808 (9am to 5pm AEST weekdays).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423124712/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19

column15: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website on the 9th June 2021 media release Victoria’s current restrictions were to be further eased from 11.59pm Thursday 10 June.    A statement from the Acting Premier of Victoria, also published on 9th June 2021, stated that “In good news for families, schools will be able to resume face-to-face learning for all students from Friday [11th June 2021].”  The Workplace Directions (No 32) also came into effect on 11:59:00am on 12 June 2021.  Schools and higher education are open for in person teaching/learning.  Nonetheless, these directions, in part impose a density requirement of one person per 4 square meters.  This applies, to the non-classroom/teaching spaces of child care services, schools, and higher education institutions.      “The density quotient does, however, still apply to office areas including shared spaces that form part of an office area, spaces such as lunchrooms, photocopier room, principal’s office, back of reception and resource rooms. The density quotient also applies to any publicly accessible areas (including in relation to a school, non-school senior secondary providers, or childcare or early childhood service, or higher education service), and any such publicly accessible areas that are subject to the signage requirements under subclause (20).”    Therefore, changed to a 1T code.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111828/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-3   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111735/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-9-june-2021   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617114259/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Workplace-Directions-%28No-32%29-10-June-2021.pdf
column24: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website on the 9th June 2021 media release Victoria’s current restrictions were to be further eased from 11.59pm Thursday 10 June.    A statement from the Acting Premier of Victoria, also published on 9th June 2021, stated that “When it comes to work, there’ll no longer be a list [of approved workers/workplaces]– but if you can work from home, you should.”  However, the Restricted Activity Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne) (No 3) also came into effect on 11:59:00am on 12 June 2021.  These directions commence at 11:59:00pm on 10 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021.  The direction requires the closing of a range of workplaces include:   “A person who owns, controls or operates a facility in subclause (2)(g) (arcade), subclause (2)(h) (amusement park), subclause (2)(i) (casino), (2)(k) (gaming machine area), subclause (2)(l) (brothel, sex on premises venue or sexually explicit entertainment venue), subclause (2)(m) (bingo centre), subclause (2)(n) (escape room), subclause (2)(p) (karaoke facility) and subclause (2)(q) (nightclub) must not operate the areas in those facilities that are accessible to the public during the restricted activity period.”    Furthermore, indoor operation of non-ticketed and seated events in the following venues is not allowed.  In practice, these venues/workplaces are therefore also closed:   “A person who owns, controls or operates a facility in subclause (2)(a) (theatre), (2)(b) (cinema except a drive-in cinema), (2)(c) (music hall, concert hall or auditorium), (2)(d) (gallery or museum except an outdoor gallery or museum), (2)(e) (State Library), (2)(f) (arena or stadium), (2)(o) (animal facility except an outdoor animal facility) or (2)(q) (convention centre)…”  Melbourne has the most restrictive COVID-19 restrictions on workplaces in Victoria and all of Australia.  However, in Melbourne, clearly non-essential workplaces are now open for in person trade.  This include: Café, restaurant, fast food store, cafeteria, canteen, winery, food court (indoor and outdoor spaces (according to the VIC government) COVID-19 webpage.     Therefore, changed to a 2T code.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617115913/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/current-restrictions-how-we-work-metropolitan-melbourne   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111828/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-3   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111735/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-9-june-2021   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617114259/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Workplace-Directions-%28No-32%29-10-June-2021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617115928/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Restricted-Activity-Directions-%28Metropolitan-Melbourne%29-%28No-3%29-12-June-2021.pdf
column33: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website on the 9th June 2021 media release Victoria’s current restrictions were to be further eased from 11.59pm Thursday 10 June.    The Stay Safe Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne) (No 2) also came into effect on 11:59:00am on 12 June 2021.  These directions commence at 11:59:00pm on 10 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021.  These Directions allow for public out door gatherings of up to 10 people within Metropolitan Melbourne.     Outdoor fixed seated entertainment: Open up to 50% of seated capacity up to a maximum of 100 people per venue. Density quotient of 1 person per 4sqm applies.  This could include open large open air sports stadiums.      Therefore, change to a 1T code.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111828/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-3   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111735/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-9-june-2021   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617121311/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Stay-Safe-Directions-%28Metropolitan-Melbourne%29-%28No-2%29-12-June-2021-%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210610152436/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-06/20210609%20Table%20of%20Restrictions.pdf

column69: Other Australian States and Territories continue to maintain travel restrictions for those that have visited Melbourne and/or Greater Melbourne.     For example: The western Australian COVID-19 Government Website states that  "Effective 6.00pm, Friday 11 June, travel from Victoria into WA is permitted, subject to 14 days of self-quarantine and COVID-19 testing". column103: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website on the 9th June 2021 media release Victoria’s current restrictions were to be further eased from 11.59pm Thursday 10 June.    The Stay Safe Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne) (No 2) also came into effect on 11:59:00am on 12 June 2021.  These directions commence at 11:59:00pm on 10 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021.  These directions maintained the same mask wearing requirements Melbourne.  Mask must be worn indoors and outdoors at all times, unless an exemption applies.   There are some exemptions for safety reasons or if it is necessary not to wear a mask in order to carry out your authorised work role.  Furthermore, if you are alone in a confined space wearing a mask is not required.  For example: an essential worker working alone in a closed office would not need to wear a mask.    Therefore, maintain 3 code.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111828/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-3   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111735/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-9-june-2021   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617121311/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Stay-Safe-Directions-%28Metropolitan-Melbourne%29-%28No-2%29-12-June-2021-%28signed%29.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210610152436/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-06/20210609%20Table%20of%20Restrictions.pdf
column115: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website on the 9th June 2021 media release Victoria’s current restrictions were to be further eased from 11.59pm Thursday 10 June.    The The Care Facilities Directions (No. 32)also came into effect on 11:59:00am on 12 June 2021.  These directions commence at 11:59:00pm on 10 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021.  These directions maintained the previous level of restrictions regarding visitors to residential aged are facilities in the whole of Victoria.   • No visitors are permitted into aged care or other residential facility settings, except for end-of-life reasons and other excepted reasons such as essential care and support  • Non-essential contractors are restricted from entry  Victoria has the most restrictive government policy response to COVID-19 (including with respect to protecting older adults).  Therefore, continue 3T code.      https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111828/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/statement-acting-premier-3   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617111735/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-update-victoria-9-june-2021   https://web.archive.org/web/20210617125411/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Care-Facilities-Directions-%28No-32%29-10-June-2021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210610152436/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-06/20210609%20Table%20of%20Restrictions.pdf

column33: As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, and in response to reducing levels of community transmission of COVID-19, restriction for both Greater Melbourne and the rest/regional Victoria were, overall/in general, relaxed from 11:59pm on Thursday 17 June 2021.      For Melbourne The Stay Safe Directions (Metropolitan Melbourne) (No. 3) came into force at 11.59pm 17 June 2021.These directions commenced at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 24 June 2021.   These restrictions allow for Public gatherings of up to 20 people.  For example, meeting outside in a park.   For Victoria The Workplace Directions (No. 33) come into force at 11.59pm 17 June 2021 and replace the Workplace Directions (No. 32).  These directions commence at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 24 June 2021.  Under these new directions no workplaces are required to close.  However, there are a range of restrictions on the operations of different workplaces.    Clearly related to public events, For entertainment venues:  “Bars, karaoke facilities and nightclubs are open with seated service, a density requirement of 1 person per 4 sqm and no more than 50 people indoors.  Indoor seated venues such as cinemas can have 50% seated capacity and up to 75 people per venue and maximum group size of 10 people (excluding babies under 1 year). A density requirement of 1 person per 4 sqm applies to indoor non-seated areas.  Outdoor seated venues can have 50% seated capacity and up to 150 people per venue and maximum group size of 20 people (excluding babies under 1 year). A density requirement of 1 person per 4 sqm applies to outdoor non-seated areas.”  Therefore maintain 4T code.   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618132230/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Stay%20Safe%20Directions%20%28Metropolitan%20Melbourne%29%20%28No%203%29%2017%20June%202021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618131254/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-06/210616%20-%20Table%20of%20Restrictions_2.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618130810/https://www.dhhs.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/202106/Workplace%20Directions%20%28No%2033%29%20-%2017%20June%202021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210618133116/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings

column106: The remains up to date as of the 18th June 2021.   On the 4th June 2021 Australian Prime Minister announces that “the National Cabinet agreed to opening access to a COVID-19 vaccine to the following groups by 8 June 2021:  people aged 40-49 years not otherwise eligible;  all Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 16 to 49 years;  NDIS participants aged 16 years and over and carers aged 16 years and over of NDIS participants of any age; and  temporary visa holders aged under 50 years who are currently in Australia and have been approved for return travel to Australia through the travel exemption process.”  While different states and territories are rolling out the vaccine at differing speed, from the 8th of June 2021, the general community of people 39 years and younger are not yet eligible for a COVID-19 Vaccine in every jurisdiction across Australia. column15: Originally coded by Lucy Ellen 18 Jun 2021 13:11:36. Originally Coded at a 1T.  Original note below.   Recoded as a 2T in light of information found at the archived link: https://web.archive.org/web/20210618131254/https://www.premier.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2021-06/210616%20-%20Table%20of%20Restrictions_2.pdf   States for both regional Victoria and Metropolitan Sydney: “Higher education and training: If you can study from home, you should continue to study from home”    Original Note:   “As published on the Victorian Government COVID-19 website, and in response to reducing levels of community transmission of COVID-19, restriction for both Greater Melbourne and the rest/regional Victoria were, overall/in general, relaxed from 11:59pm on Thursday 17 June 2021.      For Victoria The Workplace Directions (No. 33) come into force at 11.59pm 17 June 2021 and replace the Workplace Directions (No. 32).  These directions commence at 11:59:00pm on 17 June 2021 and end at 11:59:00pm on 24 June 2021.    These directions maintain the previous density requirement of one person per 4 square meters.  This applies, to the non-classroom/teaching spaces of child care services, schools, and higher education institutions.      “The density quotient does, however, still apply to office areas including shared spaces that form part of an office area, spaces such as lunchrooms, photocopier room, principal’s office, back of reception and resource rooms. column24: New South Wales  In response to a growing cluster of community transmission within Sydney the Premier of NSW announced that some workplaces would be ordered to close from Saturday 26th June 2021.  Workplace closures are in effect for Greater Sydney, including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour (all in New South Wales).      The following places in Greater Sydney are directed to be closed to the public.  •	Pubs and registered clubs except for  o	selling food or beverages for people to consume off-site and  o	providing accommodation, including allowing food and drinks to be consumed in a person’s room.  •	Food and drink premises, except for  o	selling food or beverages for people to consume off-site  o	selling food to be consumed in a person’s room if in a hotel or motel  o	if the premises are part of a shopping centre, selling food or beverages for people to consume outside of the shopping centre  o	holding a wedding or funeral in accordance with relevant restrictions.  •	Entertainment facilities, such as theatres, cinemas, music halls, concert halls and dance halls.  •	Amusement centres, such as places to play billiards, pool, pinball machines or video games.  •	Micro-breweries or small distilleries holding a drink on-premises authorisation under the Liquor Act 2007 or cellar door premises, except for selling food or beverages for people to consume off the premises.  •	Indoor recreation facilities such as squash courts, indoor swimming pools, gyms, table tennis centres, health studios, bowling alleys and ice rinks.  •	Places of public worship, except for the purposes of conducting a wedding or funeral service.  •	Hairdressers, spas, nail salons, beauty salons, waxing salons, tanning salons, tattoo parlours, massage parlours.  •	Auction houses  •	Betting agencies and gaming lounges  •	Markets, except for food markets  •	Caravan parks and camping grounds, except for  o	permanent residents or other people who have no other place of permanent residence, and their visitors  o	people who were staying there on Friday 25 June 2021 and have not extended their booking.  •	Sex on premises services  •	Sex services premises  •	Strip clubs  •	Public swimming pools (except natural swimming pools, which may open)  •	National Trust properties and Historic Houses Trust of NSW properties (other than retail shops)  •	Nightclubs  •	Casinos, except for selling food or beverages for people to consume off-site and providing accommodation, including allowing food and drinks to be consumed in a person’s room..”  Given that in person retail has not been ordered to close, decision made to code as a level 2. column60: New South Wales  In response to a growing cluster of community transmission within Sydney the Premier of NSW announced stay at home orders which came into effect on Saturday 26th June 2021.  Residents of Greater Sydney, including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour (all in New South Wales) were ordered to stay at home, unless they had one of the “reasonable excuses” to leave home.    According to the NSW government COVID-19 website the following are considered reasonable excuses to leave home:     “A reasonable excuse is if you need to  •	obtain food or other goods and services  o	for the personal needs of the household or for other household purposes (including pets)  o	for vulnerable people  o	if the food or goods and services are not available in the local government area that you live in  •	travel for work or education if it is not possible to do it at home  •	exercise and take outdoor recreation in Greater Sydney  •	go out for medical or caring reasons, including obtaining a COVID-19 vaccination  •	donate blood  •	access childcare  •	continue existing arrangements for access to, and contact between, parents and children  •	attend a wedding or funeral  •	provide care or assistance (including personal care) to a vulnerable person or to provide emergency assistance  •	access social services, employment services, services provided to victims (including as victims of crime), domestic violence services, and mental health services  •	move to a new place of residence, or between your different places of residence  •	undertake legal obligations  •	avoid injury or illness or to escape the risk of harm  •	in case of emergencies  •	for compassionate reasons, including where two people are in a relationship but do not necessarily live together  •	to provide pastoral care if you are a priest, minister of religion or member of a religious order.  Taking a holiday is not a reasonable excuse.”    This stay at home order is enforced by the Public Health (COVID-19 Temporary Movement and Gathering Restrictions) Order 2021 which came into effect from 26th June 2021.  Under the new Order, stay at home requirements have been extended to all LGAs in the Greater Sydney area which includes the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour. There are also additional restrictions across NSW. The Order has commenced and will be in place until midnight 9 July, unless extended or repealed earlier.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210628115210/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/greater-sydney   https://web.archive.org/web/20210628115612/https://legislation.nsw.gov.au/file/Public%20Health%20%28COVID-19%20Temporary%20Movement%20and%20Gathering%20Restrictions%29%20Order%202021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210628115746/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx

column24: The Northern Territory  According to a press release on the Northern Territory Government Covid-19 website, on the 27th June the Northern Territory “Security and Emergency Management Committee made the decision under the Northern Territory pandemic plan to lock down part of the NT community.  Effective from 1.00pm today – the Darwin, Palmerston and Darwin rural areas will enter a full lockdown for 48 hours.  That means the local government areas of the City of Darwin, the City of Palmerston, and Litchfield.  This follows confirmation of four new covid-19 positive cases all linked to the Tanami case.”  “All businesses, retail outlets, hospitality venues, gyms, pools and community centres will be closed. Cafes and restaurants will be able to offer take-away and delivery services.”  Furthermore, it the NT COVID-19 government website clearly states that only essential workers are able to leave home in order to work.  See archived URL for list of essential workers.   Northern territory workplace closures are more restrictive compared to the New South Wales work place closures that came into effect on the 26th June 2021.  Therefore changed to a 3T code.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210628121424/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/advice-care/covid-19-lockdown-restrictions-in-place  web.archive.org/web/20210627060745/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-06-27-covid-19-update-lockdown-restrictions-in-place   http://web.archive.org/web/20210628132726/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/essential-workers

column15: New South Wales  In response to a growing cluster of community transmission within Sydney the Premier of NSW announced that more stringent policy settings would be in place for residents of NSW from Saturday 26th June 2021.  More specially for residents of Greater Sydney, including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour (all in New South Wales):   Residents must only attend in person education if it is not possible to learn from home.    School holidays commenced in NSW on Saturday 26th June 2021, so this does not impact school attendance.  However, university and other tertiary education students are affected.  As an example the University of Sydney (see archived URL) has moved all in person exams and intensive courses online or otherwise rescheduled/cancelled.  However, the university library remains open for students to access with swipe access.    Decision made to code level 2 to indicate that the higher education sector in the Greater Sydney area is not able to undertake face to face learning.   Changed the code from Monday 28th June as this is the first day that the new government policy is likely to have impacted upon the operation of the higher education sector (ie. it is unlikely that classes were scheduled for over the weekend.)    https://web.archive.org/web/20210628140944/https://www.sydney.edu.au/study/coronavirus-infection-university-of-sydney-advice.html   http://web.archive.org/web/20210628124921/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/greater-sydney   https://web.archive.org/web/20210628115612/https://legislation.nsw.gov.au/file/Public%20Health%20%28COVID-19%20Temporary%20Movement%20and%20Gathering%20Restrictions%29%20Order%202021.pdf   https://web.archive.org/web/20210628115746/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/public-health-orders.aspx     The Northern Territory  The Northern Territory  According to a press release on the Northern Territory Government Covid-19 website, on the 27th June the Northern Territory “Security and Emergency Management Committee made the decision under the Northern Territory pandemic plan to lock down part of the NT community.  Effective from 1.00pm today – the Darwin, Palmerston and Darwin rural areas will enter a full lockdown for 48 hours.  That means the local government areas of the City of Darwin, the City of Palmerston, and Litchfield.  This follows confirmation of four new covid-19 positive cases all linked to the Tanami case.”  “Although it is school holidays, schools and vacation care will be closed except for the children of essential workers. Childcare centres will open only for the children of essential workers.”  Furthermore, leaving home to attend education is not one of the allowable reasons to leaving home under the current stay at home orders for the Darwin, Palmerston and Darwin rural areas.    “People are only permitted to leave home for the following five reasons. Masks must be worn at all times when leaving  home:  To receive health care or attend medical appointments. Including to get the COVID-19 vaccine  To undertake shopping and get necessary goods and services. Supermarkets will stay open, so there is no need to stock up on groceries.  To exercise for up to one hour a day within 5km of your home and only with members of your household or one other person.  To undertake essential work. If you can work from home – you must work from home.  In case of an emergency or if there is family violence or to provide essential care to a loved one or dependent.”  Decided to maintain the 2T code, indicating that the higher education sector is closed for in person teaching.  Reason for not coding a level 3 is that it is currently school holidays within the Northern Territory.  This is in line with the interpretation guide “If schools are closed, and this same closure policy then rolls into school holidays, we keep the code the same, for example ‘all levels of education remain closed’. This coding would only go down only once students actually return, when schools reopen”    https://web.archive.org/web/20210628121424/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/advice-care/covid-19-lockdown-restrictions-in-place  web.archive.org/web/20210627060745/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2021-06-27-covid-19-update-lockdown-restrictions-in-place   http://web.archive.org/web/20210628132726/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/essential-workers

column106: The following people are now eligible for the COVID-19 vaccine:    All adults aged 40-49  All Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 16 to 49  NDIS participants, and carers of NDIS participants, aged 16 years and over  Temporary visa holders aged under 50 years who are currently in Australia and have been approved for return travel to Australia through the travel exemption process  Adults aged 18-39 can choose to receive the AstraZeneca vaccine.    This is in addition to the following people, who were already eligible for vaccination:  All adults aged 50 and over  Quarantine and border workers  Health care workers  Aged care and disability care residents and staff  People aged 16 and over with an underlying medical condition or significant disability  Critical and high risk workers aged 16 and over including defence, police, fire, emergency services and meat processing. column119: Since the 30th of June 2021, the AstraZeneca vaccine is made accessible to people aged between 18 and 39. This is in addition to the people who were already eligible.     “The medical advice on which vaccine to get – Pfizer or AstraZeneca – has not changed. The Pfizer vaccine remains the preferred vaccine for people under 60. However, under new arrangements, access to the AstraZeneca vaccine is being expanded to adults under 40 – provided they give informed consent.    This means people aged 18 to 39 – who are not otherwise eligible – can choose to receive the AstraZeneca vaccine after the appropriate individual risk-benefit analysis and consent process. Health providers should only vaccinate patients with AstraZeneca without a contraindication and if the person gives fully informed consent, noting they will be able to access Pfizer later in the year.    https://web.archive.org/web/20211201162845/https://hhmp.com.au/18-to-39-covid-19-vaccine/    https://web.archive.org/web/20210702192041/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19/when-will-i-get-a-covid-19-vaccine

column15: NSW:  The stay-at-home order has been extended for Greater SydneyExternal link until Friday 30 July. This will mean 2 separate guidelines for schools depending on their location.  The guidelines are separated as those for:    Schools in Greater Sydney (including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour): Learning from home is encouraged; schools are open for families who need it – applies to schools in Greater Sydney (including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour).Students encouraged to learn from home. No student is to be turned away. Early childhood education services can continue to operate. All non–core curriculum related activities are to cease on school site. Schools should not turn away any student from attending the school site in person. Schools will provide a program of learning for students who attend school, and students who are learning from home.      Schools outside of Greater Sydney: Restrictions on certain activities and non-essential visitors apply to schools outside of Greater Sydney (including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour).All students are expected to be at school unless they have even the mildest of COVID-19 symptoms. Under the current health orders, there is nothing to restrict students and staff who must attend school from moving between two areas with different levels of restrictions. Students and staff should reduce traveling to geographical areas with higher levels of COVID-19 restrictions in place.  See here: https://www.web.archive.org/web/20210716162458/https://education.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/advice-for-families    Victoria:  All schools will close, except for vulnerable children and children of authorised workers or authorised providers, who can attend for onsite supervision. Schools will continue to support students through remote learning or providing learning materials for use at home. Universities and TAFEs will close or move to remote learning. Childcare, family day care, early learning centres and kinders will remain open. A permit is not required  see here: https:www.web.archive.org/web/20210716162108/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/coronavirus-covidsafe-settings

"The covid-19 disaster payment is a lump sum payment to help workers unable to earn income due to a COVID-19 lockdown, hotspot or period of restricted movement. column24: “Canberrans are being asked to stay at home to assist in our response to positive cases of COVID-19 in the ACT.  The lockdown restrictions came into effect from 5:00pm Thursday 12 August 2021 and will continue until 5:00pm Thursday 2 September 2021…  The six essential reasons you can leave your home during this lockdown”, include:  “to buy essential groceries and medicine…  for essential work” Source: https://archive.vn/gJcyH   “From 12:59pm Saturday 21 August 2021, restrictions across Victoria change:…  You cannot leave your home other than for one of the five following reasons”. These reasons include “shopping for necessary goods and services”. “Necessary goods and services include supermarkets, pharmacies, butchers, green grocers, bottle shops, petrol stations, post offices, banks, food stores, newsagents, liquor stores, and pet stores. Supermarkets will continue to stay open to allow people to buy food, so there is no need to stock up on groceries. Other retail shops will only be available for delivery or contactless click and collect, and authorised workers may attend onsite to facilitate these orders.” Source: https://archive.vn/Qup0D   “Most retail premises in Greater Sydney including the Greater Sydney including the Blue Mountains and Wollongong are closed to the public.”  “Businesses providing essential products and services that can be open include  supermarkets  grocery stores  kiosks and other small food and drinks premises  petrol stations” Source: https://archive.vn/CC5Ng

column15: It remains the case that schools are closed in New South Wales. It was reported on August 23rd by The Guardian that   “New South Wales’s plans for schools should be known by the end of the week, but with rising Covid case numbers among children the premier, Gladys Berejiklian, has ruled out schools opening in term three.  Term four is looking dubious, especially for primary-age children.  “We have provided certainty for parents in terms of September,” Berejiklian said. “Unfortunately, we have said that current conditions remain in place. If you’re still home schooling, that will continue,” she said, apparently referring to last week’s extension of the lockdown until the end of September.  “September doesn’t change what is happening for schools, but in terms of when schools are likely to go back, we will provide information when it’s available.”” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827222949/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2021/aug/23/nsw-covid-update-september-return-to-school-ruled-out-and-fourth-term-in-doubt-amid-states-818-new-cases
column33: It remains the case that in some regions of Australia, it is required that public events are cancelled.   For example, “all Canberrans [are asked] to stay at home and leave only for essential reasons detailed below.”   “No more than 2 people permitted to visit another household, but only for the approved reasons outlined below or for compassionate purposes.  Intimate partner visits are allowed. ACT residents are permitted to enter approved postcodes in the border community to visit intimate partners.People who live alone can identify one other household that they can visit or receive visits from. This includes single parents who live alone with their children.  Parents needing to leave home for essential reasons and requiring childcare are permitted to leave their children with family members. Children can be cared for by one identified household only.”  Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827221505/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/act-status-and-response/lockdown
