column51: In Victoria, “when travelling on public transport you must wear a fitted face mask covering your nose and mouth and ensure you wear it for your entire journey.” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827213916/https://www.ptv.vic.gov.au/more/coronavirus-covid-19/wearing-a-mask-on-public-transport/   Also, “From Monday 23 August”, as part of “COVID-19 restrictions in NSW”,  “Weekday public transport services will be reduced in Greater Sydney, including Intercity train services”. Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827214028/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance
column60: In some regions of Australia, it remains the case that there are stay at home requirements.  For example, in New South Wales, “You must stay home. Only leave your home if you have a reasonable excuse.” “A reasonable excuse is if you need to  obtain food or other goods and services…  exercise and take outdoor recreation within your local government area or, if you need to cross into another local government area, stay within 5km of your home.” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827214512/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/greater-sydney
column69: In some regions of Australia, there are restrictions on internal movement, ranging from recommendations to banning travel from certain regions and quarantine requirements.  “The ACT is in lockdown from 5:00 pm Thursday 12 August until 5:00 pm Thursday 2 September” and “ACT Policing will be undertaking compliance checks across the ACT and you will be expected to provide proof of residential address and the reason you are moving about the ACT community.” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827220344/https://www.covid19.act.gov.au/travel/entering-the-act   “If you live in NSW regional and rural areas, you must have a reasonable excuse to enter Greater Sydney including the Blue Mountains, Central Coast, Wollongong and Shellharbour local government areas.  If you enter these areas for any reason, stay at home rules will also apply to you for 14 days after you were last in any of these areas.  This means that you will need to stay at your place of residence for 14 days, even if it is in a regional area, unless you have a reasonable excuse.”  Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827220435/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/border-restrictions   “Visitors to the Northern Territory (NT) will not be permitted entry if they are from a declared Covid-19 hotspot or public exposure site. Any visitors from these areas will be turned around and may be subject to a fine.” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827220527/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/quarantine   Also, there is a “Pause on hotspot arrivals into Queensland  Due to hotel capacity issues, from 12pm (midday) AEST 25 August until 12pm (midday) AEST 8 September, Queensland residents and those intending to relocate to Queensland who have been in a hotspot in the last 14 days or since the identified start date (whichever is shorter) will not be allowed to enter Queensland, without an exemption. Exemptions will only be granted in extreme exceptional circumstances. If you need support, there are services available to you.  Hotspots  The Australian Capital Territory, the Jervis Bay Territory, New South Wales, and Victoria are declared hotspots.  Border zone  New South Wales border zone residents can only cross the border for an essential purpose. Likewise, Queensland residents can only enter the New South Wales border zone for an essential purpose. New South Wales border zone residents and Queensland residents who have travelled into the border zone must comply with stay at home and face mask requirements, while in Queensland.” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827220551/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/travelling-to-queensland
column74: Arrivals are banned from most regions but not all regions because there is an exception for travellers from New Zealand. See the below:  “Australia’s borders are closed. The only people who can travel to Australia are:  Australian citizens  permanent residents  immediate family members  travellers who have been in New Zealand for at least the 14 days before the date of departure. This does not include the Realm Countries of the Cook Islands and Niue. (Please note quarantine free travel from New Zealand is suspended until at least 11:59pm on 27 August 2021.)” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827224442/https://www.health.gov.au/news/health-alerts/novel-coronavirus-2019-ncov-health-alert/coronavirus-covid-19-travel-and-restrictions/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-international-travellers

column88: “The coronavirus that causes COVID-19 can have a range of symptoms...  If you have any of these symptoms, you should consider getting tested for COVID-19 — even if your symptoms are mild.” Source: https://archive.vn/1FGTr
column90: No policy change found. Comprehensive contact tracing remains in place. Sources:  https://archive.vn/n0MZZ & https://archive.vn/y2RfD
column103: “Where there is low community transmission of COVID-19, wearing a mask in the community when you are well is not generally recommended.  However, where there is significant community transmission (as determined by jurisdictional public health authorities), you may choose, or be required to, wear a mask. If physical distancing is difficult to maintain, for example on public transport, covering your face with a mask can provide some extra protection.” Source: https://archive.vn/6Ps0I   In Greater Sydney, “you must wear a face mask when you are outside in a public place including   on a street or at a park,  queuing outside a cafe or shop to collect take away food or drink  walking on a street near shop fronts”  “There are limited exceptions, such as where you are in a vehicle with members of your household or your nominated visitor (‘singles bubble’), when exercising and in emergencies.”  Source: https://archive.vn/pBndc   In Victoria, “Face masks must be worn indoors and outdoors whenever you leave your home. Face masks do not need to be worn indoors or outdoors if you are working alone unless another person enters that indoor or outdoor space.”  Source: https://archive.vn/PeHCy   “Wearing face masks became mandatory in the ACT from 5:00pm Thursday 12 August 2021.  All individuals aged 12 years and over must wear a mask at all times when leaving home, including in workplaces.  Only children in years 7 to 12 are required to wear a face mask while at school.  A person is not required to wear a face mask if they have a physical or mental health illness or condition, or disability, which makes wearing a face mask detrimental to their condition.  A person may remove their face mask in the following situations:  When the person is consuming food, drink or medicine.  When communicating with a person who is deaf or hard of hearing and visibility of the mouth is essential for communication.  If asked to remove a face mask to ascertain identity (eg if asked by a police officer).  While undertaking vigorous exercise outdoors.  When performing essential work in an outdoor space where no other people are present.  When alone or only with members of the same household in an outdoor space when no other people are present.  When performing essential work in an indoor space, but only when  In an office where no other people are present; OR  When sitting or standing at a workstation, and 1.5 metres away from any other person.  (As soon as a person is no longer alone or is moving, they must wear a face mask).  If they are in the process of getting married.  If alone in a vehicle or with members of the same household only.  Because of an emergency.” Source: https://archive.vn/DsHqz
column106: “The Eligibility Checker is currently being updated for eligibility for the COVID-19 Pfizer (Comirnaty) vaccine, including people aged:  16 to 39 years from 30 August 2021  12 to 15 years from 13 September 2021  General practices are currently experiencing a high number of phone calls from people interested in receiving the vaccine. Please use online bookings where possible, and refrain from calling your GP until bookings open for these age groups.” Source: https://archive.vn/vomWh   “All residential aged care workers are eligible to receive the Pfizer vaccine regardless of age. If you work in residential aged care, you can contact the National Coronavirus Helpline on 1800 020 080 and select option 4 for priority assistance and advice relating to booking your COVID-19 vaccine.  If you are aged 40 years or more, you are eligible for vaccination.  If you are aged 16 to 39 years you may be eligible for vaccination. Complete the checker to find out.  If you are not yet eligible and aged 18 years or over, you can register to be notified when you are.  If you are eligible and can’t find a clinic or make a booking that suits you, please check back later. New clinics and appointments are being added all the time.  People aged 12 to 15 years of age are eligible to get vaccinated if they meet the following criteria:  children with specified medical conditions  Aboriginal and Torres Strait islander children  children in remote communities, as part of community outreach vaccination programs  Those living with disability requiring frequent assistance with activities of daily living, including down syndrome, muscular dystrophy, traumatic brain and spinal cord injury, and severe intellectual disability.”  Source: https://archive.vn/0e0o6
column115: In some regions of Australia, it remains the case that there are stay at home requirements. These stay at home requirements include elderly people. For example, in New South Wales, “You must stay home. Only leave your home if you have a reasonable excuse.” “A reasonable excuse is if you need to obtain food or other goods and services… exercise and take outdoor recreation within your local government area or, if you need to cross into another local government area, stay within 5km of your home.” Source: http://web.archive.org/web/20210827214512/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/greater-sydney   In addition, extensive restrictions for staff, visitors, and residents of Residential Aged Care Facilities in New South Wales continue to apply: https://web.archive.org/web/20210830043934/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/covid-19/Pages/racf-latest-advice.aspx

column129: COVID-19 vaccines will be voluntary and free  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia. Vaccination providers cannot charge you for the COVID-19 vaccine or your appointments to receive the vaccine.    If your vaccination provider charges for any costs associated with the administration of the COVID-19 vaccination (including booking fees), you should:    contact us by emailing provider.benefits.integrity@health.gov.au,  or call the Provider Benefits Integrity Hotline on 1800 314 808 (9am to 5pm AEST weekdays).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423124712/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19

column94: 3 September Four million extra Pfizer-BioNTech (Pfizer) vaccine doses will begin arriving in Australia in days following a historic partnership between the Australian and United Kingdom governments. Doses from the UK will be distributed around Australia on an equal population share basis, with 60 per cent being distributed through the primary care network and 40 per cent through the state based vaccination clinics.    There are no purchase costs associated with these deals.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210907133809/https://www.pm.gov.au/media/four-million-pfizer-doses-arrive-super-charge-vaccine-roll-out

column15: No policy change. Regulations updated 16 Sept. No NSW public schools are currently operating without restrictions (level 1). Region specific rules are in place that range from staggering in-person teaching, mask requirements, and mask recommendations. Further lockdowns (starting from 6pm) are in place for regions with high incidence rates (Albury, Lismore, Glen Innes, Hilltops LGAs).  https://web.archive.org/web/20210920175125/https://education.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/advice-for-families#Levels1  https://web.archive.org/web/20210920184201/https://www.dese.gov.au/covid-19/announcements/covid19-nsw-what-new-lockdowns-and-easing-restrictions-mean-child-care

column60: Continuing from early September many regions are on stay-at-home orders with exceptions for reasonable, essential excuses. 16 September stay-at-home orders will be lifted for Bega, Blayney, Bogan, Cabonne, Dungog, Forbes, Muswellbrook, Narrabri, Parkes, Singleton, Snowy Monaro and Upper Hunter Local Government Areas (LGAs) from 1pm. column129: COVID-19 vaccines will be voluntary and free  The COVID-19 vaccine is free for everyone in Australia. Vaccination providers cannot charge you for the COVID-19 vaccine or your appointments to receive the vaccine.    If your vaccination provider charges for any costs associated with the administration of the COVID-19 vaccination (including booking fees), you should:    contact us by emailing provider.benefits.integrity@health.gov.au,  or call the Provider Benefits Integrity Hotline on 1800 314 808 (9am to 5pm AEST weekdays).    https://web.archive.org/web/20210423124712/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/getting-vaccinated-for-covid-19
column131: On Monday, National Cabinet agreed that the COVID-19 vaccination of residential aged care workers will become mandatory by mid-September 2021.    https://archive.vn/pATH2#selection-495.19-495.161

column24: 16 Sept twelve regions are coming out of lockdown but other areas of AUS remain in lockdown with a stay-at-home order. Under stay-at-home there is a closure of retail businesses for all but essential businesses. The goods and services must be picked up within the local government area where you live, or within 5km of where you live but not in another area of concern.     In the twelve areas coming out of lockdown restrictions are still in place: Retail stores can reopen under the one person per 4sqm rule. Personal services such as hairdressers and nail salons can open with one person per 4sqm, capped at five clients per premises.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210920193638/https://www.nsw.gov.au/media-releases/changes-to-stay-at-home-orders-for-regional-nsw-local-government-areas    https://web.archive.org/web/20210920194523/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/affected-area

column33: Targeted regions have canceled events to comply with stay-at-home orders. New starting 20 September, those who are fully vaccinated and over 16 years old can attend an outdoor gathering in a public space of up to 5 people for exercise or outdoor recreation. Previously vaccination was not considered. Those who are not fully vaccinated can attend an outdoor gathering in a public space with 1 other person that you do not live with, or your nominated visitor ("singles bubble") for exercise or outdoor recreation.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210920195542/https://www.nsw.gov.au/media-releases/restrictions-to-ease-lgas-of-concern  https://web.archive.org/web/20210920194117/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/affected-regions
column42: Starting 20 September, those who live alone no longer have to register their nominated visitor ('singles bubble') with Service NSW. Although the registration is lifted, these 'nominated visitors' can only live either in a local government area of concern or Greater Sydney.    Continuing policy restrictions are in place for regions under stay-at-home order where visiting another person is not a reasonable excuse to leave your home and you must not allow a person to visit your home.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210920194523/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/affected-area
column60: From 20 September, stay-at-home conditions are slightly relaxed. There is now no limit on the duration of outdoor exercise and recreation (previously a two-hour limit); Outdoor gatherings of up to five fully vaccinated people (not including children aged 12 and under) will be allowed in a person’s LGA or within 5km of home (previously only fully vaccinated household members could gather outdoors, or up to two fully vaccinated people from different households); Shopping, exercise and outdoor recreation can be done 5km from home or within your LGA (previously only 5km from home); Attend a small wedding (maximum 11 people) in Greater Sydney as a guest (previously only allowed in their LGA); and A person’s single’s bubble buddy can live in Greater Sydney (previously must have resided within 5km of a person’s home). A person from a LGA of concern can also now be a buddy for someone in Greater Sydney.    From 20 September 2021 stay-at-home rules are in place for the local government area of Cowra until Tuesday 28 September 2021. The stay at home orders apply to anyone who has been in Cowra from 13 September 2021.    Continuing from early September many regions are on stay-at-home orders with exceptions for reasonable, essential excuses. Stay-at-home orders remain in place in the following local government areas – Bathurst Regional, Bourke, Brewarrina, City of Broken Hill, Central Coast, Central Darling, City of Cessnock, Dubbo Regional, Eurobodalla, Gilgandra, Goulburn Mulwarre, Kiama, City of Lake Macquarie, City of Lithgow, City of Maitland, Mid-Western Regional, Narromine, City of Newcastle, City of Orange, Port Stephens, Queanbeyan-Palerang Regional, City of Shellharbour, City of Shoalhaven, Walgett and Wingecarribee.     https://web.archive.org/web/20210920195542/https://www.nsw.gov.au/media-releases/restrictions-to-ease-lgas-of-concern  https://web.archive.org/web/20210920194523/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/rules/affected-area

column15: Starting 11 October stay-at-home orders are being lifted in stages across NSW, Victoria, and part of Sydney. Schools will return to face-to-face learning either through a ‘staged return’ or ‘full return’, depending on health conditions in their area. Now in areas where 75% of the eligible population over the age of 16 are fully vaccinated, students in kindergarten, years 1 and 12 return to in-person teaching. The remaining grades will return the week beginning 25 October.     Schools in areas where stay-at-home rules had been in place for an extended period of time (continuously from August 2021) will follow a staged return to on-site learning under Level 3 plus settings from 18 October.    https://web.archive.org/web/20211018075601/https://education.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/advice-for-families  https://web.archive.org/web/20211017214229/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2021/oct/17/victoria-and-nsw-schools-are-reopening-amid-covid-outbreaks-what-can-be-learnt-from-overseas
column24: On 11 October 2021 NSW opened and ended lockdown as the state passed the 70% double vaccination target. Staff who work indoors must wear face masks.    https://web.archive.org/web/20211015073454/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/business/rules  https://web.archive.org/web/20211018081943/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/easing-covid-19-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20211018082938/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/Pages/20211003_01.aspx    Currently Melbourne is still under lockdown (to end this Fri 22 Oct). column24: Starting 22 Oct curfews and lockdowns have ended. Restrictions vary by region but generally are eased for people who are fully vaccinated or have a medical exemption. Workplaces have opened with hygiene or capacity restrictions. Example: up to 75 per cent or up to 30 people can return if total workplace is 40 or less, if there’s no more than 1 person per 4 square metres for Metropolitan Melbourne locations, and no more than 1 person per 2 square metres for Regional Victoria locations.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210708181532/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/vic  https://web.archive.org/web/20211022184508/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2021/oct/22/melbourne-covid-lockdown-ends-lifts-today-friday-victoria-end-lift-change-what-are-the-new-restrictions-rules-freedoms-reopening-plan  https://web.archive.org/web/20211022043759/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/easing-covid-19-restrictions/80-percent
column33: Starting 22 Oct curfews and lockdowns have ended. However, music festivals still can't be held in NSW.   Other restrictions vary by region but generally are eased for people who are fully vaccinated or have a medical exemption. Generally indoor events have a capacity restriction of 75 per cent but outdoor events have 100 per cent capacity with social distancing. Example: A maximum of 2,000 people can attend a controlled, ticketed outdoor event if they’re assigned a specific seat — subject to 1 person per 2 square metres. A maximum of 500 people can attend a controlled, ticketed outdoor event if they’re allocated to a designated seating area — subject to 1 person per 2 square metres. Indoor entertainment facilities can sell tickets for seated events at 75 per cent of fixed seating capacity or no more than 1 person per 2 square metres on the premises. Outdoor entertainment facilities can sell tickets for seated events at 100 per cent of fixed seating capacity or no more than 1 person per 2 square metres on the premises.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210307062444/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/nsw  https://web.archive.org/web/20210708181532/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/vic  https://web.archive.org/web/20211022184508/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2021/oct/22/melbourne-covid-lockdown-ends-lifts-today-friday-victoria-end-lift-change-what-are-the-new-restrictions-rules-freedoms-reopening-plan  https://web.archive.org/web/20211022043759/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/easing-covid-19-restrictions/80-percent

column33: No new policy. Continuing from 22 Oct curfews and lockdowns have ended. However, music festivals still can't be held in NSW.   Other restrictions vary by region but generally are eased for people who are fully vaccinated or have a medical exemption. Generally indoor events have a capacity restriction of 75 per cent but outdoor events have 100 per cent capacity with social distancing. Example: A maximum of 2,000 people can attend a controlled, ticketed outdoor event if they’re assigned a specific seat — subject to 1 person per 2 square metres. A maximum of 500 people can attend a controlled, ticketed outdoor event if they’re allocated to a designated seating area — subject to 1 person per 2 square metres. Indoor entertainment facilities can sell tickets for seated events at 75 per cent of fixed seating capacity or no more than 1 person per 2 square metres on the premises. Outdoor entertainment facilities can sell tickets for seated events at 100 per cent of fixed seating capacity or no more than 1 person per 2 square metres on the premises.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210620091338/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/nsw  https://web.archive.org/web/20210708181532/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/vic  https://web.archive.org/web/20211022184508/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2021/oct/22/melbourne-covid-lockdown-ends-lifts-today-friday-victoria-end-lift-change-what-are-the-new-restrictions-rules-freedoms-reopening-plan  https://web.archive.org/web/20211022043759/https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/easing-covid-19-restrictions/80-percent

column15: Starting 8 November all schools in NSW will operate on updated Level 3 settings for the rest of Term. Level 3 is a relaxation of COVID restrictions made of of a combination of layered safety measures allowing student and staff wellness while keeping schools open. This provision has allowed for more school activities including dancing and some music classes, school sports, and now fully vaccinated visitors being allowed on site to support curriculum delivery, wellbeing programs and school operations.    Continuing 25 October policy is still in place where schools of all grades have returned to face-to-face learning either through a ‘staged return’ or ‘full return’, depending on health conditions in their area. Now in areas where 75% of the eligible population over the age of 16 are fully vaccinated, students in kindergarten, years 1 and 12 return to in-person teaching. Other countries restricts travel to Australia where travel is only available if you are exempt or you have been granted an individual exemption (even if you have been vaccinated). All travelers entering Australia from overseas will be quarantined in a hotel or designated facility for 14 days. Travelers are required to undertake their mandatory 14-day quarantine at their port of entry.       https://web.archive.org/web/20211102094008/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/  https://web.archive.org/web/20211102094556/https://www.health.gov.au/news/health-alerts/novel-coronavirus-2019-ncov-health-alert/coronavirus-covid-19-travel-and-restrictions/international-travel-and-covid-19  https://web.archive.org/web/20211102094513/https://www.cnbc.com/2021/11/01/australia-reopens-its-borders-to-new-zealand-and-singapore.html

column24: From 14 Dec lockdown and lockouts have ended but some areas have restrictions on workers who are unvaccinated. In NSW if you're not fully vaccinated, your employer must require you to work from home unless it's not reasonably practicable. Also many workers in the Northern Territory are required to be vaccinated with a first dose of COVID-19 vaccine in order to keep working. Workers need to be fully vaccinated by 24 December 2021.    https://web.archive.org/web/20211214173212/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/nt    https://web.archive.org/web/20211214171839/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/nsw
column33: From 14 Dec NSW is no longer under lockdown. However, different rules apply to people who are fully vaccinated and those who aren’t fully vaccinated. If you are not fully vaccinated, you cannot visit a recreation or entertainment facility or attend an outdoor event. If you're not fully vaccinated, you can only gather with 1 other person or with your household outdoors.    Also Music festivals are still prohibited for all vaccinated and unvaccinated like.   COVID-19 Safety Plans are required if more than 1000 people attend an outdoor gathering.     https://web.archive.org/web/20211115210410/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/nsw
column42: From 14 December in the NT region restrictions have eased for people who are fully vaccinated, have a medical exemption, or are under 16 years. If all members of your household who are aged 16 years and over are fully vaccinated, there is no limit to the number of fully vaccinated visitors you can have to your home. However, in NT you can't have a person aged over 16 years who's not fully vaccinated visit your place of residence, except in limited circumstances.    http://web.archive.org/web/20211105145855/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/nsw
column51: No new policy. More green physical distancing dots will be rolled out across the network, allowing you to choose the best places to sit and stand on all modes. Other regions (ex. Victoria) have returned to full capacity after passing 80% of vaccinated 29 Oct.     https://web.archive.org/web/20211102095556/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance  https://web.archive.org/web/20211214171205/https://transportsafety.vic.gov.au/bus-safety/newsroom/bus-industry-covid
column69: From 14 Dec lockouts and lockdowns in NT have ended. Continuing policy in limited regions has canceled public transit routes or limited travel for essential work and essential purposes ex. within the Queensland/New South Wales border zone. The Queensland Government declared parts of NSW a hotspot. Anyone who has been to a hotspot in the last 14 days will not be allowed to enter Queensland, unless they are a Queensland resident or have an exemption.     https://web.archive.org/web/20211214155411/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance  https://web.archive.org/web/20211214155551/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/travelling-to-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20211214163831/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

column103: Lockouts and lockdowns have ended (from 21 November) but many in the NT require everyone to wear a mask outside of their home. column115: No new policies. National policy outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms.    https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf    https://web.archive.org/web/20211021011733/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors

column15: From 14 Dec lockout and lockdowns have ended. School and childcare centres can provide onsite learning to all students  https://web.archive.org/web/20211214173520/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/education-and-childcare/nt
column60: From 14 Dec lockouts and lockdowns in NT have ended.     https://web.archive.org/web/20211214155411/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance  https://web.archive.org/web/20211214155551/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/travelling-to-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20211214163831/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

column24: Following the end of lockdowns (14 Dec) workplaces are now open but typically with restrictions - ex. only vaccinated staff and patrons, and those with a medical exemption, will be allowed to enter: ex.  - indoor entertainment venues such as nightclubs, live music venues, karaoke bars, concerts, theatres or cinemas and casinos  - outdoor entertainment activities such as sporting stadiums or theme parks  - festivals — either indoor or outdoor — such as musical, folk or arts festivals  - activities — either indoor or outdoor — such as convention centres and showgrounds    https://web.archive.org/web/20220106095740/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/qld
column33: Following the end of lockdowns (14 Dec) events can operate but typically with restrictions - ex. only vaccinated staff and patrons, and those with a medical exemption, will be allowed to enter:   - indoor entertainment venues such as nightclubs, live music venues, karaoke bars, concerts, theatres or cinemas and casinos  - outdoor entertainment activities such as sporting stadiums or theme parks  - festivals — either indoor or outdoor — such as musical, folk or arts festivals  - activities — either indoor or outdoor — such as convention centres and showgrounds    https://web.archive.org/web/20220106095740/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/qld
column42: Capacity limits still exist on events and gatherings. In NSW only up to 10 people are permitted in a home, including residents.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106100858/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/sa
column51: Continuing policy is in place where from Monday 1 November, COVIDSafe capacity on all modes in NSW will increase from 50 per cent to around 75 per cent with an increase in service capacities. More green physical distancing dots will be rolled out across the network, allowing you to choose the best places to sit and stand on all modes. Other regions (ex. Victoria) have returned to full capacity.    Regional trains are still operating at reduced services pursuant to the Queensland border restrictions.   https://web.archive.org/web/20220106101509/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance
column69: Continuing policy in limited regions has canceled public transit routes or limited travel for essential work and essential purposes ex. within the Queensland/New South Wales border zone. The Queensland Government declared parts of NSW a hotspot. Anyone who has been to a hotspot in the last 14 days will not be allowed to enter Queensland, unless they are a Queensland resident or have an exemption.     https://web.archive.org/web/20220106101509/https://transportnsw.info/covid-19/covid-19-safer-travel-guidance  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106102201/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/travelling-to-queensland  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106102244/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/
column74: On 15 December AUS reopens the borders to fully vaccinated international students and Temporary Graduate (subclass 485) visa holders. Continuing policy restricts other travelers to Australia to fully vaccinated Australians and permanent residents.   https://web.archive.org/web/20220106102743/https://www.dese.gov.au/reopening-international-travel-students  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106102244/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106103048/https://www.health.gov.au/health-alerts/covid-19/international-travel/inbound

column24: Rules vary from state to state, but there generally there are increased restrictions for non-vaccinated people either state-wide or in specific lockdown/lockout regions.  For example, in Queensland state-wide restriction apply to non-vaccinated individuals (over 16) from 17-Dec-21) whereas in the Northern Territories restrictions apply in designated lockdown areas.  Most workers are required to have at least the first vaccine dose and must be fully vaccinated by 24-Dec21    https://web.archive.org/web/20211206062438/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/queensland-restrictions-update/public-health-and-social-measures-linked-to-vaccination-status  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106095740/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/qld

column103: Face masks are mandatory in all indoor settings across the Northern Territory. This includes pubs, clubs, restaurants, shopping centres, hairdressers, workplaces, public transport, taxi cabs, ride share and indoor boat cruises.    It is also recommended you wear a mask outdoors if you can't maintain a physical distance of 1.5 metres.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220108124308/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/face-masks/nt

column42: As published on the Queensland Government website on 21 January 2022, gathering restrictions were in place from 17 December 2021, restricting gatherings with attendees who are not fully vaccinated to a maximum of 20 people. Funerals are less strictly restricted, but "the 1 people per 2 square metres rule or 200 people or 100% allocated seated and ticketed capacity will apply — whichever is the greater". The policy was implemented across Queensland.   This represents a relaxation of the policy response to COVID-19, as gathering restrictions are lifted if all attendees are fully vaccinated.  The policy remained current on 21 January 2022.  http://web.archive.org/web/20220121082234/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/queensland-restrictions-update/public-health-and-social-measures-linked-to-vaccination-status  http://web.archive.org/web/20220121084746/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/weddings-funerals-religions-services/qld
